01/67·The Confidence Ladder
AWS re:Invent 2026 · Games & Interactive · interactive deck — demos run live

The Confidence Ladder

Climb from guesswork to proof in your game

A studio-side toolkit that turns “we think our game is fair” into “we can prove it” — using AI/ML where it earns its keep, and mathematics where the stakes demand certainty. No data-science background needed; every idea gets a plain-English analogy, a worked example, and a live demo you can poke at.

◆ SHARDFALL our running example ✦ STAR ORACLE our “other genre” foil
▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Quick show of hands — how many of you, right now, could prove that your game’s loot odds are exactly what you published? Not ‘we’re pretty sure.’ Prove it.” [let the hands — or lack of them — sit for a beat]

“Right. Most of us can’t. And that’s not a criticism — it’s the gap this whole talk is about. I’m going to show you a way to climb from ‘we think our game is fair’ all the way up to ‘we can prove it’ — and, just as importantly, how to know exactly how high you actually need to climb.”

“We call it the Confidence Ladder. And here’s my promise: you do not need a data-science background. Every idea gets a plain-English story first, then a live demo you’ll watch compute right here on screen — no slideware, no trust-me numbers.” [gesture to the rail on the right] “That ladder on the edge of every slide? That’s our map. It lights up as we climb.”

Cold open. The room mixes engineers, LiveOps, designers, producers — many not data scientists. Plant the metaphor (a ladder of confidence), signal it's a studio-side talk, and promise plain language + live, pokeable demos. ~30s.

Title. One mental model: every signal about your game sits on a ladder, from a probabilistic hunch to a mathematical proof. The right-edge rail IS this ladder; it lights up as we climb.

Two games. Introduce them by badge now, fully next slide. SHARDFALL = the running example every demo and worked example uses (consistency). STAR ORACLE = the breadth foil that shows each idea in a totally different genre. This is how we get one coherent spine AND genre breadth.

"Live demos you can poke at." Set the expectation early: this deck computes in front of them — drag a threshold and watch cheats slip under it, run a money-pump loop, tamper a record and watch the hash go red. For a dry topic, interaction is what makes it stick.

02/67·Meet the games
Our worked examples · one spine, one foil

Two games carry the whole talk.

◆ The running example — every demo uses it

SHARDFALL

A competitive live-service shooter-RPG. One title, but it has every integrity surface we need:

  • 1Gunplay — aim, headshots, reaction time → aimbots & evasive cheats
  • 2Ranked ladder — climb for rewards → win-trading rings
  • 3Marketplace + crafting — gold, shards, gems → economy dupes
  • 4Loot crates — paid random drops → RNG / odds integrity
✦ The “other genre” foil — appears at every rung

STAR ORACLE

A gacha hero-collector — totally different shape from SHARDFALL. At each rung we ask: “same idea, different genre — how does it land here?”

  • ↻No gunplay — but auto-battle scripts & bot farms
  • ↻No ranked ladder — but guild collusion & account-selling
  • ↻A deep summon economy and pity timers
  • ↻Banner odds under heavy regulatory scrutiny

Watch for the dashed ✦ in your genre box on each rung — that’s where the technique jumps from SHARDFALL to gacha, MOBA, card games, MMOs, even single-player.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“To keep this concrete, two games carry the whole talk. The first is our running example — every single live demo uses it.”

[point left] “Meet SHARDFALL — a competitive live-service shooter-RPG. I picked it because, like Destiny or Warframe or Apex, one game has every integrity surface I need: there’s gunplay, so there are aimbots. There’s a ranked ladder, so there’s win-trading. There’s a marketplace with crafting, so there are economy dupes. And there are loot crates, so there are odds people don’t trust. One game — we’ll climb it rung by rung.”

[point right] “The second game is our reality check. STAR ORACLE — a gacha hero-collector. Totally different shape: no guns, no ladder, but auto-battle bots, guild collusion, a deep summon economy, and banner odds under real regulatory heat.”

“So here’s the deal: I’ll teach each technique on SHARDFALL, and then — watch for this dashed purple box — I’ll show you the same idea jumping to gacha, MOBAs, card games, even single-player. Because the question in your head is going to be ‘okay, but does this work for my game?’ — and the answer is yes.”

Purpose. Fix the consistency problem head-on: establish ONE running game so the four demos aren't four disconnected contexts, and ONE foil so we still cover genre breadth. Spend ~60s; the audience must hold both names.

SHARDFALL (the spine). A shooter-RPG deliberately chosen because real live-service titles (Destiny 2, Warframe, Apex, Warzone) genuinely contain all four surfaces at once: combat, ranked, an economy, and loot. So one game can plausibly host all four demos — no genre-hopping mid-talk. Every live demo and worked number uses SHARDFALL.

STAR ORACLE (the foil). A gacha hero-collector — structurally the opposite of a shooter. Used ONLY in the dashed "in your genre" callouts to show each technique generalises. This is how we answer "what about MY game?" without diluting the spine.

Reader note. Genres covered across the callouts: gacha, MOBA, trading-card, MMO, racing, single-player. The point each time: the rung's technique is genre-agnostic; only the signal changes.

✦ plain-English terms (for non-gamers)
Live-service game
A game that runs continuously and keeps getting updates, events, and new content — not a one-time purchase you finish. Think an ongoing online world rather than a boxed single-player story.
Shooter-RPG
A blend of a shooting game (aiming, gunfights) and a role-playing game (you level up a character, collect gear, build an inventory).
Gunplay
The shooting mechanics — how aiming and firing work. We measure things like accuracy and reaction time from it.
Aimbot
A cheat program that aims the gun for the player — perfect, inhuman accuracy. A classic shooter cheat.
Ranked ladder
A competitive leaderboard. Win matches, climb the rankings, earn rewards or status. "Ladder" = the ranking itself.
Win-trading
Players secretly agree to lose to each other on purpose so they take turns climbing the ladder — fake wins, not real skill. A "ring" is a group doing this together.
Marketplace / economy
The in-game shop and player trading system — buying, selling, and crafting items with in-game currency (here, gold/shards/gems).
Economy dupe
"Dupe" = duplicate. An exploit that lets a player create currency or items out of nothing (e.g. an endless money loop), which floods and breaks the economy.
Loot crate / loot box
A purchasable mystery box that gives a random reward. You pay, you don't know what you'll get — the "odds" are the published chances of each reward.
RNG
"Random Number Generation" — the randomness behind any chance-based outcome (a crate drop, a critical hit). "RNG integrity" = is that randomness honest?
Gacha
A game genre built around paying for random character/item pulls (named after Japanese capsule-toy machines). Star Oracle is one.
Hero-collector
A gacha game where the goal is collecting a roster of characters ("heroes"), usually via random summons.
Auto-battle / bot farm
"Bot" = an automated program playing instead of a human. A "farm" runs many bots to grind rewards/currency to sell for real money.
Guild
An in-game group/clan of players who team up. "Guild collusion" = a guild coordinating to cheat the system together.
Summon economy / pity timer
"Summon" = a gacha pull for a random hero. A "pity timer" guarantees a top-tier reward after N unlucky pulls, so you're never unlucky forever.
Banner odds
A "banner" is a limited-time event offering specific heroes; "banner odds" are the published drop chances — the thing regulators want disclosed and verified.
MOBA
"Multiplayer Online Battle Arena" — team-vs-team strategy games (e.g. League of Legends, Dota 2).
MMO
"Massively Multiplayer Online" — a huge persistent online world with thousands of players (e.g. World of Warcraft).
03/67·The operator's question
The business problem · 01 ◆ SHARDFALL

Two things that are both true at once.

SHARDFALL’s dashboard says the economy is healthy and matchmaking is fair. SHARDFALL’s subreddit says the crate odds are rigged, the ladder is full of win-traders, and that ban was bogus.

The operator’s real question: “Is my game behaving as I designed it — and how strongly can I actually know it is?” Today, the honest answer is usually: we think so.

◆ SHARDFALL — live ops dashboard● all systems normal

“All green ✅”

Aggregate metrics inside normal bands. Faucets and sinks balanced. No alert fired.

✦ r/SHARDFALL — hot today▲ 2.4k upvotes
▲ 891“These crate odds are rigged — 0 rares in 300 opens.”
▲ 763“There’s a win-trading ring sitting at the top of ranked.”
▲ 540“Banned for nothing. Support won’t even tell me why.”

“It’s rigged.”

Threads on impossible crate luck, a win-trading ring on the ladder, and a wrongful ban — none of which the dashboard can confirm or deny.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Here’s a Tuesday in the life of a SHARDFALL operator. [point to the green card] Your dashboard is all green. Economy metrics inside their bands, matchmaking looks balanced, no alert has fired. By every instrument you own, the game is healthy.”

[point to the red card] “Meanwhile, your subreddit is on fire. ‘The crate odds are rigged.’ ‘There’s a win-trading ring at the top of the ladder.’ ‘I got banned and I did nothing wrong.’”

“And here’s the uncomfortable part: both of these can be true at the same time. Not because someone’s lying — because your dashboard measures the wrong thing. It measures averages. A targeted exploit, a small collusion ring, a quietly tampered drop table — those hide underneath a green average.”

“So the real question every operator is sitting with is this:” [read the gold box] “‘Is my game behaving the way I designed it — and how strongly can I actually know that it is?’ And if we’re honest, today the answer is almost always three words: we think so.” [pause on ‘we think so’ — this phrase comes back at the very end]

Purpose. Frame the problem as tension, anchored in SHARDFALL specifically. The pain isn't "we have cheaters" — it's the gap between what the instruments say and what reality is.

Headline. Both can be true because the dashboard measures the wrong altitude of certainty: aggregate health stays green while a targeted exploit, a ring, or a tampered drop table runs underneath.

The two cards. Left = today's observability (aggregate, lagging). Right = the three perennial accusations (odds, win-trading, bad ban), each of which maps to a later rung. Concretely SHARDFALL's, not generic.

"We think so." Land it; pause. The whole talk replaces this phrase with calibrated answers; the closing slide pays it off with "we can prove it."

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Telemetry
The stream of data the game sends back about what's happening — players' actions, matches, purchases. It's what feeds the dashboards.
Matchmaking
The system that decides who plays against whom, trying to make fair, balanced matches.
Faucets & sinks
Economy plumbing terms. A "faucet" is anything that creates currency (quest rewards); a "sink" is anything that removes it (shop prices, repair costs). Balanced faucets and sinks keep prices stable.
Drop table
The behind-the-scenes list of possible rewards and their odds. "Tampered drop table" = someone secretly changed those odds.
04/67·The stakes (P&L)
The business problem · 02 · why it costs real money

Four places the gap shows up on the P&L.

Revenue erosion

Cheats, fraud, RMT

Refunds, chargebacks, gold-farming, and paying players who quit a cheater-ridden ladder. Direct, measurable loss.

Trust & retention

Churn from doubt

When players stop believing the crate odds or the rank are fair, they stop spending — long before they post about it.

Regulatory exposure

Loot-box scrutiny

Odds-disclosure mandates (China ’17; Korea, Taiwan) and a patchwork of bans & pressure elsewhere mean you may need casino-grade audit trails — depending on your market.

Operational cost

Analyst hours & disputes

Every alert with no rationale is an investigation. Every “I was robbed” is a CS escalation. It scales badly.

Every rung in this talk is accountable to these four. If a technique doesn’t move one of them, it’s a science project — not an investment.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Now, why should anyone fund work on this? Because that gap isn’t academic — it shows up in four places on the P&L.”

[walk the four cards left to right] “One — revenue erosion. Cheats, fraud, gold-farming mean refunds, chargebacks, and your paying players quitting a ladder full of cheaters. Direct, measurable loss. Two — trust and retention. This one’s silent: when players stop believing the odds are fair, they stop spending — long before they ever post about it. Three — regulatory exposure. Loot boxes face a patchwork of regulation — outright bans like Belgium in 2018, mandatory odds-disclosure in China, Korea and Taiwan, and pressure from the EU Parliament — though it’s uneven, and some classification attempts were reversed, like the Netherlands in 2022. If your market tightens, you’ll need casino-grade audit trails whether you like it or not. Four — operational cost. Every alert with no reason attached is an analyst investigation. Every ‘I was robbed’ is a support ticket.”

“Hold onto these four, because they’re my honesty test for the whole talk:” [read the bottom line] “if a technique doesn’t move one of these four numbers, it’s a science project — not an investment. I’m not going to sell you anything that fails that test.”

Purpose. Convert tension into money — the room includes the people who fund the work. Four distinct cost centers we return to when deciding which rungs are worth building.

Revenue erosion. The most concrete; lead ROI; maps to Rung 1.

Trust & retention. The silent one — doubt suppresses spend before any complaint. Maps to Rungs 2 & 6–7 (confidence, then verifiable fairness).

Regulatory exposure. Board-level; loot-box-as-gambling; if it lands you need auditable RNG anyway. Maps to Rung 6 / optional 7.

Operational cost. Felt daily; alerts without rationale = investigation hours. Maps to the cross-cutting "every signal ships a reason" theme.

"Or it's a science project." Credibility line: techniques are judged against these four outcomes only. Pre-empts the tech-for-tech's-sake skeptic.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
RMT
"Real-Money Trading" — selling in-game currency or items for real-world cash, usually against the rules. It's what gold-farming bots feed.
Gold-farming
Running bots or cheap labour to grind in-game currency en masse, then selling it for real money (a form of RMT).
Chargeback
When a player disputes a purchase with their bank and the payment is reversed — the studio loses the money and often pays a fee. Common with stolen cards / fraud.
Churn
Players quitting and not coming back. "Churn from doubt" = players leaving because they no longer trust the game is fair.
P&L
"Profit and Loss" — the business's bottom line. "Shows up on the P&L" = it costs real money.
CS escalation
"CS" = Customer Support. An "escalation" is a ticket a frontline agent has to bump up to a specialist — slow and expensive.
05/67·The traditional toolkit
Baseline · 01 · what studios already do well

The toolkit you already run.

Modern operators are not naive. This stack is serious, and the talk does not replace any of it.

We build on top of this, not throw it away. Respecting the baseline earns the right to talk about what it misses.

  • ✓Server-authoritative simulation — the player’s device isn’t trusted to report the truth.
  • ✓Rules & thresholds — “ban if accuracy > X or actions/min > Y.”
  • ✓Statistical & ML anti-cheat — reputation scoring, bot heuristics.
  • ✓Payment-fraud rules engines — blocklists, velocity checks.
  • ✓Economy dashboards & anomaly alerts — faucets/sinks, spike detection.
  • ✓Manual review & player reports — the human backstop.
▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Before I tell you what’s missing, let me be clear about what you already do — because modern operators are not naive, and I’m not here to pretend you are.”

[gesture down the list, briskly — the room should be nodding] “You run server-authoritative simulation, so the player’s device isn’t trusted to report the truth. You have rules and thresholds. You’ve got statistical and ML anti-cheat, reputation scoring. Payment-fraud engines. Economy dashboards with anomaly alerts. And a human review team behind player reports.”

“This is a serious stack. So I want to say this plainly: nothing in this talk replaces any of it. Everything I show you sits on top of this and closes specific gaps. Respecting what already works is what earns me the right to talk about where it goes blind — which is the next slide.”

Purpose. Disarm the skeptic by naming the real machinery studios run, accurately. An audience that feels understood accepts the critique that follows.

Headline. "This does not replace any of it — it sits alongside and closes specific gaps."

The six items. Walk fast; the room should nod. Name Valve Trust Factor / Activision Ricochet only if re-verified, else describe generically. Listing all six avoids any straw-man accusation.

Strip. The new rungs compose with this. Reader note: Rung 1 (ML detection) is itself "traditional" for big studios — we're honest the novelty is in the higher rungs and the posture, not in inventing anti-cheat.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Server-authoritative
The game's official server — not the player's PC/console — decides what really happened. So a hacked device can't just claim a headshot; the server has the final say.
ML anti-cheat
"ML" = machine learning. Software that learns to spot cheating from data, rather than from hand-written rules.
Reputation scoring
Giving each account a trust score from its history/behaviour, used to prioritise who to watch (e.g. Valve's "Trust Factor").
Heuristic
A rule-of-thumb shortcut — "if clicks-per-second > 20, probably a bot." Quick and useful, but crude and easy to evade.
Velocity check
A fraud rule that flags things happening too fast to be human — e.g. 50 purchases from one account in a minute.
Blocklist
A list of known-bad things (cards, IP addresses, devices) that are automatically denied.
06/67·Where the baseline falls short
Baseline · 02 · the gaps that hit the P&L

Good tools. Four blind spots.

Blind spot · reactive

You find out after the damage

The SHARDFALL dupe is noticed when marketplace prices crater; the bot ring when reports pile up. Tools say “something broke,” rarely “here’s the rule that allowed it.”

Blind spot · evasion

Cheats tune just under the line

A fixed threshold is a target. An aimbot dialed to 54% accuracy sits one notch below a 55% rule and passes clean.

Blind spot · relational

Per-account rules can’t see rings

Win-traders share wins ~50/50, so each looks ordinary. The signal isn’t in any account — it’s in the relationships.

Blind spot · self-trust

You assume your own pipeline is honest

Is the crate service running the odds you configured? The binary you shipped? Usually there’s no artifact that proves it — it’s assumed.

And a fifth, cutting across all of them: most alerts don’t localize the root cause. “Metric X spiked” still costs an analyst hours. Every rung that follows hands back a reason, not just an alarm.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Good tools — but they have four blind spots, and each one maps to a rung we’re about to climb.”

[top-left] “One — they’re reactive. You find out about the dupe when marketplace prices crater; about the bot ring when the reports pile up. The tools tell you something broke; they rarely tell you which rule allowed it.”

[top-right] “Two — evasion. A fixed threshold is a target. An aimbot dialed to 54% accuracy sits one notch under your 55% rule and walks right through. You’ll feel this one yourself in two minutes.”

[bottom-left] “Three — relational blindness. Win-traders split their wins fifty-fifty, so every one of them looks average. The signal isn’t in any account — it’s in the connections between them, and per-account rules literally cannot see that.”

[bottom-right] “Four — self-trust. Is your crate service actually running the odds you configured? Is production running the build you shipped? Most studios just… assume so. There’s no artifact that proves it.”

“And the thread running through all four:” [read the strip] “your alerts don’t tell you where the problem is. ‘Metric X spiked’ still costs an analyst hours. So every rung from here on hands back a reason, not just an alarm.”

Purpose. The pivot. Name precisely where the baseline goes blind, each tied to a SHARDFALL scenario and a P&L cost. These gaps are exactly what the ladder's rungs solve — the structural hinge of the deck. Diagnostic tone, not gleeful.

Reactive → motivates Rung 5 (prove the exploit can't exist before launch).

Evasion → motivates Rung 1 anomaly detection (model "normal," not a fixed line). The 54%-under-55% example is exactly what the next demo lets them feel by dragging a slider.

Relational → motivates Rung 1 graph beat.

Self-trust → motivates Rung 6 — the most under-served gap (studios verify the client, assume the backend).

Fifth gap: no localization. The unifying principle every rung obeys — "hand back a reason." Connects otherwise different techniques (feature attribution, the implicated subgraph, the named tampered record).

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Exploit
A way of using the game's own legal rules in an unintended combination to gain an unfair advantage — not "hacking" the code, just abusing how the rules interact.
Binary / build
The actual compiled program running on the servers. "The build you shipped" = the exact version you released. The worry: is the live server really running that, unchanged?
Pipeline / backend
Your own behind-the-scenes systems — the servers and services that run the game. "Self-trust" = you assume your own backend is honest because nothing checks it.
Client
The game running on the player's device. Studios scrutinise the client heavily but rarely verify their own backend — that's the gap.
Localize the root cause
Pinpoint where the problem is, not just that one exists — "this rule / this service / this record," instead of "a number looks off."
07/67·The idea: the ladder
The idea

One idea: match the strength of your evidence to the stakes.

Every signal sits on a ladder — from a probabilistic suspicion to a mathematical proof. Higher rungs cost more and prove more.

You don’t need every rung. The skill isn’t climbing as high as possible — it’s knowing which rung your problem needs, and stopping there. Pick and choose.

1

Detect · aimbots, win-trading rings

Spot likely abuse — “we suspect, and here’s why.”

Probabilistic
2

Quantify · how sure, provably?

Put a guaranteed error bar on the suspicion.

Calibrated
3

Attribute · did the fix actually work?

Prove cause & effect, not correlation.

Causal
4

Stress-test · break it before players do

Red-team the design & economy pre-launch.

Empirical
5

Verify rules · the infinite-gold dupe

Prove no legal play breaks the economy.

Proof · of the rules
6

Verify pipeline · tampered crate odds

Prove nobody altered the live system.

Proof · cryptographic
7

Expose · regulator-grade odds

Let outsiders check it — “don’t trust us, verify.”

Optional · outward
1–4 statistical · 5–7 proof↑ effort & certainty · harder to fool

The “rank evidence by strength” lens is borrowed from the hierarchy of evidence in evidence-based medicine (Guyatt & Sackett; GRADE) — adapted here to game integrity.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“So here’s the one idea I want you to carry out of this room. Every integrity signal you can produce has a strength — and the skill isn’t producing the strongest possible signal everywhere. It’s matching the strength of your evidence to the stakes of the decision.”

[walk up the ladder, bottom to top — the lower four are statistical, the top three are proof] “Rung one, Detect: ‘we suspect, and here’s why’ — probabilistic. Rung two, Quantify: put a guaranteed error bar on that suspicion. Rung three, Attribute: prove your fix actually caused the improvement, not the season. Rung four, Stress-test: break the design and the economy yourself, before players do. Those four are all statistical — increasingly rigorous, but still about evidence. Then the top three cross into proof: rung five, Verify the rules — no legal play can break the economy. Rung six, Verify the pipeline — nobody altered the running system, cryptographically. And rung seven, Expose — don’t trust us, check it yourself.”

[point to the caption] “As you climb, effort and certainty both rise — and it gets harder to fool. Notice the line at four: below it, statistics; above it, mathematical proof.”

“But here’s the line that matters most, and I mean it:” [read the gold box] “you do not need every rung. Seven rungs is the menu, not the mandate. The skill is knowing which rungs your game needs and stopping there — I’ll show you exactly how to choose at the end.”

Keystone slide. The one image the audience carries out — now seven rungs in two bands: 1–4 statistical (detect → quantify → attribute → stress-test) and 5–7 proof (verify rules → verify pipeline → expose). ~110s.

Headline. Not "use more math" — calibrate. The thesis and the line that makes the talk defensible.

The seven rungs. 1 Detect (probabilistic suspicion) · 2 Quantify (calibrated confidence — conformal) · 3 Attribute (causal effect of interventions) · 4 Stress-test (empirical pre-launch breaking — RL/sim) · 5 Verify rules (formal proof of the spec) · 6 Verify pipeline (cryptographic proof) · 7 Expose (optional outward proof). The "1–4 statistical / 5–7 proof" caption is the key mental grouping — don't let the audience think all seven are the same kind of guarantee.

"You don't need every rung." Even more important now that there are seven — say it firmly. Seven is a menu; most games build 2–3. The §decision slide makes the choice concrete.

08/67·How the next slides work
Before we climb · the pattern for every rung

Each rung, the same rhythm: plain English → the math → a worked example → your game.

A · Analogy

Something you know

Each technique starts with an everyday analogy — no maths, no jargon, names attached last.

B · Compute it

Demo or worked example

Either a live in-page demo you can poke (Rung 1, 5, 6), or a worked example that runs one real data row through the formula to a verdict.

C · Today → with this

Old vs new

The current approach, where it falls short, and the one thing the new way does that it structurally can’t.

D · In your genre

Beyond SHARDFALL

The same idea mapped to gacha, MOBA, card games, MMOs — so it’s your game, not just ours.

Remember the shape: analogy → compute it → today-vs-new → your genre. Same rhythm on every rung — so seven techniques feel familiar in form even when the content is new.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“One quick roadmap, then we climb. Every rung follows the exact same four-beat rhythm, so even when the technique is new, the shape is familiar.”

[tap each card] “First, an everyday analogy — something you already understand, no maths. Then a live demo you watch compute on SHARDFALL data — and several of these you can call out values for and I’ll change them on the fly. Then today versus this — the old way, exactly where it breaks, and the one thing the new way does that the old way structurally can’t. And finally in your genre — the same idea in gacha, MOBA, card games, MMOs.”

“So: analogy, demo, old-versus-new, your genre. Four times. Here we go — Rung one.”

Purpose. Signpost the repeating structure so a lay audience can follow seven unfamiliar techniques without getting lost, and reassure them every technique starts in plain language with a demo they can see compute.

A Analogy. Inclusivity contract — intuition before jargon, every time.

B Live demo. The differentiator for a dry topic: it recomputes live on SHARDFALL data. Tell them they can call out values to try.

C Today → with this → result. The comparison engine: current method + its specific failure; the new method + the one structural thing it adds; the measurable business outcome.

D In your genre. The breadth move via STAR ORACLE and others. Reader note: tell them the rhythm repeats so each new rung feels familiar.

09/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Anomaly, explained
Rung 1 · Detect · technique 1 of 2: anomaly detection ◆ SHARDFALL

Spotting the fake without ever being shown one.

🏦✍️
Imagine…

A bank teller who has handled thousands of real signatures. Nobody taught them what a forgery looks like — but when a fake slides across the counter, it just feels wrong. The slant, the pressure, the speed: each fine alone, but together they don’t match “real.”

1

Study thousands of normal SHARDFALL players across many traits at once — aim steadiness, reaction time, headshot rate.

2

Build a sense of “what normal feels like” — not a rulebook, a feel for the whole crowd.

3

Flag anything that doesn’t fit — even when every single trait, on its own, looks perfectly legal.

The gist: instead of a checklist of what’s bad (which a cheat dodges), it learns what’s normal and notices what’s off — weighing many small oddities together. The fancy name is anomaly detection. Next slide: drag the old rule’s threshold and watch the evasive cheats walk right under it.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Rung one is detection, and the first technique sounds fancy — ‘anomaly detection’ — but here’s the whole idea in a story.”

“Picture a bank teller who’s handled thousands of real signatures. Nobody ever sat them down and taught them what a forgery looks like. But when a fake slides across the counter, it just… feels wrong. The slant, the pressure, the speed — each one could be fine on its own, but together they don’t match what ‘real’ feels like.”

“That’s exactly what we do with SHARDFALL players.” [walk the three steps] “We study thousands of normal players across lots of traits at once — aim steadiness, reaction time, headshot rate. We build a feel for what normal looks like — not a rulebook, a feel. And then we flag anything that doesn’t fit, even when every single trait, on its own, looks perfectly legal.”

“So the punchline:” [read the gist] “instead of a checklist of what’s bad — which a cheat can just dodge — we learn what’s normal and notice what’s off. That’s anomaly detection. And on the next slide, I’m going to let you try to beat it.”

First ELI5 slide. Goal: a non-technical attendee can describe anomaly detection in one sentence after. Rail's bottom rung (cool) lit. ~75s; let the analogy breathe.

Analogy — bank teller. Captures the two ideas: you learn from normal (never labelled fakes), and the giveaway is the combination of traits. Tell it as a story.

Mechanism (plain). Study many normal players × many traits → build a feel for normal → flag what doesn't fit even when each trait alone is legal. Resist "z-score / distribution / feature vector" out loud.

Gist. Attach "anomaly detection" only after intuition lands. Tee up the demo: the contrast "checklist of bad (dodgeable) vs sense of normal (not dodgeable)" becomes literally draggable next.

✦ plain-English terms (the gameplay stats we measure)
Accuracy
The percent of shots that hit. Cheats using an aimbot have unnaturally high accuracy.
Headshot ratio
What fraction of hits land on the head (the hardest, highest-damage spot). Suspiciously high = likely aim assistance.
Reaction time
How fast a player responds to something appearing. Inhuman speed (or eerily consistent speed) suggests automation.
APM
"Actions Per Minute" — how many inputs per minute. Bots are often fast and metronomic; humans are slower and noisier.
"Evasive" cheat
A cheater who deliberately dials every stat to just below the ban thresholds, so each number looks legal on its own.
10/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Anomaly — how it works
Rung 1 · Detect · how anomaly detection actually works — plain-first, formula beside

From “feels off” to a number you can rank.

the recipe — in plain English
  1. Pick the signals. 6 per session: accuracy, headshot %, reaction-time mean & its variability, APM, session length.
  2. Learn what “normal” is from the crowd. For each signal, take the median (the typical value) and the MAD (how much normal players naturally vary).
  3. Score each player by how many “normal-widths” they sit from typical, across all 6 signals at once. A few signals each slightly off → a big combined score. That’s the evasive cheat.
  4. Rank, then cut at the top ~5–9%. Those go to a human, with the single most-off signal printed as the reason.
the score, for the engineersrobust z · per session
# median & MAD are computed over ALL players,
# per feature — they define "normal".
z[f] = (x[f] − median[f]) / (1.4826 · MAD[f])
score = Σf z[f]²            # sum across 6 features
flag  = score ≥ quantile(scores, 0.93)
reason = argmaxf |z[f]|       # the dominant signal
every symbol, decoded x[f] player’s value for feature f · median[f] typical value · MAD[f] normal spread · 1.4826 makes MAD comparable to std-dev for normal data · z[f] “normal-widths” from typical · 0.93 cutoff = top 7% reviewed (your precision/recall dial).
why median & MAD, not mean & std-dev? Cheats in the data inflate the mean and std-dev and hide themselves (“masking”). Median/MAD are robust — outliers barely move them — so cheats stay far from “normal.”

Honest limits: summing per-feature z² treats features as independent — it ignores correlation (that’s what Mahalanobis on the next slide fixes); it assumes most of your data is genuinely normal; needs features a cheat can’t trivially fake; and the cutoff is a precision/recall dial, not a truth line. It ranks suspects — a human still decides.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Let me show you exactly how ‘feels off’ becomes a number — because this is simpler than it looks. [walk the left column] Pick six signals per player. For each one, learn two things from the whole crowd: the typical value, and how much normal players naturally vary. Then score every player by how many of those ‘normal-widths’ they sit away from typical — added up across all six. A blatant bot is wildly off on one axis; the evasive cheat is a little off on several, and the sum still lights up. Rank everyone, take the top few percent, and print the single most-off signal as the reason.”

[gesture to the code panel — for the engineers] “For the engineers: this is a robust z-score. The one design choice that matters — and the reason naive versions fail — is on the right: [read the formula box] if you use the average and standard deviation, the cheats sitting in your data inflate those numbers and camouflage themselves. Use the median and MAD instead and they can’t hide. That single swap is the difference between catching the evasive cohort and missing it.”

“And be honest about the limits — [read the strip] it assumes most of your data is normal, the features have to be ones a cheat can’t trivially fake, and that cutoff is a dial you tune, not a law. It ranks; a human decides.”

Depth slide — anomaly mechanism. Practitioner-level: the recipe in plain English (left), the actual robust-z formula (right), and the one insight that makes or breaks it (median/MAD vs mean/std). This is the slide that turns "understands the idea" into "could prototype it."

The masking point. The single most important teaching beat on this slide: naive mean/std-dev anomaly detection fails on evasive cheats because the cheats inflate the very statistics meant to catch them. Robust statistics (median/MAD) fix it. If an engineer takes one thing home, it's this.

Honest limits. Say them out loud — assumes mostly-normal data, needs un-fakeable features, the threshold is a precision/recall trade. Credibility.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Median
The middle value when you line everyone up — the "typical" player. Unlike the average, a few extreme cheats barely shift it.
MAD (median absolute deviation)
A robust measure of "how spread out" normal players are — like standard deviation, but it ignores a handful of outliers instead of being dragged by them.
Robust z-score
How many "normal-widths" a value sits from typical, measured so a few cheats in the data can't disguise themselves. Big total = suspicious.
Masking
When outliers inflate the average/spread so much that they end up looking "normal" — the trap that naive anomaly detection falls into.
Precision / recall
Precision = of those you flag, how many are real cheats. Recall = of all real cheats, how many you catch. The cutoff trades one for the other.
11/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Anomaly — your data & I/O
Rung 1 · Detect · the inputs, the outputs, and where to get them

What you feed it — and where that data already lives.

▼ INPUT — one row per player-session
FieldWhat it meansType / rangeWhere it comes from
player_idWho/which session (the key, not a feature)idmatch service / session log
accuracyshots hit ÷ shots fired0–1combat telemetry → hit events
headshot_ratioheadshots ÷ kills0–1combat telemetry → damage events
reaction_ms_meanavg ms from enemy-visible → first shotmsserver tick log + render/visibility
reaction_ms_stdvariability of that reaction (humans = noisy)msderived from the same events
actions_per_mininput events per minutecountclient input stream / server cmds
session_minsession length (context, normalizes the rest)minsession start/stop events
▲ OUTPUT — one row per flagged session  {player_id, score, is_flagged, top_reason_feature, per_feature_z[]} → review queue + the LLM copilot

The data contract is the whole job. If you can emit these 6 columns to a CSV or a Kinesis stream, the detector runs. No labels needed to run it (it learns “normal” unsupervised); you only need a few labeled cases to tune the cutoff. Start with whatever your telemetry already logs; add fields as you go.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Everyone asks the same question after the last slide: ‘okay — but what do I actually feed it, and where do I get that?’ So here’s the real data contract.”

[walk the input table] “One row per player-session, and just six columns. Accuracy is hits over shots — it’s already in your combat telemetry. Headshot ratio, same place. Reaction time is the milliseconds from when an enemy becomes visible to the first shot — that’s your server tick log joined with visibility; its variability falls out of the same events, and that’s the gold one, because humans are noisy and scripts are metronomic. Actions-per-minute comes off the input stream. Session length is context. Point: every one of these is something you already emit, or can — you are not instrumenting from scratch.”

[output line] “What comes back is one row per flagged session: the score, the flag, the single most-off feature as the reason, and the full per-feature breakdown — which is exactly what feeds the analyst copilot we’ll meet in a minute.”

[strip] “And the liberating part: you need no labels to run it — it learns ‘normal’ from the crowd. You only need a handful of known cases to tune where you cut. If you can write these six columns to a CSV, you can run this on your game this week.”

Depth slide — anomaly I/O & data sourcing. The missing buildability layer: every input field with meaning, type, AND the concrete telemetry source; the exact output schema; and the data-contract framing. This is what lets a dev actually wire it into their system.

Unsupervised to run, few labels to tune. Key practical point: this needs no labeled cheat data to operate (it models normal); labels only calibrate the threshold. Removes the #1 "we don't have training data" objection.

Sources are deliberately generic. "combat telemetry," "server tick log," "input stream" — every studio has equivalents. The message: start with what you already log.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Feature
One measurable input column the model uses (accuracy, reaction time…). "Feature engineering" = choosing/deriving good ones.
Telemetry event
A single recorded thing-that-happened (a shot, a hit, a session start). Features are computed by aggregating these.
Server tick
The server's heartbeat (e.g. 30–60×/sec). Tick logs timestamp what was true each moment — used to measure true reaction time.
Unsupervised
Learns from data with no "this one's a cheat" labels — it models normal and flags deviation. (Supervised = needs labeled examples.)
Data contract
The agreed schema (field names, types) your pipeline must emit for the detector to run — the integration boundary.
Kinesis
An AWS service for streaming events in real time — one way to pipe live telemetry to the detector (a CSV works for offline).
12/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Anomaly — worked example
Rung 1 · Detect · one real row → the formula → the verdict — the whole pipeline on one player

Let’s run one evasive cheat through the math.

1
your data — one session row (from the table you just saw) Player p412: accuracy 0.54, headshot 0.38, reaction_mean 146 ms, reaction_std 14 ms, apm 168, session 52 min. Every value looks legal on its own — under the 0.55 / 175 / 130 rules.
2
what “normal” is — the crowd’s median & MAD (computed once, over all players)
feature median MAD accuracy 0.34 0.07 headshot 0.22 0.06 reaction_mean 270 45 reaction_std 60 15 apm 95 28 session 35 18
3
plug into z[f] = (x − median) / (1.4826 · MAD) — “normal-widths from typical”
accuracy: (0.54−0.34)/(1.4826·0.07) = +1.9 headshot: (0.38−0.22)/(1.4826·0.06) = +1.8 reaction: (146−270)/(1.4826·45) = −1.9 (too fast) react_std: (14−60)/(1.4826·15) = −2.1 (too steady — the tell) apm: (168−95)/(1.4826·28) = +1.8 session: (52−35)/(1.4826·18) = +0.6
4
score = Σ z² · flag if ≥ cutoff · reason = biggest |z|
score = 1.9²+1.8²+1.9²+2.1²+1.8²+0.6² = 18.3 cutoff (93rd pct of all scores) = ~9.5 18.3 ≥ 9.5 → FLAGGED · reason = react_std (|−2.1|)

⚑ Output row: {p412, score 18.3, flagged, reason "reaction too consistent"} → review queue. No single stat broke a rule — but six small oddities summed past the line. That’s the evasive cheat the thresholds cleared, caught.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — the bridge that makes the formula real (verbatim)

“I’ve shown you the data and the formula on separate slides. Now let me connect them completely — one real player, all the way through, so there’s zero mystery left.”

[step 1] “Here’s player p412 — a session row exactly like the table two slides back. Look at the values: 54% accuracy, 146-millisecond reaction, and so on. Every one is under the old ban thresholds. The rules engine waves p412 through.”

[step 2] “Now the ‘normal’ we learned from the whole crowd — the median and MAD for each feature. This is computed once, offline, and reused.”

[step 3 — walk two rows] “Plug each value in. Accuracy: 0.54 minus the 0.34 median, over 1.4826 times the MAD, gives plus-1.9 — almost two normal-widths high. But watch reaction-variability: minus-2.1. A human’s aim timing is noisy; p412’s is robotically steady. That’s the tell no single threshold was even looking at.”

[step 4] “Square them, add them up: 18.3. Our cutoff — the 93rd percentile of everyone’s score — is about 9.5. Eighteen is way over. p412 is flagged, and the reason printed is ‘reaction too consistent,’ because that was the biggest single deviation.”

[verdict] “And there’s the whole point in one row: no stat broke a rule, but six small oddities summed past the line — with a plain-English reason attached. That output row is what lands in your review queue and feeds the copilot.”

Depth slide — anomaly WORKED EXAMPLE. The explicit data→formula→verdict bridge the user asked for. Takes one evasive-cheat row (p412, matching the demo's flagged session), the crowd's median/MAD, computes all six robust-z values by hand, sums to the score, compares to the cutoff, and emits the output row. Demystifies the formula completely — nothing left abstract.

Numbers are illustrative-but-faithful. The medians/MADs mirror generate_data.py's legit distributions; the z-values and score are arithmetically correct for the stated inputs. The verdict (flagged, reason=react_std) matches the demo's behaviour for evasive cheats.

The teaching climax of Rung 1. This is where "I understand anomaly detection" becomes "I could compute it." Linger on step 3 (the react_std tell) and step 4 (small oddities summing).

13/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Anomaly — the methods menu
Rung 1 · Detect · not one formula — a menu · pick by your constraints

Several ways to score “how abnormal.” Here’s when to use which.

MethodThe formula / ideaBest when…Watch out
Robust z-sum our demoΣ ((x−median)/(1.4826·MAD))²Few features, want a transparent, explainable score on day oneAssumes features independent & ~symmetric
Mahalanobis distance(x−μ)ᵀ Σ⁻¹ (x−μ)Features are correlated (accuracy ↔ headshot%); want one principled distanceNeeds a stable covariance Σ; sensitive to outliers unless robustified
Isolation Forestrandom splits; “how few cuts to isolate this point”Many features, non-linear, want it to scale; no distribution assumedLess directly explainable; tune contamination
Autoencoder (neural)train to reconstruct normal; flag high reconstruction errorHigh-dim / sequ-ish data, lots of normal samples, GPU availableHeaviest to train & operate; needs MLOps
LOF (local outlier factor)density of a point vs its neighbours’“Normal” has several distinct clusters (playstyles)O(n²)-ish; struggles at large scale

Same inputs, same outputs — swap the engine in the middle. Start with robust z-sum (explainable, no training); graduate to Isolation Forest or an Autoencoder on SageMaker AI as feature count and scale grow. Attach SHAP to any of them to keep the “why” for your reviewer. The ladder rung doesn’t change — only the scorer does.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“I showed you one formula, but I’d be lying if I implied it’s the only one — so here’s the actual menu, and how to choose. The good news: all of them take the same six-column input and produce the same kind of score-plus-reason output. You’re only swapping the engine in the middle.”

[walk the rows] “Robust z-sum — what our demo uses — is the one to start with: transparent, no training, runs today. Move to Mahalanobis distance the moment your features are correlated — accuracy and headshot rate move together, and Mahalanobis accounts for that with the covariance matrix. Isolation Forest is your workhorse at scale and many features — it makes no assumption about the shape of the data. An autoencoder is the heavy option when you’ve got high-dimensional or sequence data and the MLOps to train it. And LOF shines when ‘normal’ is actually several clusters — different legit playstyles.”

[strip] “The point for your architecture: the rung doesn’t change, the scorer does. Begin explainable and cheap; graduate to SageMaker AI as you grow. And whichever you pick, bolt SHAP on top so every flag still carries its ‘why’ — because a detection without a reason is useless to your reviewer.”

Depth slide — the methods menu. Directly answers "are there multiple formulae?" — yes, five, brought together with the formula, the selection criterion, and the failure mode for each. Turns a single recipe into an engineering decision.

The unifying message. Same I/O contract, swappable scorer. So choosing a method is a constraint problem (feature count, correlation, scale, explainability, MLOps maturity), not a leap of faith.

Progression advice. Robust-z → Mahalanobis → Isolation Forest → Autoencoder as scale/dimensionality grow; SHAP throughout for explainability. This is the practitioner's roadmap.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Mahalanobis distance
A distance that accounts for features moving together — it won't double-count "high accuracy" and "high headshot %" as two separate surprises when they're really one.
Covariance (Σ)
A table of how each pair of features varies together. Mahalanobis uses it; it needs enough clean data to estimate stably.
Isolation Forest
Flags points that are easy to "cut off" from the rest with a few random splits — abnormal points isolate quickly. Scales well, assumes nothing about shape.
Autoencoder
A neural net trained to recreate normal sessions; if it reconstructs one badly (high error), that session is abnormal.
LOF (local outlier factor)
Compares how crowded a point's neighbourhood is vs its neighbours' — good when "normal" is several distinct clusters.
SHAP
A method that explains which features drove a model's score and by how much — how you keep a "why" on every flag, even for complex models.
MLOps
The operational plumbing to train, deploy, monitor, and retrain a model in production — the cost of the heavier methods.
14/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Anomaly — before & after
Rung 1 · Detect · before → after · the code and the validation

Same data. Swap the method. Watch the numbers move.

● BEFORE — fixed thresholdstraditional
def is_cheat(p):
    return (p.accuracy > 0.55
         or p.apm      > 175
         or p.reaction < 130)
# one axis at a time; a hard line
validation on the labeled session set bots 45/45 · evasive 0/35 · false alarms 9
precision 0.83 · recall 0.56 · F1 0.67
● AFTER — robust anomaly scorerecommended
med, mad = median(X), mad(X)      # per feature
Z      = (X − med) / (1.4826·mad)
score  = (Z**2).sum(axis=1)
flag   = score >= quantile(score, .93)
reason = FEATURES[ argmax(abs(Z)) ]
same sessions, same labels bots 45/45 · evasive 35/35 · false alarms 12
precision 0.87 · recall 1.00 · F1 0.93

The trade is explicit and honest: +3 false alarms (9→12) bought every evasive cheat (0→35) and F1 0.67 → 0.93. That’s the right trade because the output goes to a human, not an auto-ban. (Numbers from detect_behavior.py in the kit — re-run it yourself.)

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Here’s the before and after, with the actual code and the actual scorecard — the same labeled session set both times. [left] The old way is three hard lines, one axis at a time. Its report card: catches every blatant bot, but zero of thirty-five evasive cheats, and recall sits at 0.56.”

[right] “The new way is the five lines we just built — robust score, cut at the 93rd percentile, reason attached. Same data, same labels. It catches all thirty-five evasive cheats; recall goes to a perfect 1.0, F1 from 0.67 to 0.93.”

[read the strip] “And I’m showing you the cost too: it raised three more false alarms. That is the right trade here — and only because these go to a human reviewer, never an auto-ban. This isn’t a slide claim; it’s detect_behavior.py in the take-home kit. Run it on your laptop and you’ll get these numbers.”

Depth slide — anomaly before/after. The user's explicit ask: traditional code + its validation, vs improved code + its validation, side by side. Numbers are the real output of detect_behavior.py.

The honest trade. Don't hide the +2 false alarms — naming the cost is what makes the recall win credible, and it reinforces the "feeds human review" boundary.

F1. If asked: F1 is the single combined score of precision & recall (their harmonic mean) — one number to compare the two methods. 0.68 → 0.92 is a large, real jump.

15/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Anomaly — LIVE
▶ This is a live interactive demo in the full deck (drag / run / tap). Below is its surrounding content; open confidence-ladder-deck-v3.html on a laptop to run it.
Rung 1 · Detect · LIVE DEMO — drag the threshold ◆ SHARDFALL
SHARDFALL sessions — accuracy vs aim-consistency · 200 players
old rule: accuracy > 55%
legit player blatant bot evasive cheat ○ open = cleared · ● filled = flagged
Blatant bots caught0 / 30
Evasive cheats caught0 / 25
False alarms (legit flagged)0
Drag the threshold — watch the evasive cheats (orange) hide just under any line you pick.

The orange cheats tuned every stat to sit just inside legal. A line — anywhere — misses them. “Anomaly detection” mode ignores the lines and flags whatever is far from the normal crowd, combination and all.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — with the live demo (verbatim + actions)

[orient them first — point to the legend under the chart] “Each dot is a SHARDFALL player. Left-to-right is aim accuracy; bottom-to-top is how robotic their aim is. The colours are the ground truth we happen to know for this demo: blue is legit, red are blatant bots up in the corner, and these orange dots — keep your eye on them — are evasive cheats who tuned every stat to sit just inside legal.”

[set up how a ‘flag’ reads on screen] “And here’s how the system votes: a dot that’s drawn hollow has been cleared; a dot that fills in solid is one the detector flagged. So watch the dots fill and empty as I change the method — that’s the system deciding, live.”

“Today’s tool is a threshold. Ban above 55% accuracy.” [point to the vertical line] “Everything to the right of that line fills in — flagged. It catches the red bots. But watch the orange.”

[drag the slider DOWN toward 45%] “I want those orange cheats, so I lower the line… and look at the false-alarm counter — it’s climbing fast. I’m now banning legit players, and I still haven’t caught the orange ones. [drag back UP] Raise it to make the false alarms go away — and the evasive count is back to zero. The rule cannot win. There is no good place to put that line.”

[click ‘switch to anomaly detection’] “Now watch what happens when I stop drawing lines and instead model normal.” [the ellipse appears] “That ring is ‘what normal players look like.’ Anything far from it lights up — and look: the bots and the orange evasive cheats are caught, with barely any false alarms. [point to the evasive counter flipping green] Same data. The difference is it’s judging the whole combination, not one axis.”

[the crucial honesty beat — say this so nobody misunderstands] “One thing to be straight about: in this demo I coloured the cheats so you could follow along. In your real game you do not get those colours — every dot arrives grey. The detector never sees a label saying ‘bot.’ All it does is measure how far each session sits from the normal crowd, score it, and rank it. It isn’t declaring guilt — it’s saying ‘these top few percent look least like everyone else; a human should look here first,’ and it hands over the most-off signal as the reason why.”

“That’s the headline of Rung one:” [read the strip] “a threshold is a target an adversary optimises against. A model of normal is not.”

LIVE DEMO 1 — the threshold trap, made physical. This is the "aha" for evasion. Drag the accuracy threshold across its range: the blue legit cloud, the red blatant bots (top-right, caught by almost any line), and the ORANGE evasive cheats sitting deliberately just under/left of the line. No matter where you drag, the evasive cheats stay uncaught and you start sweeping up legit players as false alarms.

How to run it on stage. (1) Start at 55%. Point out: bots caught, evasive 0, few false alarms. (2) Drag the line DOWN to catch the evasive ones — show the false-alarm counter spiking (you're now banning legit players). (3) Drag UP — false alarms vanish but evasive caught stays 0. The rule can't win. (4) Hit "switch to anomaly detection": the line disappears, an ellipse of "normal" appears, and the points FAR from the crowd light up — bots AND evasive caught, few false alarms — because it judges the whole combination, not one axis.

The teaching point. A threshold is a target an adversary optimises against; a model of normal is not. Say it as they watch it. This is the demo, not a slide claim.

Honesty. Anomaly mode still raises some false alarms (a few legit on the fringe) — correct, because it surfaces for review, never auto-bans. Numbers are computed live in-browser.

16/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Graph, explained
Rung 1 · Detect · technique 2 of 2: graph analysis ◆ SHARDFALL

The conspiracy you can’t see one suspect at a time.

🕵️🧵📌
Imagine…

A detective’s corkboard. Interview any one suspect and they seem ordinary. But pin up who meets whom and stretch string between them — and a tight little cluster lights up. The plot was never in one person. It was in the connections.

1

A dot for every SHARDFALL player, a line between two each time they’re matched on the ladder.

2

Win-traders light up as a tight knot — they face each other far more than chance would ever explain.

3

The shape gives them away, even though each player’s own win rate looks completely normal.

The gist: some cheating only exists between people — win-trading, boosting, fraud rings. Looking at the map of who-plays-whom reveals the group. The fancy name is a graph; the learning version is a graph neural network (GNN). Next slide: build the matches yourself and watch the ring appear.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Second technique on Rung one. Some cheating you simply cannot see one player at a time — and here’s the story for it.”

“Think of a detective’s corkboard. You interview any one suspect and they seem totally ordinary. But you pin up who meets whom, you stretch string between them — and suddenly a tight little cluster lights up. The plot was never in any one person. It was in the connections.”

“Same thing on the SHARDFALL ladder.” [walk the three steps] “Draw a dot for every player, and a line between two players every time they’re matched. Win-traders show up as a tight knot — they face each other far more than chance could ever explain. And the shape gives them away, even though each player’s own win rate looks completely normal.”

[read the gist] “That map of who-plays-whom is called a graph. The learning version that gets really good at spotting these patterns is a graph neural network. Don’t worry about the name — on the next slide you’re going to build the matches yourself and watch the ring appear.”

Second ELI5. Paired with anomaly because both are Rung 1. Goal: understand that some abuse is relational — invisible per-account, obvious as structure.

Analogy — corkboard. The string-and-pins image carries it: one suspect innocent; the web reveals the conspiracy. Maps exactly to win-trading where each colluder's record is unremarkable.

Mechanism. A dot per player, a line per match = literally "a graph"; say so to demystify the word. Colluders = a tight knot (face each other far more than chance). Shape betrays them despite normal individual stats. Avoid "embeddings / topology / adjacency."

Gist. "Graph = map of connections," then "GNN = the learning version." Tee up the build-the-ring demo.

17/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Graph — how it works
Rung 1 · Detect · how graph ring-detection actually works — plain-first, formula beside

Turn matches into a map, then find the dense knots.

the recipe — in plain English
  1. Build the map. A dot (node) per player; a line (edge) between two players, weighted by how many times they were matched.
  2. Keep only the “suspiciously frequent” lines. Normal pairs meet once or twice. Win-traders meet 8–10×. Keep edges far above the typical pair-count.
  3. Find connected clusters in what’s left. A clump of players all heavily linked to each other = a candidate ring.
  4. Score & surface the cluster, not the account. Output the whole ring with its internal edges as the evidence a reviewer sees.
the transparent version — for the engineersgraph clustering
for (a,b) in matches:           # weighted edge
    w[(a,b)] += 1
strong = { e for e,c in w
           if c >= 4 · median(w.values) }
rings  = connected_components(strong)
return [r for r in rings if len(r) >= 3]
…and what a production GNN adds Instead of a hand-set “4× median” rule, a graph neural network learns an embedding for each node from its neighbourhood, so a ring is flagged by its learned pattern — robust to colluders who vary their match counts to dodge a fixed cutoff.

Honest limits: needs enough interactions to form structure (cold-start is hard); a clever ring can inject “camouflage” matches; clustering finds candidates — a human confirms intent. The transparent version runs in <1s; the GNN needs training data & a graph store.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“How does ‘look at who-plays-whom’ become an actual detector? Four steps. [left column] Build the map — a dot per player, a line between two players weighted by how often they were matched. Throw away the ordinary lines: normal opponents meet once or twice, win-traders meet eight or ten times, so keep only the lines far above typical. What’s left forms clumps — find the connected clusters. And you surface the whole cluster as the evidence, not a lone account.”

[code panel] “For the engineers, the transparent version is just this: tally weighted edges, keep the strong ones, take connected components of size three or more. It runs in under a second, which is why the live demo can do it on stage. [formula box] What does a real graph neural network add? Instead of me hand-picking ‘four times the median,’ the GNN learns each player’s pattern from their neighbourhood — so it still catches a ring that deliberately varies its match counts to slip under a fixed cutoff.”

[strip] “Limits, honestly: it needs enough play to form structure, a smart ring can pad itself with camouflage matches, and clustering gives you candidates — a human confirms the intent.”

Depth slide — graph mechanism. Demystifies "graph/GNN": node, edge, weight, strong-edge filter, connected components — the exact transparent algorithm the live demo runs — then names precisely what a trained GNN adds over it (learned embeddings vs a hand-set threshold).

Transparent-vs-GNN. Important honesty: the demo is graph clustering, not a trained GNN. This slide states what the GNN buys you (robustness to count-varying rings) so nobody thinks clustering == GNN.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Node / edge / weight
Node = a player (a dot). Edge = a line between two players who interacted. Weight = how strong that link is (here, how many matches they shared).
Connected component
A clump of dots all reachable from each other through edges — once you keep only suspicious links, each clump is a candidate ring.
Embedding
A compact list of numbers a model learns to summarise each player's position in the network, so similar/suspicious patterns sit close together.
GNN (graph neural network)
A model that learns those embeddings from the graph's shape, rather than you hand-writing a rule like "4× the median."
Cold-start
The early period when there's too little data (few matches) for any structure to show — detection is weak until enough interactions accumulate.
18/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Graph — your data & I/O
Rung 1 · Detect · the inputs, the outputs, and where to get them

A graph is just two tables you probably already have.

▼ INPUT — edges (one row per interaction between two players)
FieldWhat it meansTypeWhere it comes from
player_a, player_bthe two players who interacted (the edge)id, idmatch history / lobby log
edge_typematched · traded · gifted · groupedenummatch svc · economy svc · party svc
weighthow many times (or total value traded)count / $aggregate of the events above
winner (opt)who won, for win-trading signalsidmatch result log
timestampwhen (to window “recent” behaviour)timeevery event already has one

Optional node table — per-player attributes (account age, region, device) that a GNN can also learn from. Pure graph-clustering needs only the edges above.

▲ OUTPUT — one row per suspected ring  {ring_id, [member_ids], internal_edge_evidence, density_score}

If you can list “who interacted with whom,” you have a graph. No new instrumentation — match history is the edge list. Store it in a graph DB (Amazon Neptune) for scale, or build it in memory from a CSV for a first pass.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Same question for the graph: what do I feed it, where’s the data? And the answer is almost anticlimactic — a graph is just two tables you already have.”

[input table] “The main one is an edge list: player A, player B, and how they’re connected — matched, traded, gifted, grouped — with a weight for how often, and a timestamp so you can ask ‘what happened recently.’ That’s your match history and your trade log; you’re not instrumenting anything new. Optionally add a winner column for win-trading, and a node table of per-player attributes — account age, region, device — which a GNN can also learn from.”

[output line] “Out the other side: one row per suspected ring — the member list, the internal edges as evidence, and a density score for triage.”

[strip] “The headline: if you can list who-interacted-with-whom, you already have a graph. Put it in Neptune when you need scale, or just build it in memory from a CSV to prove the value first.”

Depth slide — graph I/O & data sourcing. Demystifies "a graph": it's an edge list (+ optional node attributes), both of which are existing logs (match history, trade log). Output schema is the ring + evidence. Removes the "we'd have to build a graph system" objection.

Edges are existing logs. The core reassurance: match/trade history IS the edge list. Neptune for scale, CSV/in-memory for a first pass.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Edge list
A simple table of "A connected to B" rows — the minimal way to describe a graph. Your match history already is one.
Node table
Optional per-player attributes (age, region, device) attached to each dot — extra signal a GNN can learn from.
Edge weight
A number on each link saying how strong it is — match count, or total value traded.
Density score
How tightly interconnected a cluster is — high density among a small group is the win-trading fingerprint.
Amazon Neptune
AWS's managed graph database — stores nodes/edges and runs graph queries at scale (vs building it in memory for a prototype).
19/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Graph — worked example
Rung 1 · Detect · your edge list → the filter → the ring — the whole pipeline on six players

From a match log to a named ring, by the numbers.

1
your data — match-pair counts (straight from the edge-list table)
# how many times each pair was matched this season (c984,c985)=9 (c984,c986)=8 (c985,c986)=10 ← suspicious (c984,c987)=8 (c985,c987)=9 ... all 15 pairs high (p21,p640)=1 (p21,p88)=2 (p640,p303)=1 ← ordinary
2
learn “typical” — the median pair-count across the whole graph
median(all pair counts) = 1 # normal opponents meet ~once strong-edge threshold = 4 · median = 4
3
keep only edges ≥ threshold, then take connected components
# keep pairs with count ≥ 4 : all 15 (c984…c989) pairs survive ✓ every (p…) ordinary pair dropped ✗ (count 1–2) connected_components(strong) → { c984,c985,c986,c987,c988,c989 }
4
size filter (≥ 3) → surface the cluster with its edges as evidence
cluster size = 6 ≥ 3 → RING each member’s solo win-rate ≈ 50% # invisible per-account

⚑ Output: {ring_id 3, members [c984…c989], 15 internal strong edges} → reviewer. Not one of these accounts is unusual alone — the ring exists only in the edges between them, and the median-based filter makes it pop out with no training.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — the bridge that makes the filter real (verbatim)

“Same move as before: I’ve shown you the edge list and the algorithm separately — now watch one real cluster go all the way through.”

[step 1] “Here are pair-counts straight from your match log. Six accounts — c984 through c989 — keep facing each other eight, nine, ten times. Meanwhile ordinary players meet a given opponent once, maybe twice.”

[step 2] “Learn ‘typical’: the median pair-count across the entire graph is one. So I set the ‘suspicious’ bar at four times that — a count of four.”

[step 3] “Keep only edges at or above four, throw the rest away. Every ordinary pair vanishes; all fifteen pairs among the six colluders survive. Take the connected components of what’s left — and the six of them fall out as one clump.”

[step 4] “Size three or more, so it’s a ring. And here’s the kicker — check each member’s own win rate: about fifty percent. Individually invisible. The signal was never in an account; it was in the edges.”

[verdict] “Output: ring 3, these six members, fifteen internal strong edges as the evidence — handed to a reviewer. That’s a match log turned into a named ring, with nothing but a median and a connected-components pass.”

Depth slide — graph WORKED EXAMPLE. Edge-list → median threshold → strong-edge filter → connected components → ring, on the demo's actual c984–c989 ring. Connects the data table to the algorithm to the output, by the numbers. The 50%-win-rate line ties back to why per-account is blind.

Numbers match the demo. The c984–c989 ring is exactly what detect_rings.py / the live demo surface; counts (8–10 intra vs 1–2 ordinary) and the 4×median rule are the real algorithm.

20/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Graph — the methods menu
Rung 1 · Detect · several ways to find the knot · pick by your constraints

From a one-line rule to a learned model.

MethodThe ideaBest when…Watch out
Strong-edge + components our demokeep edges ≥ k×median; take connected clustersDay-one, explainable, blatant repeated-matchup ringsOne hand-set threshold k; misses subtle rings
Community detection (Louvain)partition the graph to maximise “modularity”You want natural clusters with no preset countResolution param; clusters ≠ guilt, still needs review
Node2Vec embeddingsrandom walks → a vector per node → cluster vectorsPatterns subtler than raw edge counts; no labelsTune walk length/dims; embeddings need a downstream model
GNN — GraphSAGE / GAT productionlearn from neighbourhood + node features; output a per-node/per-edge riskSubtle, evolving rings; you have some labels & node attributesHeaviest; needs a graph store, training, MLOps
Temporal / graph-transformer SOTAadd when edges form + global attention past local camouflageBursty win-trading windows; rings that pad their neighbourhood to hideMost data/compute-hungry; newest, least off-the-shelf
Money-flow / cycle findingfollow directed value transfers; find farm→mule→buyer pathsRMT & fraud rings on the transaction graphNeeds signed/directed edges; launderers add hops

Same edge list in, same “rings + evidence” out — the cluster-finder is what changes. Start with strong-edge components (transparent, instant); graduate to a GNN on SageMaker AI when rings get subtle and you’ve gathered labels. Keep the implicated subgraph as the reviewer’s “why” at every level.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“And just like anomaly, there isn’t one graph method — there’s a menu, and they all take the same edge list and return the same ‘rings plus evidence.’ You’re swapping the cluster-finder.”

[walk the rows] “Start with strong-edge plus connected components — our demo — transparent and instant, great for blatant repeated-matchup rings. Louvain community detection finds natural clusters without you guessing how many. Node2Vec turns each player into a vector from random walks, so you can catch patterns subtler than raw counts. The production answer for subtle, evolving rings is a GNN — GraphSAGE or GAT — which learns from both the neighbourhood and your node attributes, but it’s the heaviest to operate. And if you’re chasing real-money trading, you want money-flow cycle finding on the directed transaction graph — that’s how you surface farm-to-mule-to-buyer chains.”

[strip] “Same input, same output, swappable engine. Start transparent; graduate to the GNN on SageMaker AI once rings get subtle and you’ve collected labels — and always keep the implicated subgraph as the reviewer’s evidence.”

Depth slide — graph methods menu. Five relational methods with idea/fit/caveat, including the transaction-graph (money-flow) angle for RMT. Same I/O, swappable finder.

Two graph problems. Note the menu spans both the interaction graph (matches → win-trading) and the transaction graph (value transfers → RMT). Same machinery, different edge semantics.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Community detection / Louvain
Algorithms that split a graph into naturally tight-knit groups, no preset number needed. "Modularity" scores how clump-y a split is.
Node2Vec / embeddings
Turns each player into a list of numbers by "walking" the graph, so similar network positions sit close — then you cluster those numbers.
GraphSAGE / GAT
Two popular graph-neural-network designs that learn a player's risk from its neighbourhood (GAT adds "attention" — weighting which neighbours matter).
Directed / signed edge
An edge with a direction (A→B value moved) or sign — needed to trace money flow from farmer to mule to buyer.
Subgraph evidence
The specific nodes-and-edges of the flagged ring, handed to a reviewer as the visual "why."
21/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Graph — before & after
Rung 1 · Detect · before → after · per-account vs relational

The exact same accounts — invisible, then obvious.

● BEFORE — per-account win-ratetraditional
def suspicious(p):
    # judge each account alone
    return p.games >= 5        and p.win_rate > 0.75
validation · 40 colluders in 12 rings ring members caught 0/40
win-traders share wins ~50/50 → win-rate looks normal
● AFTER — relational graphrecommended
# judge the relationships
strong = edges with count >= 4×median
rings  = connected_components(strong)
flag   = members of rings (size >= 3)
same 40 colluders, same matches ring members caught 40/40 · false members 0
8 rings surfaced, each with its internal edges as evidence

Per-account scoring is structurally blind here — no threshold on a single account’s stats can ever catch a 50%-win-rate colluder. The signal only exists between accounts. (Numbers from detect_rings.py — re-run it.)

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Before and after, real code, real scorecard. [left] The traditional approach judges each account alone: lots of games, win rate over 75%. Run it on our 40 colluders across 12 rings and it catches zero — because win-traders split their wins fifty-fifty, so every one of them looks like a perfectly average player.”

[right] “The relational version keeps only the suspiciously frequent matchups and takes the connected clusters. Same 40 accounts, same matches — 40 out of 40 caught, zero false members, eight rings surfaced, each handed over with its internal edges as the evidence.”

[strip] “The point isn’t that the old rule is badly tuned — it’s that it’s structurally blind. No threshold on a single account’s numbers can ever catch a 50%-win-rate colluder. The signal simply doesn’t live in any one account. That’s detect_rings.py in the kit.”

Depth slide — graph before/after. Drives home that per-account detection isn't under-tuned, it's structurally incapable — the strongest argument for the relational method.

22/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Rings — LIVE
▶ This is a live interactive demo in the full deck (drag / run / tap). Below is its surrounding content; open confidence-ladder-deck-v3.html on a laptop to run it.
Rung 1 · Detect · LIVE DEMO — build the matches, watch the ring ◆ SHARDFALL
SHARDFALL ladder — match interaction graph
Matches played0
Per-account rule (win-rate > 75%)0 / 12 caught
Graph view0 / 12 caught
Press play. Legit players get scattered opponents; the ring quietly farms each other.

Every ring member keeps a ~50% win rate, so the per-account rule sees nothing. The same matches, drawn as a graph, expose the dense knot instantly — the whole ring, not one account.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — with the live demo (verbatim + actions)

“Let’s play a season of SHARDFALL ranked and watch the matches pile up.” [click ‘play a season of matches’ — narrate over the animation] “Every line is a match. Legit players get spread across the whole field — random opponents, lots of different lines. But watch this corner down here… [point to the clustering nodes] these players keep facing the same handful of opponents, over and over. The lines between them are getting thick. A knot is forming.”

“Now — your per-account anti-cheat only has one number for these players: win rate. Let’s look at it.” [click ‘view: per-account’] “Every one of them sits around fifty percent. [point to the rule counter: 0/12] By the only number a per-account rule has, they are invisible. Zero caught. They’re textbook-average citizens.”

[click back to ‘graph view’] “But the moment we look at the relationships instead of the individuals — there’s the ring. The whole thing. [point to the cluster lighting up, 12/12] Twelve accounts, caught together, because the signal was never in any one of them. It was in the edges.”

“In production that clustering is a graph neural network on SageMaker AI over a graph in Neptune — but the structure it finds is exactly what you just watched form.”

LIVE DEMO 2 — the ring assembling before their eyes. Press play: edges animate in. Legit players (blue) get faint edges to random opponents across the field. The 12 colluders (will turn red) accumulate thick edges only among themselves — a visible tightening knot in one corner.

How to run it. (1) Play the season — narrate "normal matchmaking spreads out; watch this corner clump." (2) Toggle "view: per-account": every node shows a win-rate label, all ~50%, rule catches 0/12 — "by the only number a per-account rule has, they're invisible." (3) Toggle to "graph view": the dense cluster highlights, edges between ring members glow, 12/12 caught. The reveal is visual and instant.

Teaching point. The signal isn't in any node — it's in the edges. This is the step-change a GNN delivers; here it's transparent graph clustering so it runs live. Say: production = a GNN learning node embeddings (Amazon SageMaker AI) over a graph (Amazon Neptune); the structure it finds is exactly this.

23/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · today → result
Rung 1 · Detect · today → with this → expected result
Today

Fixed thresholds & per-account checks

“Ban if accuracy > 55% or APM > 175.” A bouncer with a list.

Falls short: evasive cheats dress just under the line; rings hide because each member looks average. 0/25 cheats, 0/12 ring.

→
With this

Anomaly + graph detection

Learn “normal,” flag the odd combination; map who-plays-whom, surface the knot.

The thing rules can’t do: judge the whole picture and the relationships, not one stat at a time. all cheats & rings surfaced.

→
Expected result

More caught, each with a reason

Higher recall on novel & evasive abuse, every flag carrying a “why” your analyst and appeals process can stand behind.

Bottom line: fewer cheats slipping through; faster, defensible review.

✦ in your genre — the same two techniques, different signal
✦ Star Oracle (gacha)anomaly on tap cadence & session rhythm catches auto-battle bot farms; graph on account-sharing finds the selling ring.
MOBAgraph on queue/draft partners exposes boosting duos; anomaly on APM & camera flags scripts.
Trading-cardgraph on trade partners surfaces value-funnelling to one “mule” account.
MMOanomaly on gather/route patterns flags gold-farm bots; graph on trades maps the farm→mule→buyer chain.

Honesty: this rung produces suspicions, not verdicts — it surfaces suspects for human review, never auto-bans on a score.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)
