“Let me put Rung one in one line. [point Today] Today you run fixed thresholds and per-account checks — a bouncer with a list. The evasive cheats dress just under the line, the ring members each look average, and you catch basically none of either. [point With this] With anomaly plus graph you judge the whole picture and the relationships — and you catch them, with a reason attached. [point Result] The result that matters to the business: more cheats caught, fewer slipping through, and every flag carries a ‘why’ your reviewers and your appeals process can stand behind.”

[gesture to the dashed purple box] “And this isn’t a SHARDFALL trick. Same two techniques, different signal: in a gacha game like Star Oracle, anomaly detection on tap rhythm catches auto-battle bot farms, and a graph of account-sharing finds the selling ring. In a MOBA it’s draft partners and APM. In an MMO it’s gather routes and the farm-to-buyer trade chain.”

“One honest caveat, and I’ll repeat it every time it applies:” [read the strip] “this rung produces suspicions, not verdicts. It surfaces suspects for a human to review. It never auto-bans on a score. Which raises a problem…” [teases the copilot slide]

Rung 1 comparison + genre breadth. The two live demos become a measurable business story, then the dashed box shows the techniques aren't SHARDFALL-specific.

Today. A reasonable rule, not a straw man. Real failures from the demos: 0/25 evasive, 0/12 ring.

With this. Anomaly judges the combination, graph judges the relationships — the one thing rules structurally can't.

Expected result. Framed as outcome: higher recall, a reason per flag, faster/defensible review. Not the algorithm.

Genre box (STAR ORACLE + MOBA/TCG/MMO). Same techniques, genre-specific signals: tap cadence, draft partners, trade graphs, gather routes. This is the breadth payoff — read two of the four aloud.

Honesty strip. detect → explain → human review → remedy; never auto-ban. Trading precision for recall is correct because it surfaces, not punishes.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Boosting
A skilled player (or a paid service) plays on/with someone's account to inflate their rank — the rank no longer reflects the real player's skill.
Mule account
A throwaway account used to funnel items or currency to one "real" account — common in trade-based fraud and gold-farming.
Account-sharing / selling
Logging into accounts that aren't yours (or trading/selling whole accounts) — against most games' rules and a sign of organized abuse.
Draft partners
In MOBAs, the teammates you're grouped with. Repeatedly drafting with the same partner can signal a boosting arrangement.
Recall (the metric)
Of all the real cheats out there, the fraction you actually catch. "Higher recall" = fewer cheats slip through. (Its partner, "precision," is how many of your flags are correct.)
False positive / false alarm
Flagging an innocent player as a cheat. The reason this rung feeds human review and never auto-bans.
24/67R1 · DetectRung 1 · Analyst copilot
Rung 1 · Detect · closing the loop: the analyst copilot ◆ SHARDFALL

A detection is useless until a human can act on it fast.

🧑‍⚖️🤖📋
Imagine…

Your trust-and-safety team drowns in flags — thousands a day, each just a number. Now imagine a sharp junior analyst who never tires: for every flag they read the player’s history, write a plain-English “here’s why this looks like win-trading,” attach the evidence, and sort the pile by severity. They don’t pass judgement — they make the human judge ten times faster.

1

It reads the messy evidence — the score, the feature that fired, recent matches and trades, the implicated ring.

2

It writes the case in plain language — “flagged: reaction times too consistent across 40 matches; only trades with 3 linked accounts.”

3

It triages and drafts — ranks by severity, drafts the case note, suggests next steps. The human decides.

The gist: a large language model (the tech behind chat assistants, e.g. on Amazon Bedrock) turns raw “score + features” into a reviewer-ready case file. It’s the force-multiplier for the human-review bottleneck every detector hits — and the natural generative-AI hook in the stack. It explains and accelerates; it never auto-bans. Not hypothetical: Ubisoft’s unified LLM moderation runs in production, surfacing ~50 actionable players per game per day and transferring labels across 7+ languages (KDD 2025).

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“…here’s the problem I just teased. If Rung one catches more — and it does — you’ve just handed your trust-and-safety team thousands of flags a day, each one just a number and a score. That doesn’t help; it just moves the bottleneck.”

“So picture a sharp junior analyst who never gets tired. For every single flag, they pull the player’s history, and they write it up in plain English — [read the example] ‘flagged because reaction times are too consistent across 40 matches, and this account only ever trades with three linked accounts.’ They attach the evidence, they sort the pile by severity. They don’t pass judgement — they make the human reviewer ten times faster.”

[read the gist] “That junior analyst is a large language model — the same kind of tech behind chat assistants, running on Amazon Bedrock. It turns ‘score plus features’ into a case file a person can act on. It’s the most natural generative-AI fit in this whole stack. And I’ll say it again: it explains and accelerates — it never auto-bans.”

“And that’s the ceiling of Rung one. Everything we’ve done so far is a ranked, explained suspicion that a human acts on. Useful — but it’s still a guess, and a guess with no honest error bar. So the very next question is: exactly how much should we trust it? That’s Rung two.”

Close of Rung 1. Answers "a model flagged 4,000 sessions… now what?" Higher recall (the two demos) would drown a review team without this. The most natural generative-AI / Amazon Bedrock hook in the talk. ~60s.

Headline. Detection volume without triage just moves the bottleneck; the copilot makes higher recall operationally affordable.

Analogy — tireless junior analyst. Bounds the claim: junior prepares, senior human decides. Keeps it on the right side of the honesty line (never auto-ban).

Mechanism. Reads evidence (incl. the ring from the graph demo) → writes plain-language case → triages + drafts; human decides. Avoid "prompt / context window / RAG / inference"; say "reads / writes / sorts."

Gist + transition. Name "LLM / generative AI" last; restate the boundary. Then transition to Rung 2 (Quantify): a flagged suspicion still has no honest error bar — the next rung puts a provable number on the confidence. → Rung 2.

25/67R2 · QuantifyRung 2 · Quantify, explained
Rung 2 · Quantify · putting an honest number on “how sure” ◆ SHARDFALL

A model says “0.83.” 0.83 what?

🌡️📋✅
Imagine…

A good weather service doesn’t just say “rain.” It says “70% chance — and over a whole season, it really does rain on about 70% of the days we say 70%.” That last part is calibration: the number means what it claims. A raw model score of 0.83 has no such promise — it’s a vibe, not a probability.

1

Hold out a clean “calibration” set of past sessions you’re confident about.

2

See how the detector scores them — that gives you the distribution of “normal” scores.

3

Turn any new score into a guarantee — “flagging this would wrongly hit at most 1% of clean players” — with no assumption about the model or the data shape.

4

If the guarantee isn’t tight enough, abstain — don’t act; send it to a human. Act automatically only when the math clears your bar.

The gist: conformal prediction wraps any detector and converts its raw score into a statement with a provable error rate — so “0.83” becomes “flag this and your false-ban rate stays ≤ 1%, guaranteed.” It’s the rung that makes “match your confidence to the stakes” literally computable. Generic, public method (Vovk, Gammerman & Shafer; Angelopoulos & Bates).

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Rung one handed us a flagged player with a score — 0.83. Here’s the uncomfortable question nobody asks: 0.83 what? It is not a probability. Two different models will both say 0.83 and mean wildly different things. You cannot run a ban wave on a vibe.”

“Think about a good weather forecast. It doesn’t just say ‘rain’ — it says ‘70% chance,’ and crucially, over a season it really does rain on about 70% of the ‘70%’ days. That property — the number meaning what it says — is called calibration, and your raw model score doesn’t have it.”

[walk the four steps] “Conformal prediction fixes that with almost embarrassingly little: hold out a clean set of sessions you trust; look at how the detector scores them; and then for any new player you can convert the score into a hard statement — ‘act on this and at most one percent of clean players get wrongly flagged’ — with no assumption about the model or the shape of your data. And if the guarantee isn’t tight enough, you abstain — you don’t act, you defer to a human.”

[gist] “That’s the whole rung: conformal prediction wraps any detector — the anomaly model, the graph model, anything — and turns ‘0.83’ into ‘≤1% false bans, guaranteed.’ It is the literal machinery of this talk’s thesis: now ‘match your confidence to the stakes’ is a number you can compute, not a slogan.”

Rung 2 (Quantify) — ELI5. The missing bridge between probabilistic detection and proof: calibrated, distribution-free confidence. The weather-forecast analogy nails 'calibration' for a lay audience. This rung is the most novel-for-a-games-audience addition and the one that most directly delivers the talk's 'confidence' title.

Why it belongs here. Rung 1 produces an uncalibrated score; before you climb to proof, you quantify how much to trust the suspicion. Conformal is model-agnostic — it wraps whatever detector you already built.

IP note. Conformal prediction is a standard, public academic method (Vovk/Gammerman/Shafer 2005; popularised by Angelopoulos & Bates' 'gentle introduction'). No proprietary IP. Credited on the appendix with links.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Calibration
A confidence number is "calibrated" if it means what it says — of all the times you say "90% sure," you're right about 90% of the time.
Conformal prediction
A wrapper around any model that turns its raw score into a statement with a guaranteed error rate, using only a clean calibration set — no assumptions about the model or data shape.
Calibration set
A held-out batch of past cases you're confident about, used to learn what "normal" scores look like so new scores can be given a guarantee.
Abstain / selective prediction
Letting the system say "not sure enough — send to a human" instead of being forced to decide. The principled way to match action to confidence.
Distribution-free
The guarantee holds without assuming your data follows any particular shape (no "bell curve" assumption) — it just needs the calibration data to resemble live data.
26/67R2 · QuantifyRung 2 · Quantify — how it works
Rung 2 · Quantify · how conformal prediction actually works — plain-first, formula beside

From a raw score to a guaranteed error rate.

the recipe — in plain English
  1. Score your calibration set of n known-clean sessions with the existing detector.
  2. Pick your risk level — say you’ll tolerate flagging 1% of clean players (α = 0.01).
  3. Find the cut-line: the score that only 1% of clean players exceed (a high quantile of the calibration scores).
  4. For a new session, flag only if its score beats that line — and you’ve provably kept clean-player flags at ≤ 1%. Report a p-value as the “how surprising” number.
the guarantee, for the engineerssplit conformal · one-sided
# calib scores s₁…sₙ on KNOWN-CLEAN sessions
q = Quantile( {s₁…sₙ}, ⌈(n+1)(1−α)⌉ / n )
flag(x)  ⇔  score(x) > q          # α = tolerated FP rate
p(x) = ( 1 + #{ sᵢ ≥ score(x) } ) / ( n + 1 )   # conformal p-value
# guarantee: P(clean player flagged) ≤ α  (distribution-free)
every symbol, decoded sᵢ detector score of clean calibration session i · n calibration size · α the false-positive rate you accept (e.g. 0.01) · q the cut-line (a quantile) · p(x) conformal p-value = how rarely a clean player scores this high.
why it’s a real guarantee, not a vibe The only assumption is exchangeability — your calibration sessions resemble live ones. No bell curve, no model trust. That’s why it wraps any detector (anomaly, graph, neural) unchanged.

Honest limits: the guarantee is only as clean as your calibration set — if cheats hide in it, or live data drifts from it, coverage slips (cure: refresh calibration, monitor drift). It bounds the error rate, not which individual is guilty. And a cheat adapting against the detector breaks exchangeability — pair with Rung-1 robustness.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Here’s the entire method, and it’s four lines. [left] Take a calibration set of sessions you’re confident are clean and score them all. Decide the false-positive rate you’ll tolerate — call it alpha, say one percent. Find the score that only that one percent of clean players exceed — that’s your cut-line. For any new player, flag only above the line.”

[code panel] “For the engineers: the cut-line is a specific quantile of the calibration scores — that ceiling formula with the (n+1) is what makes the guarantee exact for a finite sample, not just asymptotically. And you can report a conformal p-value — literally ‘how rarely does a clean player score at least this high.’”

[second formula box] “Why is this a real guarantee and not hand-waving? The only assumption is exchangeability — your calibration sessions look like your live ones. No bell-curve assumption, no trusting the model’s internals. That’s exactly why it wraps any detector you already have, untouched.”

[limits] “Be honest about the edges: the guarantee is only as clean as your calibration set — if cheats are hiding in it, or your live data drifts, coverage slips, so you refresh and monitor. It bounds the error rate, not which specific person is guilty. And a cheat actively adapting breaks exchangeability — which is why this sits on top of Rung 1, not instead of it.”

Rung 2 — mechanism. Split (inductive) conformal prediction: calibration scores → quantile cut-line at level α → flag above it → distribution-free FP guarantee, plus the conformal p-value. The (n+1) finite-sample correction and the exchangeability assumption are the two things that make it rigorous; both are stated.

The one idea that matters. Distribution-free + model-agnostic: it bounds the false-positive rate of ANY detector using only a clean calibration set. That's the whole pitch.

Limits = credibility. Calibration cleanliness, drift, and adversarial breaking of exchangeability. Say them; they're the honest boundary.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
α (alpha)
The error rate you choose to tolerate — e.g. α = 0.01 means "I accept wrongly flagging at most 1% of clean players."
Quantile
The value below which a given fraction of the data falls — the 99th-percentile score is the line only 1% of clean players exceed.
Conformal p-value
How surprising a new score is, measured against clean cases: small p = "a clean player almost never scores this high."
Exchangeability
The mild assumption that calibration and live sessions are drawn from the same pool (order doesn't matter) — weaker than the usual "independent and identically distributed."
Coverage
How often the guarantee actually holds in practice (target: ≥ 1−α). Drops if data drifts — so you monitor it.
27/67R2 · QuantifyRung 2 · Quantify — your data & I/O
Rung 2 · Quantify · the inputs, the outputs, and where to get them

It needs almost nothing new — just scores you already produce.

▼ INPUT — your existing detector’s scores + a clean calibration set
FieldWhat it meansTypeWhere it comes from
scoreany Rung-1 detector’s raw outputfloatthe anomaly / graph model you already run
calib_scores[]scores on a held-out clean setfloat[]past reviewed-clean sessions (or pre-incident data)
αtolerated false-positive rate0–1you set it — a policy/stakes decision
clean label (few)“confirmed not-cheating” for calibrationboolmanual review / trusted cohort
▲ OUTPUT — a decision with a guarantee attached  {score, p_value, decision: act | ABSTAIN→review, guaranteed_FP_rate ≤ α}

This is the cheapest rung to add and the highest-trust payoff. You don’t need new telemetry — you need the scores you already compute plus a clean calibration set. The output is the same flag, now carrying a number your legal, trust-&-safety, and appeals teams can stand behind: “our automated bans wrongly hit ≤ 1% of clean players, provably.”

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Here’s why this rung is almost free to adopt. [input table] It doesn’t want new telemetry. It wants the scores you already produce from Rung 1, a held-out set of sessions you’ve confirmed are clean — you probably have these from past manual reviews — and one policy number: the false-positive rate you’re willing to tolerate.”

[output line] “What comes out is the same flag you had before, but now wrapped with a p-value and a decision: act automatically, or abstain and send to a human — plus the guarantee that your automated actions wrongly hit at most alpha percent of clean players.”

[strip] “For the cost of a calibration set, you hand your legal and trust-and-safety teams the sentence they’ve always wanted: ‘our automated bans wrongly hit at most one percent of clean players, and we can prove it.’ That is a different conversation than ‘the model said 0.83.’”

Rung 2 — I/O & data sourcing. The buildability story: input is existing detector scores + a clean calibration set + a chosen α; output is the decision with a provable FP bound and an abstain path. The headline: cheapest rung to add (no new telemetry), highest trust payoff (a defensible number for legal/appeals).

Calibration data sourcing. Past manually-reviewed-clean sessions, or pre-incident "known good" windows. The realistic place studios get the clean labels.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Held-out set
Data set aside and not used to build the model, kept to honestly measure or calibrate it.
False-positive rate
The fraction of innocent players your system wrongly flags — the number conformal bounds and the one appeals teams care about most.
Abstain path
The route a case takes when confidence isn't high enough to act automatically — straight to a human reviewer.
28/67R2 · QuantifyRung 2 · Quantify — worked example
Rung 2 · Quantify · your scores → the formula → the guarantee — two players, one calibration set

Turn two raw scores into two honest decisions.

1
your data — Rung-1 anomaly scores on a clean calibration set (n = 1000)
# the 99th-percentile clean score (only 1% of clean players exceed it): α = 0.01 → cut-line q = Quantile(calib, 0.990) = 9.6
2
two new flagged players arrive from Rung 1
player A (the evasive cheat, p412): score = 18.3 player B (a fringe legit “smurf”): score = 9.9
3
compute the conformal p-value = (1 + #clean ≥ score) / (n+1)
A: only 2 of 1000 clean ≥ 18.3 → p = 3/1001 = 0.003 (far past the line) B: 11 of 1000 clean ≥ 9.9 → p = 12/1001 = 0.012 (just over)
4
decide against α = 0.01 — act only if p ≤ α, else ABSTAIN
A: p 0.003 ≤ 0.01 → ACT (auto-restrict), FP risk provably ≤1% B: p 0.012 > 0.01 → ABSTAIN → human review # not sure enough

🎯 Same two scores Rung 1 produced — now graded. The blatant case is auto-actioned with a provable ≤1% false-ban rate; the borderline case is held back for a human instead of risking a wrongful ban. That’s “match the strength of your evidence to the stakes,” computed.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — the bridge that makes conformal real (verbatim)

“Let me run it end to end on two players, so the guarantee stops being abstract.”

[step 1] “Calibration set of a thousand clean sessions. I want at most one percent false flags, so I find the score only the top one percent of clean players exceed — here it’s 9.6. That’s my line.”

[step 2] “Two flagged players land from Rung one. Player A is our evasive cheat from earlier, score 18.3. Player B is a borderline case — a skilled smurf maybe — score 9.9, just over the line.”

[step 3] “Compute the conformal p-value — how rarely a clean player scores this high. For A, basically never: p = 0.003. For B, it happens to about one in eighty clean players: p = 0.012.”

[step 4] “Now decide against my one-percent bar. A is way under — act automatically, and I can prove my false-ban rate stays at or under one percent. B is over the bar — so I abstain: no auto-action, send it to a human. Same two scores Rung one gave me, but now I’m acting on a guarantee, not a gut feeling. That borderline player I didn’t wrongly ban is the whole value.”

Rung 2 — WORKED EXAMPLE. Data→formula→decision bridge: calibration quantile (cut-line 9.6 at α=0.01), conformal p-values for a clear cheat (p=0.003→act) and a borderline case (p=0.012→abstain). Reuses p412 from the anomaly worked example for continuity. Demonstrates the abstain rule concretely — the borderline case saved from a wrongful ban is the punchline.

Arithmetic is exact. p=(1+k)/(n+1): A → (1+2)/1001=0.003; B → (1+11)/1001≈0.012. The accept/abstain split against α=0.01 is correct.

Continuity. p412 (score 18.3) is the same evasive cheat from the Rung-1 anomaly worked example — shows the rungs composing on one player.

29/67R2 · QuantifyRung 2 · Quantify — the methods menu
Rung 2 · Quantify · several ways to put a number on confidence · pick by need

Conformal isn’t the only way to be honest about uncertainty.

MethodThe ideaBest when…Watch out
Split conformal our demoquantile of clean scores → distribution-free FP guaranteeYou want a guarantee on any existing detector, cheaplyNeeds a clean, representative calibration set
Mondrian / class-conditional conformalseparate guarantee per group (region, platform, skill tier)Fairness — bound false-bans within each cohort, not just overallEach group needs enough calibration data
Conformal risk controlbound a chosen risk (not just error rate) at a targetAsymmetric costs (a wrongful ban ≫ a missed cheat)Pick the risk function carefully
Platt / isotonic calibrationsquash raw scores into calibrated probabilitiesYou just need believable probabilities, not a hard setNo finite-sample guarantee; assumes more
Bayesian posterior / credible intervalsmodel uncertainty as a distribution; report intervalsYou can model the process (e.g. skill rating — see ▶)Guarantee depends on the model being right

Same goal — an honest, actionable confidence — different machinery. Start with split conformal (wraps anything, guarantee with no model assumptions). Reach for Mondrian conformal when fairness across cohorts matters, conformal risk control when a wrongful ban costs far more than a miss. Bayesian methods shine where you can model the process — like matchmaking skill, which is its own worked example next.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“As always, conformal isn’t the only tool — here’s the menu, and they all chase the same thing: an honest, actionable number for confidence.”

[walk the rows] “Split conformal is the one to start with — it wraps anything and the guarantee needs no model assumptions. The moment fairness matters — you don’t want your false-ban rate to be one percent overall but five percent for one region — you switch to Mondrian conformal, which gives the guarantee per cohort. When a wrongful ban costs far more than a missed cheat, conformal risk control lets you bound that asymmetric risk directly. Platt and isotonic just squash scores into believable probabilities — easier, but no hard guarantee. And Bayesian methods give you full uncertainty when you can actually model the process.”

[strip] “That last one — Bayesian uncertainty — has a beautiful games home: skill rating for fair matchmaking. Let me make that the worked example for the Bayesian branch on the next slide.”

Rung 2 — methods menu. Five ways to quantify uncertainty: split conformal (default), Mondrian/class-conditional (fairness across cohorts), conformal risk control (asymmetric costs), Platt/isotonic (calibrated probs, no guarantee), Bayesian (model-based intervals). Selection axis: do you need a hard guarantee, fairness-per-group, or a modelled process? All public methods.

Fairness hook. Mondrian conformal = bound false-bans within each cohort — pre-empts the "your detector is biased against region X" critique with a method, not a promise.

Bridge to Bayesian skill. The Bayesian row tees up the next slide (skill rating for fair matchmaking) — a second, very games-native worked example for this rung.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Mondrian / class-conditional
Running the conformal guarantee separately within each group (region, platform, skill tier) so fairness holds per-cohort, not just on average.
Conformal risk control
Extends conformal from "error rate" to any cost you define — useful when a wrongful ban is far more costly than a missed cheat.
Platt / isotonic calibration
Simple recipes that reshape raw scores into believable probabilities — cheaper than conformal but without the finite-sample guarantee.
Credible interval (Bayesian)
A range that contains the true value with stated probability, given your model — e.g. "skill is 1500 ± 80."
30/67R2 · QuantifyRung 2 · Quantify — fair matchmaking
Rung 2 · Quantify · a second face: uncertainty = fair matchmaking ◆ SHARDFALL

The same idea, applied to who you match.

● BEFORE — Elo (a single number)traditional
# one point estimate, no uncertainty
R_winner += K · (1 − expected)
R_loser  −= K · (expected)
# a brand-new player and a 500-game
# veteran on 1500 look identical.
the problem can’t tell a confident 1500 from an unsure 1500
new players mismatched; built for 1-v-1, not teams
● AFTER — skill as a distributionrecommended
# track mean μ AND uncertainty σ
skill ~ Normal(μ, σ²)
match to minimise P(draw-mismatch);
σ shrinks as evidence accrues
# wide σ for newbies → learn fast,
# then stabilise. Handles teams/FFA.
the payoff fair matches + a confidence on every rating
don’t act on a rating until σ is small enough

Same rung-2 principle — carry the uncertainty, act only when it’s small enough — pointed at fairness instead of bans. Public methods: Glicko/Glicko-2 (open), OpenSkill (open-source); the Bayesian factor-graph approach is well documented. (Proprietary systems like TrueSkill exist too — we use the open methods.)

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Conformal was Rung two pointed at bans. Let me show the exact same principle — carry your uncertainty, act only when it’s small enough — pointed at matchmaking fairness, because it’s the most relatable version.”

[left] “Old way: Elo. One number. The problem is a brand-new player and a grizzled five-hundred-game veteran can both sit on 1500, and Elo treats them identically — so it mismatches newcomers, and it was built for one-versus-one, not teams.”

[right] “Better way: track skill as a distribution — a mean and an uncertainty. New players start with wide uncertainty so the system learns fast, then narrows as evidence accrues. You match to minimise mismatch, and — here’s the rung-two move — you don’t fully trust a rating until its uncertainty is small. Same idea as ‘abstain until you’re sure enough,’ just for placement instead of punishment.”

[strip] “And to keep us clean on IP: the open methods here are Glicko and OpenSkill; the Bayesian factor-graph approach is well documented. Proprietary systems exist, but everything we teach uses the open ones.”

Rung 2 — second worked face + before/after. Bayesian skill rating as the games-native instance of "carry uncertainty, act when confident." Elo (point estimate) vs distribution (μ, σ): new-player handling, team support, and the rung-2 move of not trusting a rating until σ is small. Doubles as the rung's before/after.

IP cleanliness. Lead with Glicko/Glicko-2 and OpenSkill (open); name TrueSkill only as "a proprietary system exists" — not the taught method. Bayesian inference itself is public domain.

Ties the rung together. Reinforces that Rung 2 is one principle (quantified, actionable uncertainty) with two faces: conformal (bans) and Bayesian (matchmaking).

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Elo
The classic single-number skill rating from chess — simple, but it carries no sense of how sure the number is, and assumes 1-v-1.
Skill as a distribution (μ, σ)
Track both a player's estimated skill (μ) and how uncertain that estimate is (σ); σ starts wide for newcomers and shrinks with games played.
Glicko / OpenSkill
Open, public skill-rating systems that carry uncertainty and handle teams/multiplayer — the methods we teach here.
Factor graph
A way to compute the Bayesian skill update efficiently across all players in a match; the documented approach behind modern team rating.
31/67R3 · AttributeRung 3 · Attribute, explained
Rung 3 · Attribute · did your fix actually work — or did you get lucky? ◆ SHARDFALL

You shipped the fix. The metric moved. Did the fix do it?

💊🪞📈
Imagine…

A new vitamin launches Monday; by Friday sales are up 20%. Did the vitamin work — or did a heatwave, payday, and a competitor’s stock-out do it? The only honest answer compares to a “what would have happened anyway” — a control group, or a carefully built stand-in for one. A dashboard going up is not proof your change caused it.

1

Name the intervention & the outcome. e.g. “the ban wave” → “cheater-reports per match.”

2

Build the counterfactual — what the metric would have done without the change (a control region, or a synthetic clone of the treated one).

3

The causal effect = actual − counterfactual, not before − after. Subtract out the seasonality and trends that fool dashboards.

4

Report the effect with uncertainty — and, with uplift, who the change actually moved.

The gist: causal inference measures the true effect of a ban wave, a balance patch, or a store promo by comparing reality to a built “what-would-have-happened.” It’s how you stop trusting the dashboard and start proving the cause — and how you tell “this player cheats” apart from “this player is just good / laggy / new.” Standard public methods: difference-in-differences, synthetic control, CUPED, uplift.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“We opened this whole talk with a dashboard that said ‘all green’ while the players screamed ‘rigged.’ Rung three closes that loop from the other side: you ship a fix, the metric moves — and the trap is believing your fix caused it.”

“A vitamin launches Monday, sales are up Friday. Did the vitamin work, or was it a heatwave, payday, and a competitor running out of stock? You genuinely cannot tell from the sales line alone. You need a ‘what would have happened anyway’ — a control, or a built stand-in for one. A dashboard going up is not proof your change caused it.”

[walk the steps] “So: name the intervention and the outcome — the ban wave, and cheater-reports per match. Build the counterfactual — a control region you didn’t ban in, or a synthetic clone of the one you did. The causal effect is actual minus counterfactual, not before minus after — that subtraction is what removes the seasonality that fools dashboards. And report it with uncertainty, plus — using uplift — which players actually changed.”

[gist] “That’s causal inference: the true effect of a ban wave, a patch, a promo. It also tells ‘this player is cheating’ apart from ‘this player is just good, or laggy, or brand new’ — the confounds that make innocent players look guilty. Stop trusting the dashboard; start proving the cause.”

Rung 3 (Attribute) — ELI5. Causal inference: measure the TRUE effect of an intervention vs a counterfactual, not before-vs-after. The vitamin analogy nails confounding for a lay audience. Bookends slide 3's "dashboard vs reality" — now from the 'did our fix work?' side. Also frames confound-removal (skill/ping/newness vs cheating).

Why it belongs on the ladder. Detection (1) + confidence (2) tell you WHO looks abusive; Attribute tells you whether your RESPONSE actually worked, and separates cheating from confounds. It's evidential honesty about your own analytics — squarely on-theme.

IP note. All methods are public/academic: difference-in-differences (econometrics, decades old), synthetic control (Abadie et al.), CUPED (Deng/Kohavi, Microsoft — published), uplift/causal forests (Wager & Athey, open GRF). Credited with links on the appendix.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Causal effect
How much a change actually caused an outcome to differ — versus mere correlation, which can be coincidence or a shared cause.
Counterfactual
"What would have happened anyway" without your change — the thing you compare reality against to isolate the true effect.
Confounder
A hidden factor that moves both things at once (a heatwave lifts sales; high skill raises both accuracy and suspicion) and fakes a cause.
Control group
A comparable set you deliberately didn't apply the change to, so it shows the counterfactual.
Uplift
The effect of a change per individual — separating those it actually moved from those who'd have done the same regardless.
32/67R3 · AttributeRung 3 · Attribute — how it works
Rung 3 · Attribute · how causal inference actually works — plain-first, formula beside

Subtract the world that would have happened anyway.

the recipe — difference-in-differences, in plain English
  1. Two groups: one you changed (treated), one you didn’t (control).
  2. Two times: before and after the change.
  3. The control’s before→after shift = everything that moved for reasons other than your change (season, meta, economy).
  4. Subtract that shift from the treated group’s before→after. What’s left is your change’s true effect.
the estimators, for the engineersDiD · synthetic control · CUPED
# Difference-in-Differences:
ATT = (Y_treat,after − Y_treat,before)
    − (Y_ctrl,after  − Y_ctrl,before)

# Synthetic control (no clean control exists):
Y_synth = Σ wᵢ · Y_controlᵢ ,  Σwᵢ=1, wᵢ≥0
effect  = Y_treat − Y_synth      # weights fit on pre-period

# CUPED (cut A/B variance with a pre-covariate X):
Y* = Y − θ·(X − E[X]) ,  θ = Cov(Y,X)/Var(X)
every symbol, decoded ATT avg effect on the treated · Y the outcome metric · wᵢ weights blending control units into a synthetic twin · θ CUPED coefficient · X a pre-experiment covariate correlated with Y (e.g. last week’s spend).
the assumption that makes it causal DiD needs parallel trends: absent your change, treated & control would have moved together. Check it on the pre-period. Synthetic control relaxes this by building a matching twin from many controls.

Honest limits: a hidden confounder that hits one group only still fools you (no method invents a missing control); parallel-trends can fail; and these estimate an average effect — use uplift / causal forests for per-player effects. When you can randomise (a true A/B), do — it’s the gold standard; these are for when you can’t.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“The cleanest method is difference-in-differences, and the name tells you the trick. [left] Two groups — one you changed, one you didn’t. Two times — before and after. The control group’s before-to-after change captures everything that moved for reasons other than you: the season, the meta, the economy. Subtract that from the treated group’s change, and what survives is your effect.”

[code panel] “For the engineers: that’s the ATT line — a difference of two differences. When you have no clean control — say you patched globally — synthetic control builds a stand-in by weighting many comparison units to match your treated group’s history before the change. And CUPED is the one every experimentation team should know: it uses a pre-period covariate, like last week’s spend, to strip variance out of an A/B test so you reach significance with far less data or time.”

[assumption box] “The assumption that makes difference-in-differences causal is parallel trends: absent your change, the two groups would have moved together. You check that on the pre-period — if they were already diverging, the method lies. Synthetic control relaxes it by constructing the twin.”

[limits] “Honest limits: a confounder that hits only one group still fools you — no method conjures a control that isn’t there. These give an average effect; for per-player effects you use uplift or causal forests. And if you can randomise a real A/B test, do — that’s the gold standard. These are the rigorous tools for when you can’t.”

Rung 3 — mechanism. DiD (difference of two before/after differences), synthetic control (weighted twin when no clean control), CUPED (variance reduction for A/B). Parallel-trends is the key causal assumption; uplift/causal forests for heterogeneous effects; randomised A/B is the gold standard when feasible. All standard estimators.

The teaching beat. "Causal effect = actual − counterfactual, not before − after." The DiD double-subtraction removing seasonality is the intuition to land.

Limits = credibility. Unobserved confounders, parallel-trends failure, average-vs-individual effects. And the honest "randomise if you can."

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Difference-in-differences (DiD)
Compare the before→after change in a group you changed to the before→after change in one you didn't; the gap is the effect, with shared trends cancelled out.
Synthetic control
When no single clean control exists, build one by blending many comparison units so their combined history matches the treated group before the change.
CUPED
"Controlled-experiment Using Pre-Experiment Data" — uses a pre-period number correlated with your metric to cut A/B-test noise, reaching a verdict faster.
Parallel trends
The assumption that, without your change, treated and control groups would have moved in lockstep — checkable on pre-change data.
ATT
"Average Treatment effect on the Treated" — the mean effect of the change on the group that received it.
33/67R3 · AttributeRung 3 · Attribute — your data & I/O
Rung 3 · Attribute · the inputs, the outputs, and where to get them

It runs on the metrics & logs you already chart.

▼ INPUT — a metric over time, split by treated vs control
FieldWhat it meansTypeWhere it comes from
unit_idregion / cohort / player (the thing measured)idyour existing segmentation
periodday/week bucket, pre & post the changedateany event timestamp
treateddid this unit get the change?boolyour rollout / ban-wave log
outcomethe metric you care aboutfloatreports/match, retention, ARPU…
covariates (opt)pre-period traits (for CUPED / matching)float[]prior spend, skill, region
▲ OUTPUT — an effect you can defend  {effect_size, confidence_interval, p_value, per-segment uplift}

No new instrumentation — this is a discipline, not a data project. You already log rollouts and chart metrics; causal inference just compares them correctly. The output replaces “reports dropped 30% after the ban wave” with “the ban wave caused a 22% drop, 95% CI [16–28%], net of seasonality” — a sentence you can put in front of leadership.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Like the last rung, the beautiful part is how little new plumbing this needs. [input table] It runs on a metric over time, split by who got the change and who didn’t — region or cohort, a date bucket, a treated flag from your rollout log, and the outcome you already chart. Optionally some pre-period covariates to sharpen it.”

[output line] “Out comes an effect size with a confidence interval and, if you want, the uplift per segment.”

[strip] “This is a discipline, not a data project — you already have all of this. It just turns ‘reports dropped thirty percent after the ban wave’ — which might be seasonal — into ‘the ban wave caused a twenty-two percent drop, plus-or-minus six, net of seasonality.’ One of those sentences survives a hard question from leadership. The other doesn’t.”

Rung 3 — I/O & data sourcing. Input is a metric-over-time split treated/control (+ optional covariates) — all from existing rollout logs and dashboards. Output is a defensible effect size with CI and per-segment uplift. Headline: it's a discipline applied to data you already have, turning correlational dashboard claims into causal ones.

The reframing line. "reports dropped 30%" → "the ban wave caused a 22% drop, 95% CI [16–28%], net of seasonality." That contrast is the whole value.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Treated vs control
The units that got your change versus comparable ones that didn't — the split every causal method needs.
Confidence interval
The range the true effect plausibly lies in (e.g. "22%, between 16% and 28%") — honesty about precision, not a single point.
Covariate
A background trait measured before the change (prior spend, skill) used to sharpen the estimate or match groups.
ARPU
"Average Revenue Per User" — a common monetization outcome you might measure the causal effect on.
34/67R3 · AttributeRung 3 · Attribute — worked example
Rung 3 · Attribute · your metric → the formula → the true effect — did the ban wave work?

The dashboard says −30%. The truth is −22%.

1
your data — cheater-reports per 1000 matches, before & after the ban wave
region before after NA (banned) 40 28 # −30% — the headline EU (not banned) 38 35 # drifted down anyway
2
the naive (wrong) read — before vs after on the treated region
NA: (28 − 40) / 40 = −30% # tempting to claim the ban did all of this
3
difference-in-differences — subtract what happened anyway (EU)
NA change: 28 − 40 = −12 EU change: 35 − 38 = −3 # season/meta, no ban ATT = −12 − (−3) = −9 reports/1000 (on a base of 40)
4
the honest effect, with uncertainty
true causal effect = −9/40 = −22% (95% CI ≈ [−28%, −16%]) # the other −8 points were happening regardless of the ban

✅ The ban wave really worked — but by 22%, not 30%. A third of the “win” was seasonal drift you’d have gotten for free. Now you can size the next ban wave on the real effect, and defend the number when finance asks. That’s proving the cause, not trusting the chart.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — the bridge that makes causal real (verbatim)

“Let me run an actual ban wave through it. [step 1] Cheater-reports per thousand matches. In North America, where we ran the ban wave, they fell from 40 to 28. In Europe, where we didn’t, they drifted from 38 to 35 on their own.”

[step 2] “The naive read — and the one that ends up in the celebratory Slack message — is North America’s 40 to 28: down thirty percent, ban wave’s a hero.”

[step 3] “But Europe shows reports were drifting down anyway — minus three, with no ban. Difference-in-differences subtracts that: North America fell twelve, Europe fell three for free, so the ban’s real contribution is minus nine on a base of forty.”

[step 4] “That’s a twenty-two percent causal effect, not thirty — with a confidence interval, because we’re being honest. A third of the apparent win was seasonal drift. [verdict] The ban wave genuinely worked — and now you can size the next one on the real number and defend it when finance asks why you spent the analyst hours. That’s the difference between proving the cause and trusting the chart.”

Rung 3 — WORKED EXAMPLE. A ban-wave DiD: NA (treated) 40→28 vs EU (control) 38→35. Naive −30% vs true ATT −9/40 = −22% with CI. The "a third of the win was seasonal" punchline is the memorable, defensible takeaway. Arithmetic exact: NA −12, EU −3, ATT −9, −9/40=−22.5%≈−22%.

Why these numbers. Chosen so the naive vs causal gap is large and clean (30% vs 22%), illustrating confounding by a shared downward trend. Verifiable on screen.

35/67R3 · AttributeRung 3 · Attribute — the methods menu
Rung 3 · Attribute · several ways to prove a cause · pick by what you can run

Match the method to how much control you have.

MethodThe ideaBest when…Watch out
Randomised A/B gold standardrandomly assign the change; compareYou can randomise (store offer, matchmaking tweak)Not always ethical/possible (global patch, ban policy)
CUPED (on top of A/B)strip pre-period variance to decide fasterAny A/B — cuts sample/time 20–50%Needs a pre-covariate correlated with the metric
Difference-in-differences our demotreated vs control, before vs afterA clean control group exists (a region you didn’t touch)Relies on parallel trends — verify on pre-period
Synthetic controlbuild a weighted “twin” from many controlsYou changed everything at once (global patch)Needs a good donor pool & stable pre-period
Uplift / causal forestsper-player effect, not just the averageTargeting: who does the offer/intervention actually move?Data-hungry; validate with care

The ladder-within-the-rung is “how much control do you have?” Can you randomise → A/B (+CUPED to go faster). Can’t, but have a clean control → diff-in-diff. Changed everything at once → synthetic control. Need to target individuals → uplift. Every one beats “the dashboard went up.” All public methods — Abadie (synthetic control), Deng & Kohavi (CUPED), Wager & Athey (causal forests).

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“The menu here sorts by one question: how much control do you have over the rollout?”

[walk the rows] “If you can randomise — a store offer, a matchmaking tweak — do a real A/B test; that’s the gold standard, and bolt CUPED on top to reach your answer with twenty to fifty percent less data. If you can’t randomise but you held out a clean control — a region you didn’t ban in — that’s difference-in-differences, our demo. If you changed everything at once, like a global patch, synthetic control builds you a twin from the data that came before. And when you need to know who a change moves, not just the average — for targeting offers without preying on whales — that’s uplift modelling.”

[strip] “Every single one of these beats ‘the dashboard went up after we shipped.’ And they’re all standard public methods — synthetic control from Abadie, CUPED from the Microsoft experimentation team, causal forests from Wager and Athey.”

Rung 3 — methods menu. Sorted by degree of experimental control: randomised A/B (gold standard) + CUPED, DiD (clean control), synthetic control (global change), uplift/causal forests (per-individual). All public/academic. The selection axis "how much control do you have" is the practitioner's decision rule.

Honest hierarchy. Lead by saying randomised A/B is best when feasible — it earns trust that the quasi-experimental methods are the for-when-you-can't tools, not a dodge.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Randomised A/B test
Randomly give some users the change and others not, then compare — randomisation removes confounders by design. The gold standard.
Donor pool
The set of untreated units synthetic control blends together to build its "twin" of the treated group.
Causal forest
A machine-learning method (random-forest-based) that estimates the effect of a change separately for each individual.
Quasi-experiment
Estimating a causal effect when you couldn't randomise, by finding or constructing a valid comparison (DiD, synthetic control).
36/67R4 · Stress-testRung 4 · Stress-test, explained
Rung 4 · Stress-test · break it yourself, before a million players do ◆ SHARDFALL

Don’t wait for players to find the broken build. Send robots first.

🤖🧗💥
Imagine…

Before a bank ships an app, a red team of hackers tries to break in — on purpose, in private. A crash-test dummy hits the wall so a person never does. Rung four is that for your game: armies of self-learning agents play your title millions of times, hunting the dominant build, the infinite-combo, the economy loophole — so the machine finds it in a sandbox, not a streamer on launch night.

1

Point reward-seeking agents at your game — reward them for winning, and for finding weird/degenerate ways to win.

2

Let them play millions of matches via self-play — far past what human QA can cover.

3

They surface the exploits — the broken build, the map skip, the money loop — with the exact sequence to reproduce.

4

For the economy, simulate a whole population of buyers/sellers/farmers and watch for inflation, hoarding, and arbitrage before launch.

The gist: reinforcement-learning self-play red-teams your design, and agent-based simulation red-teams your economy — both empirically breaking the game in a sandbox so you fix it pre-launch. It’s the experimental cousin of Rung 5’s proof: where you can’t (yet) prove soundness, you can try very hard to break it at superhuman scale. Standard public methods; EA SEED, DeepMind, NetEase Fuxi are illustrative.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Rungs one through three watch the live game. Rung four flips the timeline: break it before players ever touch it.”

“Before a bank ships an app, a red team of friendly hackers attacks it in private. A crash-test dummy hits the wall so a person doesn’t. Rung four is that for your game — you send robots first. Armies of self-learning agents play your title millions of times, deliberately hunting the dominant build, the infinite combo, the economy loophole, so the machine finds it in a sandbox instead of a streamer finding it on launch night and torching your economy by Tuesday.”

[walk the steps] “You point reward-seeking agents at the game and reward them not just for winning but for winning in weird ways. You let them self-play far past anything human QA could cover. They hand you the exploits with the exact reproduction steps. And for the economy specifically, you simulate a whole population — buyers, sellers, farmers, whales — and watch for inflation or arbitrage before a single real player is exposed.”

[gist] “Reinforcement-learning self-play red-teams your design; agent-based simulation red-teams your economy. Think of it as the experimental cousin of the proof rung that’s coming next: where you can’t yet prove the game is sound, you can try extraordinarily hard to break it, at superhuman scale.”

Rung 4 (Stress-test) — ELI5. RL self-play for design exploit discovery + agent-based simulation for economy stress-testing. The red-team / crash-test-dummy analogy frames it. Positioned as the empirical, pre-launch cousin of Rung 5's formal proof — "break it if you can't yet prove it."

Why it belongs here. It's the bridge from live-game statistical rungs (1–3) to pre-launch proof (5): empirical pre-launch breaking. Sits just below formal proof because it's "try hard to break" not "prove can't break."

IP note. RL, self-play, and agent-based modelling are public/academic. EA SEED (CoG 2020), DeepMind self-play, NetEase Fuxi (KDD 2025), DeepMind×CCP/EVE are named only as illustrative real-world examples — not methods we claim. Credited with links.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Reinforcement learning (RL)
Training an agent by reward and trial-and-error — it learns which actions win, including unintended ones, by playing a lot.
Self-play
Agents improve by repeatedly playing against copies of themselves, escalating skill with no human opponents needed.
Red-teaming
Deliberately attacking your own system to find weaknesses before adversaries do — borrowed from security.
Agent-based simulation
Modelling many individual "agent" players with simple rules and watching the whole economy/behaviour emerge from their interactions.
Emergent behaviour
Large-scale patterns (inflation, market manipulation) that arise from many small interactions — invisible in a spreadsheet, visible in simulation.
37/67R4 · Stress-testRung 4 · Stress-test — how it works
Rung 4 · Stress-test · how RL self-play & economy sim actually work — plain-first, formula beside

Reward the agent for breaking your game.

the recipe — in plain English
  1. Wrap your game as an environment — state in, action out, reward back.
  2. Shape the reward to seek exploits — reward winning, plus a bonus for unusual states (high gold, novel positions) — “curiosity.”
  3. Train by self-play — the agent plays itself, both sides improving, exploring strategies humans never try.
  4. Harvest the degenerate policies — whatever the agent converges on that you didn’t intend is an exploit, with a replay.
the objective, for the engineerspolicy gradient + curiosity
# RL agent maximises expected discounted reward:
maximise  𝔼[ Σ γᵗ · rₜ ]
rₜ = win_reward + β · novelty(stateₜ)   # exploit-seeking

# Agent-based economy model — emergent macro:
for agent in population:        # buyers, farmers, whales
    action = policy(prices, inventory, goals)
prices ← market_clear(all_actions)   # watch CPI, Gini
every symbol, decoded γ discount (future vs now) · rₜ reward at step t · β curiosity weight (how hard to chase the weird) · novelty() bonus for rarely-seen states · market_clear() sets prices from everyone’s supply & demand each tick.
why this finds what QA can’t Human testers try the intended strategies. A reward-seeking agent is indifferent to your intentions — it optimises, full stop, so it walks straight into the degenerate corner of the strategy space where exploits live.

Honest limits: needs a runnable simulation/headless build (engineering cost); reward-shaping is fiddly (a mis-shaped reward finds boring exploits); agents can find “exploits” that are artifacts of the sim, not the real game; and it’s evidence by search, not proof — absence of a found exploit ≠ none exists (that’s Rung 5). Compute-hungry at scale.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Two engines here. [left] For design: wrap your game so an agent can act on it — state in, action out, reward back. Then the key move — shape the reward to seek exploits: reward winning, but add a bonus for landing in unusual states, like absurd gold totals or weird map positions. That ‘curiosity’ term is what pushes it off the beaten path. Train by self-play, millions of games, and harvest whatever degenerate strategy it converges on — that’s your exploit, with a replay attached.”

[code panel] “For the engineers, the agent maximises expected discounted reward, with that novelty bonus weighted by beta. And the economy version is an agent-based model: every simulated buyer, farmer, and whale acts on prices and inventory, the market clears each tick, and you watch macro indicators — a price index, a wealth-inequality Gini — for runaway inflation or hoarding.”

[why it works] “Why does this catch what QA can’t? Your human testers try the strategies you intended. A reward-seeking agent is completely indifferent to your intentions — it just optimises — so it walks straight into the degenerate corner of the strategy space where the exploits actually live.”

[limits] “Limits, honestly: you need a runnable headless build — real engineering. Reward-shaping is fiddly; shape it wrong and it finds boring exploits. Sometimes it finds an ‘exploit’ that’s an artifact of your simulation, not the real game. And the big one: this is evidence by search — not finding an exploit doesn’t prove there isn’t one. That’s exactly what the next rung, formal proof, is for.”

Rung 4 — mechanism. RL self-play (policy gradient + curiosity/novelty reward to seek exploits) and agent-based economy modelling (population of heterogeneous agents → market clearing → emergent macro metrics like CPI/Gini). The "agent is indifferent to your intentions" point is why it beats human QA. All standard methods.

The key contrast with Rung 5. Stress-test = evidence by search (found-it or didn't-find-it); Verify-rules = proof (can't-exist). Say it explicitly — it's why Stress-test sits just below the proof rung.

Limits = credibility. Needs a sim/headless build, reward-shaping is hard, sim-vs-real artifacts, and search≠proof. Compute cost.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Environment (RL)
A runnable version of your game the agent can act on — it sends an action, gets back the new state and a reward.
Reward shaping
Designing the reward signal so the agent pursues what you want — here, a bonus for "unusual" states to push it toward exploits.
Curiosity / novelty bonus
Extra reward for visiting rarely-seen states, which drives the agent to explore the weird corners where exploits hide.
Policy
The agent's learned strategy — a mapping from situation to action.
Market clearing
Setting prices each step so total supply meets total demand — how the economy sim turns individual actions into prices.
Gini coefficient
A 0–1 measure of inequality (here, wealth concentration among simulated players) — a red flag for a pay-to-win or hoarding spiral.
38/67R4 · Stress-testRung 4 · Stress-test — your data & I/O
Rung 4 · Stress-test · the inputs, the outputs, and where to get them

It needs a sandbox, not your players.

▼ INPUT — a runnable model of your game
You provideWhat it isTypeWhere it comes from
Headless build / envthe game runnable without graphics, fastsim APIyour engine (Unreal/Unity sim mode)
Action & state spacewhat an agent can see & dospecgameplay code
Reward signalwin + novelty/exploit bonusfnyou design it (the key knob)
Economy rules + agent profilesshop/craft rules; player archetypesconfigconfig + clusters from real logs
▲ OUTPUT — exploits & economy failures, pre-launch, with replays  {dominant_strategies[], exploit_replays[], econ_metrics: inflation/Gini/arbitrage}

The one real cost on the whole ladder: you need a runnable sandbox. But you likely already have a headless/sim build for CI and bots. Feed it a reward and agent profiles (cluster them from your real telemetry) and you get exploits + economy failures before launch — with replays your designers can act on. Player archetypes come from the same behavioural data Rung 1 already uses.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“I’ll be straight: this is the one rung that needs real new infrastructure. [input table] It wants a headless build — your game runnable fast, without graphics — plus the action and state space, a reward signal you design, and for the economy version, your shop rules and a set of player archetypes.”

[where it comes from] “But here’s the good news: you very likely already have a headless build for CI and for your in-game bots. And the player archetypes? You cluster them from the exact same behavioural telemetry Rung one already consumes. So you’re reusing two things you have.”

[output + strip] “Out comes the dominant strategies, exploit replays your designers can immediately reproduce, and economy health metrics — inflation, inequality, arbitrage paths — all before launch. The cost is real, but it buys you the one thing you can never get back: finding the game-breaker before a million players do.”

Rung 4 — I/O & data sourcing. Input is a headless/sim build + action/state space + a designed reward + economy config & agent profiles. Output is dominant strategies, exploit replays, and economy-health metrics — pre-launch. Honest: this is the one rung with a genuine new-infrastructure cost, mitigated by reuse (CI headless build, archetypes clustered from Rung-1 telemetry).

Honesty about cost. Don't pretend it's free like rungs 2–3. The mitigation is real reuse (headless build often exists; archetypes from existing telemetry).

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Headless build
A version of the game that runs without rendering graphics, so it can play thousands of matches fast on servers — what agents train against.
Action / state space
The full set of things an agent can do (actions) and observe (states) — the interface between the agent and the game.
Player archetype
A representative player type (casual, grinder, whale, farmer) — built by clustering real telemetry — used to populate the economy simulation.
Replay
The exact recorded sequence of actions reproducing an exploit, so designers can see and fix it.
39/67R4 · Stress-testRung 4 · Stress-test — worked example
Rung 4 · Stress-test · sandbox → agents → the exploit, pre-launch — an economy meltdown caught in sim

10,000 simulated players find what QA didn’t.

1
your data — the SHARDFALL economy + 10k agents clustered from real telemetry
# archetypes & mix, from Rung-1 behavioural clusters casual 70% · grinder 22% · whale 5% · arbitrageur 3% reward = net worth growth + β·novelty
2
run 2,000 simulated market-days of self-play before launch
watch macro: price index (CPI), wealth Gini, currency supply day 1–600: stable · day ~640: CPI starts climbing
3
the agents discover a loop QA never tried — and the replay shows it
arbitrage found: craft_gem ↔ marketplace price gap 3% of agents pour in → gem supply ×8 → CPI +140%, Gini 0.71 # the 2-shard recipe again — but found by emergent market pressure
4
fix the recipe, re-run — economy stays stable for all 2,000 days
craft 3-shard: CPI flat ±4%, Gini 0.38 ✓ stable shipped a sound economy — meltdown never reached players

🧪 The meltdown happened — in a sandbox, on simulated day 640, not on your launch weekend. Self-play agents, indifferent to your intentions, found the arbitrage and the replay showed exactly how. Note the handoff: stress-test found it empirically; Rung 5 (next) can prove the fix leaves no such loop at all.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — the bridge that makes stress-test real (verbatim)

“Let me run the SHARDFALL economy through it. [step 1] Ten thousand simulated players, and crucially their mix comes from real telemetry clusters — mostly casuals, some grinders, a few whales, and three percent arbitrageurs. Reward them for growing net worth, plus a curiosity bonus.”

[step 2] “Run two thousand simulated market-days before launch and watch the macro indicators — a consumer price index, a wealth-inequality Gini. Stable for six hundred days… then around day 640, the price index starts climbing.”

[step 3] “The agents have discovered, on their own, an arbitrage loop between crafting and the marketplace — it’s our two-shard recipe again, but this time found by emergent market pressure, not by us. Three percent of agents pile in, gem supply explodes eightfold, prices spike a hundred and forty percent, inequality hits 0.71. The replay shows the exact loop.”

[step 4] “Fix the recipe, re-run: flat prices, healthy Gini, stable for all two thousand days. [verdict] The meltdown happened — on simulated day 640, in a sandbox, not on your launch weekend. And notice the beautiful handoff: stress-test found it by searching; the next rung, formal proof, can prove the fix leaves no such loop anywhere at all.”

Rung 4 — WORKED EXAMPLE. Agent-based economy sim on SHARDFALL: 10k archetype-mixed agents, 2000 sim-days, emergent arbitrage discovered ~day 640 (CPI +140%, Gini 0.71), fix → stable. Deliberately ties back to the same 2-shard craft exploit from Rung 5's worked example — but found empirically by market pressure here, then provable there. Shows the rungs composing.

The handoff to Rung 5. Same exploit, two methods: stress-test finds it by search; automated reasoning proves the fix is complete. This is the deliberate bridge — say it.

Numbers are illustrative. The CPI/Gini figures are plausible simulation outputs, not from a specific run; framed as "an example run." The arbitrage mechanism is the real craft-recipe mispricing from Rung 5.

40/67R4 · Stress-testRung 4 · Stress-test — the methods menu
Rung 4 · Stress-test · several ways to break it first · pick by what you’re testing

Different breakage, different robot.

MethodThe ideaBest for…Watch out
RL self-play design exploitsagents learn to win, incl. degeneratelyDominant builds, combos, map/skill exploitsNeeds env + compute; reward-shaping is an art
Agent-based simulation economypopulation of archetypes → emergent macroInflation, hoarding, arbitrage, P2W spiralsGarbage-in archetypes → garbage macro
Curiosity / novelty searchreward coverage of weird statesFinding the unknown-unknown edge casesCan chase irrelevant novelty
Fuzzing / property-based testingrandom & structured input bombardmentCrashes, soft-locks, input-handling bugsConfidence, not proof (→ Rung 5)
Imitation-learning botsagents trained to play like humansRealistic load tests & human-like adversariesSame tech attackers use for human-like cheats

Same goal — empirically break it in private — different robot per target. RL self-play for skill/design exploits; agent-based sim for the economy; novelty search for unknown-unknowns; fuzzing for crashes/soft-locks. Public methods; real-world examples include EA SEED (RL playtesting), DeepMind self-play, and NetEase Fuxi / DeepMind-×-EVE for economy simulation.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“The menu sorts by what kind of breakage you’re hunting. [walk the rows] RL self-play for skill and design exploits — dominant builds, infinite combos. Agent-based simulation for the economy — inflation, hoarding, arbitrage. Curiosity or novelty search when you’re after the unknown-unknowns, the edge cases nobody thought to test. Fuzzing and property-based testing for crashes and soft-locks — though remember, that’s confidence, not proof, which is the next rung. And imitation-learning bots when you want realistic load tests or human-like adversaries — noting, with a wink, that’s the same technology attackers use to build human-like cheats, which is why Rung one exists.”

[strip] “Same goal every time — empirically break it in private, at superhuman scale, before players do. These are all public methods; the well-known real examples are EA’s SEED team using RL for playtesting, DeepMind’s self-play work, and on the economy side, NetEase’s Fuxi lab and DeepMind’s tie-up with EVE Online.”

Rung 4 — methods menu. RL self-play (design exploits), agent-based sim (economy), novelty search (unknown-unknowns), fuzzing/PBT (crashes/soft-locks — note links to Rung 5), imitation-learning bots (load tests / human-like adversaries — note dual-use with cheating). Selection axis: what kind of breakage. Public methods; studios named as illustrative.

Nice connective tissue. Imitation-learning bots = the same tech as human-like cheats (ties to Rung 1's adversarial story); fuzzing = confidence not proof (ties to Rung 5). The menu quietly threads the whole ladder.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Novelty search
Rewarding agents for doing things they haven't done before, to maximise coverage of weird states rather than just winning.
Fuzzing
Bombarding the game with random or malformed inputs to trigger crashes, soft-locks, and edge-case bugs.
Imitation learning
Training an agent to copy human play from recorded data — useful for realistic bots, and (the dark side) for human-like cheats.
Unknown-unknown
A failure mode you didn't even know to look for — exactly what curiosity-driven agents are good at surfacing.
41/67R5 · Verify rulesRung 5 · Automated Reasoning, explained
Rung 5 · Verify rules · the technique ◆ SHARDFALL

A tireless rule-lawyer who checks every possible move.

📜⚖️♾️
Imagine…

Your playtesters try thousands of trades hoping to break SHARDFALL’s economy. Now imagine instead a tireless logician who reads the shop rulebook and checks every buy/sell/craft sequence that could ever happen — and either hands you the exact recipe that breaks it, or proves, with certainty, that nothing can.

1

Write the shop & crafting rules as precise logic — what buying, selling, crafting each do to gold and items.

2

State the promise to keep — “no loop of trades can leave a player with the same items but more gold.”

3

The solver searches all possibilities at once — by reasoning, not trial-and-error — and reports the exact exploit or proves there is none.

The gist: playtesting says “we tried a lot and didn’t break it.” This says “we proved it can’t break” — like proving a Sudoku has exactly one solution without trying every grid. The fancy name is Automated Reasoning (formal methods). Next slide: run the search and watch it find the infinite-gold loop, then prove the fix.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Rung five. This is the one that’s genuinely uncommon in games, and it’s my favourite — so stay with me through the story.”

“Today, to check a game economy, your playtesters try thousands of things hoping to break it. Now imagine instead a tireless logician who reads your shop rulebook and checks every sequence of buys, sells, and crafts that could ever happen — and either hands you the exact recipe that breaks it, or proves, with certainty, that nothing can.”

[walk the steps] “How? You write the shop and crafting rules as precise logic — what each action does to gold and items. You state the promise you want kept — ‘no loop of trades can leave a player with the same items but more gold.’ And the solver searches every possibility at once, by reasoning — not by trying them one at a time.”

[read the gist] “Here’s the difference in a sentence: playtesting says ‘we tried a lot and didn’t break it.’ This says ‘we proved it can’t break’ — like proving a Sudoku has exactly one solution without filling in every possible grid. The name is Automated Reasoning, and it’s the same family as AWS’s own provable-security work. Next slide — let’s actually break SHARDFALL’s economy, then prove the fix.”

ELI5 for the most unfamiliar + most differentiated technique. The leap: from "test a lot and hope" to "prove it can't happen." Rail's 2nd rung (teal). Conceptual heart; ~90s.

Analogy — tireless logician. Familiar (playtesters) vs new (a logician checking every sequence by reasoning). Two outcomes: exact exploit, or proof of none. Both valuable.

Mechanism. Write rules as logic → state the promise → solver searches all at once by reasoning (not sampling) → loophole or proof. Stress "all at once, by reasoning" = why it's certainty. Avoid "SMT / satisfiability / state space."

Gist. The Sudoku image is the cleanest proof-vs-search intuition. Name "Automated Reasoning" last. Honest ceiling (full on the credibility slide): proof is over the rules as written, not shipped code. AWS hook: Bedrock Automated Reasoning checks; the Zelkova/Dafny provable-security lineage — verify naming.

42/67R5 · Verify rulesRung 5 · Auto-Reasoning — how it works
Rung 5 · Verify rules · how automated reasoning actually works — plain-first, formula beside

Describe the rules as math; ask “can this ever break?”

the recipe — in plain English
  1. Write each action as its effect on gold & items. buy_shard: −40 gold, +1 shard. craft: −2 shards, +1 gem. …
  2. State the promise (invariant) you want to hold for all play. “No sequence ends with the same items but more gold.”
  3. Search for a counter-example — any combination of actions that breaks it. Here: a set of action-counts that nets items to zero but gold positive.
  4. Get an answer with certainty. A counter-example (the exact exploit), or a proof none exists in the searched space.
the property, for the engineersmoney-pump = positive inventory-neutral cycle
# find non-neg action counts n[a] such that:
∀ item i:  Σa n[a]·Δitem[a,i] = 0   # items unchanged
           Σa n[a]·Δgold[a]    > 0   # gold goes UP
           Σa n[a] > 0                # at least one action
# (integer arithmetic ⇒ an SMT problem)
# SAT → exploit found · UNSAT → proven safe
why this is proof, not testing Fuzzing tries specific sequences and reports what it happened to hit. A solver reasons over all assignments at once — so “UNSAT” means no exploit exists, not “we didn’t find one.” In production: AWS Bedrock Automated Reasoning checks / Z3.

Honest limits: the proof covers the rules as written, not the shipped code; you must encode the economy faithfully; and unbounded state (timers, RNG, huge recipe trees) can blow up — scope the model. Bounded search is complete only within its bound.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“How do you ‘prove’ an economy is safe? You stop testing it and start describing it. [left] Step one: write each action purely as its effect — buying a shard is minus forty gold, plus one shard; crafting is minus two shards, plus one gem. Step two: state the promise you want true for all play — no sequence ends with the same items but more gold. Step three: ask the solver to find any counter-example. Step four: you get a certain answer — either the exact exploit, or a proof there isn’t one.”

[code panel] “For the engineers, a money pump has a precise definition: a set of action-counts where every item nets to zero but gold comes out positive. [formula box] And here’s why this is proof and not testing: fuzzing tries specific sequences and tells you what it happened to hit. The solver reasons over every combination at once — so when it says ‘unsatisfiable,’ that means no exploit exists, full stop. In production that solver is AWS’s Automated Reasoning checks, or Z3 underneath.”

[strip] “Limits, honestly: it proves the rules as you wrote them, not the shipped binary; you have to encode the economy faithfully; and unbounded things — timers, randomness, enormous recipe trees — can explode, so you scope the model.”

Depth slide — automated reasoning mechanism. Demystifies "formal methods": actions-as-effects, the invariant, and the precise definition of a money pump (positive inventory-neutral cycle) as a satisfiability query. SAT=exploit, UNSAT=proof.

Proof-vs-testing. The core teaching beat: "UNSAT" is qualitatively different from "fuzzing found nothing." Say it explicitly.

Limits. Proof is over the spec not the binary; encoding fidelity matters; state explosion is real. This keeps the claim honest — and matches the §3 credibility slide.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Invariant
A promise that must hold no matter what players do — e.g. "you can't end with more gold than you put in." The thing we prove.
Counter-example
A specific sequence of actions that breaks the promise — the solver hands you the exact exploit recipe if one exists.
Inventory-neutral cycle
A loop of trades that leaves your items exactly as they started — if it also nets positive gold, it's a money pump you can repeat forever.
SAT / UNSAT
"Satisfiable" = a breaking case exists (exploit found). "Unsatisfiable" = no breaking case can exist (proven safe). The two possible verdicts.
Solver (SMT)
A program that reasons over all possible value assignments at once to decide SAT/UNSAT — e.g. Z3, or AWS's Automated Reasoning checks.
State explosion
When the number of possible game states grows so huge the solver can't finish — why you scope the model to a tractable slice.
43/67R5 · Verify rulesRung 5 · Auto-Reasoning — your data & I/O
Rung 5 · Verify rules · the inputs, the outputs, and where to get them

Its “data” isn’t telemetry — it’s your design spec.

▼ INPUT — the rules, as a table of action-effects (you already have these in design docs / config)
You provideWhat it isExample (SHARDFALL)Where it comes from
Resourcesthe quantities a player holdsgold, shard, gemeconomy design doc
Actions + effectseach action’s delta on each resourcecraft: −2 shard, +1 gemshop/crafting config (often already JSON)
Preconditionswhen an action is legalsell_gem needs gem ≥ 1game rules
Invariantthe promise to provegold_out ≤ gold_inyou state it (the design intent)
Bound (opt)max actions to search≤ 6 per actionyou pick (tractability)
▲ OUTPUT — a verdict  SAT → {exact action sequence, net gold, items}  ·  UNSAT → proof: no exploit in scope

The “data” is your economy config — which is usually already machine-readable. If your shop/recipes live in JSON or a spreadsheet, you can generate the model from them, so the proof tracks the real design as it changes. No telemetry, no labels — this runs pre-launch, in CI.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“This rung is different, and it trips people up: its ‘data’ is not telemetry at all — it’s your design spec. You’re not feeding it player behaviour; you’re feeding it the rules.”

[input table] “Four things. The resources players hold — gold, shards, gems. Each action as its effect — crafting is minus two shards, plus one gem. The preconditions that make an action legal. And the invariant — the promise you want proven, like ‘gold out never exceeds gold in.’ Optionally a bound, so the search stays fast. And here’s the kicker: your shop and crafting rules already live in a config file — JSON, a spreadsheet — so you can generate this model straight from the real economy, and the proof tracks your design as it changes.”

[output line] “The output is a verdict: either SAT — and it hands you the exact exploit sequence — or UNSAT, a proof there’s no exploit in scope.”

[strip] “No telemetry, no labels, nothing to collect from players — this runs before launch, in your CI pipeline, every time a designer tweaks a price.”

Depth slide — AR I/O & data sourcing. The crucial reframe: input is the design spec (resources, action-effects, preconditions, invariant), not telemetry — and that spec usually already exists as config (JSON/sheet), so the model can be generated and run in CI. Output is SAT(sequence)/UNSAT(proof).

Config-as-source. The buildability unlock: economy config is already machine-readable, so this isn't extra authoring — it's a check over what you already maintain. Runs pre-launch in CI.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Action-effect model
Each game action written purely as how it changes resources (gold/items) — the machine-readable form of your economy rules.
Precondition
The condition that must hold for an action to be legal (you can't sell a gem you don't have) — keeps the search realistic.
Invariant
The promise you ask the solver to prove holds for all play — e.g. "gold out ≤ gold in."
Bound
A cap on how many actions to consider, so the search finishes quickly. Within the bound the result is complete.
CI (continuous integration)
The automated pipeline that checks every code/config change. Running the proof here catches an economy exploit the moment a designer introduces it.
44/67R5 · Verify rulesRung 5 · Auto-Reasoning — worked example
Rung 5 · Verify rules · your config → the constraints → the verdict — the whole proof on one economy

Turn the shop config into math, and let it break.

1
your data — the shop & craft config (the JSON you already ship)
buy_shard: gold −40, shard +1 sell_shard: gold +35, shard −1 buy_gem: gold −100, gem +1 sell_gem: gold +90, gem −1 craft_gem: gold 0, shard −2, gem +1 ← the 2-shard recipe
2
let n[a] = how many times each action is used; write the “money pump” conditions
# items must net to ZERO (you end as you started): shard: n[buy_shard] − n[sell_shard] − 2·n[craft] = 0 gem: n[buy_gem] − n[sell_gem] + 1·n[craft] = 0 # gold must net POSITIVE; at least one action: gold: 35·n[sell_shard] + 90·n[sell_gem] − 40·n[buy_shard] − 100·n[buy_gem] > 0
3
solver searches all non-negative integer n[a] — finds one that satisfies all three
n[buy_shard]=2, n[craft]=1, n[sell_gem]=1, rest=0 check shard: 2 − 0 − 2·1 = 0 ✓ gem: 0 − 1 + 1 = 0 ✓ check gold: 90 − 40·2 = +10 > 0 ✓ SAT → money pump!
4
fix the config (craft needs 3 shards), re-run the SAME search
craft_gem: shard −3 # now 3·n[craft] in the shard eqn best possible inventory-neutral loop nets gold ≤ 0 no n[a] satisfies gold > 0 → UNSAT → PROVEN safe

✓ Buggy config: SAT — the solver hands you buy_shard×2 → craft → sell_gem = +10g, the exact recipe. Patched config: UNSAT — not “we didn’t find one,” but there is no such loop, across every integer combination in scope. That’s the leap to certainty.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — the bridge from config to proof (verbatim)

“This is the one people find most magical, so let me strip the magic out completely — config in, proof out, every step shown.”

[step 1] “Step one is literally your shop config — the JSON you already ship. Buy a shard for 40, sell for 35; buy a gem for 100, sell for 90; craft two shards into a gem.”

[step 2] “Now the only clever move. Let n-of-each-action be unknowns.
