A ‘money pump’ is any choice of those counts where your items net to zero — you end holding what you started — but your gold nets positive. I write that as three lines of arithmetic: shard nets zero, gem nets zero, gold greater than zero.”

[step 3] “The solver searches every non-negative integer combination and finds one: buy two shards, craft once, sell one gem. Check it — shards: two minus two, zero. Gems: minus one plus one, zero. Gold: ninety minus eighty, plus ten. All three satisfied. SAT — there’s your exploit, and it printed the recipe.”

[step 4] “Change one number — craft now needs three shards — and run the same search. Now no combination can make gold positive while items net to zero. UNSAT. And UNSAT is the whole point: it doesn’t mean ‘we didn’t find one,’ it means there isn’t one, across every combination in scope.”

[verdict] “Config to constraints to certainty — that’s automated reasoning, and there’s nothing left hidden in the word ‘proof.’”

Depth slide — AR WORKED EXAMPLE. The config→constraints→SAT/UNSAT bridge. Shows the actual integer-arithmetic encoding of a money pump (item-neutral, gold-positive), the satisfying assignment for the buggy economy, the arithmetic check, and the UNSAT after the 3-shard fix. This is exactly what verify_economy.py computes — now legible as math.

Arithmetic is exact. n=[2 buy_shard,1 craft,1 sell_gem]: shard 2−2·1=0, gem −1+1=0, gold 90−80=+10. Correct and verifiable on screen. The fix (3-shard craft) genuinely makes every inventory-neutral loop ≤0 gold.

SAT/SMT note. Integer counts + arithmetic = SMT (linear integer arithmetic); the slide says "solver searches all non-negative integer n[a]" which is accurate.

45/67R5 · Verify rulesRung 5 · Auto-Reasoning — the methods menu
Rung 5 · Verify rules · several tools to prove a property · pick by the question

Different properties want different provers.

Tool / approachProves…Best for…Watch out
Cycle search our demono positive inventory-neutral loop (money pump)Linear shop/craft economies — fast, no dependenciesLinear effects only; bounded counts
SMT solver — Z3 / cvc5arbitrary arithmetic/logic invariants over all statesConditional rules, caps, non-linear pricingCan be slow / undecidable; needs encoding skill
Bedrock Automated Reasoning checks AWS, managedpolicy/rule statements stated in natural-ish formLLM- or policy-driven rules; want a managed serviceVerify current scope/GA; model your domain to it
Model checker — TLA+ / Alloytemporal/state-machine properties (“never reaches X”)Matchmaking, quest state, progression soft-locksSpec language to learn; state explosion
Property-based testing (QuickCheck)nothing — but auto-tries thousands of smart casesA cheap first net before formal proofConfidence, not certainty (it’s still testing)

Match the tool to the property. Money-pump in a linear economy → cycle search (today). Conditional/non-linear rules → an SMT solver. “This state is never reachable” → a model checker. Want a managed path → Bedrock Automated Reasoning checks. Property-based testing is the cheap warm-up — just don’t mistake its silence for a proof.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“And there’s a menu here too — because the right prover depends on the shape of the property you’re proving.”

[walk the rows] “Our demo uses a cycle search — perfect for a linear shop-and-craft economy, fast, no dependencies. The moment your rules get conditional or non-linear — caps, tiered pricing, ‘if X then Y’ — you want a real SMT solver like Z3. If you’d rather a managed AWS path, Bedrock Automated Reasoning checks is the service to evaluate. When the property is about states over time — ‘matchmaking never deadlocks,’ ‘no quest soft-locks’ — that’s a model checker like TLA+. And property-based testing is the cheap warm-up that tries thousands of smart cases — just remember it gives you confidence, not a proof.”

[strip] “So: match the tool to the property. Linear money pump, cycle search. Conditional rules, SMT. Reachability over time, model checker. And never mistake a tester’s silence for certainty.”

Depth slide — AR methods menu. Five verification approaches mapped to the property type (cycle search, SMT, Bedrock AR checks, model checking, property-based testing). The selection axis is the SHAPE of the property, not scale.

Property → tool. The teaching: money-pump→cycle search; arithmetic invariant→SMT; temporal/reachability→model checker; managed→Bedrock. Property-based testing included honestly as "confidence not proof."

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
SMT solver
"Satisfiability Modulo Theories" — a tool that decides whether math/logic constraints can all be true at once; proves invariants by showing the "bad" case is impossible. Z3 and cvc5 are the common ones.
Model checker (TLA+/Alloy)
TLA+ and Alloy are specification languages; their checkers (TLC and the Alloy Analyzer) explore reachable states to prove temporal properties like "it can never deadlock" or "X never happens."
Temporal property
A promise about behaviour over time/sequence (eventually, never, always) — what model checkers specialise in.
Property-based testing
Auto-generates many smart test cases to try to break a property. Great net, but it samples — it can't prove the absence of a bug.
Decidable / undecidable
Some questions a solver can always answer; others it may never finish on. Why bounding and careful encoding matter.
46/67R5 · Verify rulesRung 5 · Auto-Reasoning — before & after
Rung 5 · Verify rules · before → after · fuzzing vs proving

“We tried a lot” vs. “it cannot happen.”

● BEFORE — fuzz & hopetraditional
for _ in range(N):
    seq = random_actions()
    if breaks_economy(seq):
        report(seq)          # only finds what it hits
# toy economy: finds the 4-step loop.
# real economy: long/rare/conditional
# loops slip through — & absence is
# never proven.
the limit verdict is always “none seen in N tries”
confidence, not certainty — coverage gaps stay invisible
● AFTER — prove the invariantrecommended
pump = find_money_pump(economy)   # solver
if pump:  fix_rule(); recheck()
# buggy:  buy_shard×2 → craft → sell_gem
#         = +10g, items net 0  → EXPLOIT
# patched (3-shard craft):       → UNSAT
validation buggy economy: exploit found in <1s, exact recipe
patched economy: PROVEN safe — no pump exists

Fuzzing’s silence means “we didn’t hit it in 10M tries.” The solver’s UNSAT means “there is nothing to hit.” One missed money pump = an economy meltdown — so the difference is the whole ballgame. (From verify_economy.py — re-run it.)

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Before and after, with the actual code. [left] The traditional approach fuzzes: generate random trade sequences, see if any breaks. To be fair, on a toy economy like ours, fuzzing will stumble onto a four-step loop. The problem is what happens in a real economy: the dangerous loops are long, rare, or only trigger under specific conditions — those slip through the random net — and crucially, fuzzing can never tell you there’s nothing left to find. Its verdict is always ‘none seen in N tries.’ That’s confidence, not certainty.”

[right] “The solver approach asks for a money pump directly. On the buggy economy it finds it in under a second — buy two shards, craft, sell the gem, plus ten gold with your items unchanged — and hands you the exact recipe. Patch the craft cost to three shards, ask again, and it returns unsatisfiable: proven safe — across the whole searched space, not just the cases it happened to try.”

[strip] “Sit with that gap. Fuzzing’s silence means ‘we didn’t hit it.’ The solver’s answer means ‘there is nothing to hit.’ When one missed pump melts your economy, that difference is the whole ballgame. It’s verify_economy.py in the kit.”

Depth slide — AR before/after. Fuzzing code + its "none seen in N tries" outcome vs the solver + its exploit-then-UNSAT outcome. Honesty note: we explicitly concede fuzzing WOULD find this toy 4-step loop — the real argument is (a) long/rare/conditional loops in a real economy slip the random net, and (b) fuzzing can never prove absence. Don't claim fuzzing "missed" a trivial loop; that's not credible.

47/67R5 · Verify rulesRung 5 · Money pump — LIVE
▶ This is a live interactive demo in the full deck (drag / run / tap). Below is its surrounding content; open confidence-ladder-deck-v3.html on a laptop to run it.
Rung 5 · Verify rules · LIVE DEMO — find the dupe, prove the fix ◆ SHARDFALL
SHARDFALL marketplace — automated reasoning over the rules
craft recipe: 2 shards → 1 gem
# the shop & crafting rules (each looks fine alone):
 buy_shard −40g · sell_shard +35g
 buy_gem −100g · sell_gem +90g
 craft: 2 shards → 1 gem
Press “search” — the solver reasons over every combination of legal actions.
Player gold after loops1000
Loops run0

Two shards cost 80g but craft into a gem worth 90g — a +10g loop with your items unchanged, repeatable forever. Slide the recipe to 3 shards and search again: PROVEN — no money pump exists.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — with the live demo (verbatim + actions)

“Here are SHARDFALL’s marketplace rules.” [read the four lines] “You buy a shard for 40 gold, sell it back for 35. Buy a gem for 100, sell it for 90. And you can craft: two shards become one gem. Look at each rule on its own — every one is completely reasonable. A playtester poking at any single action finds nothing wrong.”

“So let’s ask the solver a different question — not ‘did we find a bug’ but ‘is there any loop that ends with the same items and more gold?’” [click ‘search every legal loop’ — let the diagram cycle] “It’s reasoning over every combination… and — there it is.” [read the readout] “Money pump found. Buy two shards — that’s 80 gold. Craft them into a gem. Sell the gem for 90. You’re up 10 gold, and you’re holding exactly what you started with. Nothing.”

“Which means you can just… do it again.” [click ‘run the exploit’ several times — point at the gold counter climbing 1000 → 1010 → 1020…] “And again. Forever. That’s an infinite-gold dupe that would wreck the economy — and no playtester trying one action at a time would ever see it, because it only exists in the combination.”

“Now watch the fix.” [drag the recipe slider to 3 shards] “The designer makes crafting cost three shards instead of two — 120 gold of shards for a 90-gold gem, so crafting is now a money sink, like it was meant to be. Ask the solver again…” [click ‘search’] “Proven. No sequence of legal actions yields free gold. Not ‘we didn’t find one’ — there isn’t one. [try the exploit button — gold won’t climb] And that’s the leap from confidence to certainty, caught before a single player logged in.”

LIVE DEMO 3 — the machine finds the exploit your playtesters missed, then proves the patch. The emotional peak of the talk.

How to run it. (1) Recipe at 2 shards. Press "search every legal loop": the SVG animates the solver trying combinations, then locks onto the cycle buy_shard→buy_shard→craft→sell_gem and prints "MONEY PUMP FOUND +10g, items unchanged." (2) The "run the exploit" button unlocks — click it repeatedly and the gold counter ticks 1000→1010→1020… while the loop diagram pulses. Visceral: free money, forever. (3) Drag the recipe slider to 3 shards (now 120g of shards for a 90g gem — a sink, as intended). Press search again → "PROVEN — no sequence of legal actions yields free gold." (4) Note the gold counter can no longer climb.

Teaching point. Playtesting says "we didn't find a bug"; Automated Reasoning says "there ISN'T one" — or hands you the exact recipe. Confidence → certainty, before a player logs in. This matches verify_economy.py exactly (same +10g pump, same fix).

Honest ceiling + AWS. Proof is over the rules as written, not the shipped binary. Production: Bedrock Automated Reasoning checks / the Zelkova-Dafny lineage prove it unbounded; the demo's search is sound over the bounded action lattice.

48/67R5 · Verify rulesRung 5 · today → result
Rung 5 · Verify rules · today → with this → expected result
Today

Playtest, fuzz, and hope

QA tries huge numbers of trade combinations before launch.

Falls short: “we tried 10 million times” ≠ “it can’t happen.” One missed loop = an economy meltdown — found after players do.

→
With this

Prove it before you ship

Encode the economy; the solver checks every reachable state.

The thing testing can’t do: give you certainty over the whole space, plus the exact exploit recipe when one exists.

→
Expected result

A class of exploits never ships

Caught left of launch, offending rule pinpointed — not a post-launch fire drill.

Bottom line: no dupe meltdown, no refund wave, no rollback.

✦ in your genre — “prove a rule can’t be broken”
✦ Star Oracle (gacha)prove the pity timer always triggers by pull N — no edge case where a guaranteed drop silently fails.
Trading-cardprove no card combo creates an infinite turn / infinite mana loop before it wrecks the meta.
Puzzle / progressionprove every level is always completable — no soft-lock reachable state.
MMO economyprove crafting + vendor prices can’t form a money pump across the whole recipe tree.

A harder guarantee than the rungs below. Rungs 1–4 are all statistical — they detect, quantify, attribute, and stress-test, but always with some residual uncertainty. Rung 5 is the first mathematical proof: a whole class of exploits provably can’t exist, before a single player logs in.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Rung five in one line. [Today] Today you playtest, fuzz, and hope — and ‘we tried ten million trades’ is not the same as ‘it can’t happen.’ The one loop you missed becomes an economy meltdown you find after players do. [With this] With Automated Reasoning you get certainty over the whole space — plus the exact exploit recipe when one exists. [Result] The result: a whole class of exploits never ships. No dupe meltdown, no refund wave, no emergency rollback at 2am.”

[gesture to the dashed box — this is the strongest breadth slide, dwell here] “And this generalises beautifully. In a gacha game, you prove the pity timer always triggers — no edge case where a guaranteed drop silently fails. In a card game, you prove no combo creates an infinite turn before it wrecks the meta. In a puzzle game, you prove every level is always completable — no soft-lock. Same technique — prove an invariant holds over every reachable state — totally different rules.”

[read the strip] “And notice how it stacks on Rung one: detection catches the cheater after they act. This proves the exploit can’t exist — before anyone logs in.”

Rung 5 comparison + genre breadth.

Today. Playtest/fuzz/hope — honour it (catches most cheaply), but it's confidence not certainty; the one missed loop is catastrophic and found after players exploit it.

With this. Certainty over the whole space + the exact recipe (the localizing signal — the offending interaction, not a vague warning).

Expected result. A whole class never ships; business terms: no meltdown/refund wave/rollback.

Genre box. The most powerful breadth slide — the pity-timer guarantee (gacha), infinite-combo (TCG), soft-lock (puzzle), recipe-tree money pump (MMO). Same technique (prove an invariant holds over all reachable states), totally different rules. Read the gacha + TCG ones aloud.

Strip. Compounding: Rungs 1–4 are statistical; Rung 5 is preventive + certain (the first proof). Restate honest ceiling if asked.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Money pump
A loop of legal trades that leaves you with the same items but more money — repeat it forever for infinite currency. The economy version of the "infinite-gold dupe."
Invariant
A promise that must always stay true no matter what players do — e.g. "you can never end up with more gold than you earned." This rung proves invariants hold.
Infinite-combo (card games)
A combination of cards that can be repeated endlessly in one turn — it breaks the game, so designers want to prove none exists.
The meta
"Metagame" — the dominant strategies players settle on. A broken combo "wrecks the meta" by making one tactic mandatory.
Soft-lock
A state where a player can't win or lose — stuck forever, often from a design oversight. Rung 2 can prove every level stays completable.
Recipe tree
The full web of crafting recipes (item A + B → C, C + D → E…). A money pump can hide in how recipes and shop prices interact across the whole tree.
49/67R6 · Verify pipelineRung 6 · Commit-reveal, explained
Rung 6 · Verify pipeline · the technique ◆ SHARDFALL

The sealed envelope you can’t secretly swap.

✉️🔒🔍
Imagine…

Before a SHARDFALL crate opens, the server writes the result on a card, seals it in an envelope, and hands you a tamper-proof fingerprint of what’s inside. You open the crate. Then the envelope is unsealed — and you check the fingerprint still matches. The server could not have changed the card after seeing your spin.

1

A “fingerprint” is published first. Change even one letter inside and it changes completely — and you can’t work backwards from it to peek.

2

The outcome is locked before you spin — and depends partly on a number you contribute, so the house can’t cherry-pick it.

3

Anyone can re-check later. If a result was edited afterward, the fingerprint won’t match — a guaranteed catch, not a guess.

The gist: a one-way “fingerprint” (a hash) lets you lock something in publicly without revealing it, then prove later it was never changed. The pattern is commit-reveal; stronger cousins are VRF and attestation. Next slide: tamper with a real crate record and watch a real hash turn red.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Rung six. This is the one that sounds the most intimidating — cryptography — and it’s actually the most intuitive once you picture a sealed envelope.”

“Before a SHARDFALL crate opens, the server writes the result on a card, seals it in an envelope, and hands you a tamper-proof fingerprint of what’s inside. Then you open the crate. Then the envelope gets unsealed — and you check the fingerprint still matches. Because you got that fingerprint first, the server could not possibly have changed the card after seeing your spin.”

[walk the steps] “Two things make the fingerprint magic: change even one letter of what’s inside and the fingerprint changes completely — and you can’t run it backwards to peek. The outcome is locked before you act, and it depends partly on a number you contribute, so the house can’t cherry-pick a result. And anyone can re-check later — if a result was edited, the fingerprint won’t match. A guaranteed catch, not a guess.”

[read the gist] “That fingerprint is called a hash; the pattern is commit-reveal; the stronger cousins are VRF and attestation. And on the next slide — this is the fun one — you’re going to tamper with a real crate record and watch a real hash turn red.”

ELI5 for the cryptographic rung — sounds intimidating, actually the most intuitive once anchored to a sealed envelope. Rail's 3rd rung (gold).

Analogy — sealed envelope. Universal (sealed bids, magician's prediction). Carries all three ideas: commit before the spin, can't change after, anyone checks at reveal.

Mechanism. Fingerprint (hash) published first — two plain properties: any tiny change flips it; can't reverse it to peek. Outcome locked before the spin, depends partly on the player's number (house can't cherry-pick). Anyone re-checks; edited result won't match. Avoid bare "SHA-256 / preimage / nonce" — describe them.

Gist + ceiling. "Hash = one-way fingerprint," then "commit-reveal," then VRF/attestation. Crucial ceiling (full two slides on): proves not-changed-after-the-fact, NOT that the odds were fair. Plant gently. Tee up the live tamper demo with a REAL hash.

50/67R6 · Verify pipelineRung 6 · Commit-reveal — how it works
Rung 6 · Verify pipeline · how commit-reveal actually works — plain-first, formula beside

Lock the answer in public, before anyone acts.

the recipe — in plain English · order matters
  1. Commit (before the spin). Server picks a secret seed, publishes only its fingerprint: hash(seed). The drop is now locked but hidden.
  2. Player adds entropy. The player contributes their own number — so the server can’t have pre-chosen a result that depends on it.
  3. Reveal & derive. Outcome = hash(server_seed + client_seed + nonce), mapped to the odds. Server now publishes the seed.
  4. Anyone re-checks. Confirm hash(seed) matches the earlier fingerprint, and that the seeds really produce that drop.
the protocol — for the engineersreal SHA-256
# BEFORE the player acts:
commit = sha256(server_seed)          # published
# AFTER reveal — anyone can run this:
roll   = int(sha256(f"{server_seed}:{client_seed}:{nonce}")[:8],16) % 10000
ok     = (sha256(server_seed) == commit)   # not swapped
         and outcome(roll) == stored_outcome     # not edited
the two properties that make it work One-way: you can’t run the hash backwards to learn the seed from the fingerprint. Collision-resistant: you can’t find a different seed giving the same fingerprint — so the committed value is truly locked.

Honest limits: proves the result wasn’t changed after the fact — NOT that the odds are fair (you can honestly commit a rigged table). Watch for seed reuse & weak entropy. Step up to a VRF for randomness the server can’t bias, or attestation for the running binary.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“The sealed-envelope trick, made precise — and the order is everything. [left] Before the spin, the server picks a secret seed and publishes only its fingerprint — the drop is locked but hidden. The player adds their own number, so the server can’t have pre-chosen a result that depends on it. On reveal, the outcome is derived from both seeds together, and the server publishes the seed. Now anyone can re-check two things: the seed still matches the fingerprint, and those seeds really do produce that drop.”

[code panel] “For the engineers, that’s the whole protocol — and it’s real SHA-256, the same function the live demo runs in your browser. [formula box] Two properties make it work: it’s one-way, so you can’t recover the seed from the fingerprint and peek; and it’s collision-resistant, so you can’t find a different seed that fits the published fingerprint. Together, the committed value is genuinely locked.”

[strip] “And the limit I’ll keep repeating: this proves the result wasn’t changed after the fact — it does not prove the odds were fair. You can honestly commit a rigged table. Watch for seed reuse and weak randomness. Want unbiasable randomness? Step up to a VRF. Want to prove the running code? That’s attestation.”

Depth slide — commit-reveal protocol. The exact sequence (commit → client entropy → reveal → verify) with real SHA-256 code matching the demo, plus the two hash properties (one-way, collision-resistant) that make it sound.

Order matters. Stress that commit happens BEFORE the act and the client contributes entropy — those two facts are what defeat the "chose-it-after" and "house-cherry-picked" attacks respectively.

The ceiling. Predetermined ≠ fair. Repeat it here; it's fully argued on the "what proof means" slide.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Seed
A secret starting number the random outcome is derived from. Whoever knows the seed can reproduce the exact result.
Hash / fingerprint
A short code computed from data; any change to the data changes the code completely, and you can't work backwards from the code to the data.
Commit
Publishing the fingerprint of the seed before the player acts — locking the outcome in without revealing it.
Entropy
Genuine randomness. The player adding their own number is "adding entropy" so the house can't have pre-arranged a convenient result.
One-way / collision-resistant
One-way = can't reverse the hash to get the seed. Collision-resistant = can't find a different seed with the same fingerprint. Both are why the lock holds.
VRF
"Verifiable Random Function" — a stronger tool that proves the randomness was both unpredictable and not biased by the server.
51/67R6 · Verify pipelineRung 6 · Commit-reveal — your data & I/O
Rung 6 · Verify pipeline · the inputs, the outputs, and where to get them

Five fields per draw — and your RNG already makes most of them.

▼ STORED PER DRAW — the audit record (one row per loot/gacha pull, crit roll, shuffle…)
FieldWhat it isWhen createdWhere it comes from
server_seedsecret random value behind the outcomebefore the drawyour CSPRNG (KMS/HSM-backed)
commitmentsha256(server_seed), shown to player firstbefore the drawcomputed; published / signed
client_seedthe player’s contributed entropyat draw timegame client (player-set or device RNG)
noncecounter so repeats can’t collideat draw timeper-player counter
outcomethe drop, derived (not chosen)at revealmap(roll → drop_table)
▲ VERIFIER OUTPUT  per record → PASS  or  FAIL{draw_id, stored_outcome, derived_outcome}

You already generate the randomness — this just records & commits it. The only new work: publish commitment before the player acts, mix in a client_seed, and log all five. Custody the server seed in AWS KMS; store the append-only log in DynamoDB (hash-chained); expose a Lambda verify endpoint.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“What does this rung need stored? Five fields per draw — and the good news is your random-number system already produces most of them; you’re just recording and committing what you already roll.”

[walk the table] “A server seed — the secret behind the outcome, from a proper cryptographic RNG, ideally key-managed in KMS. Its commitment — the SHA-256 fingerprint you show the player before they act. The client seed, the player’s own contributed randomness. A nonce, just a counter so repeats can’t collide. And the outcome, which is derived from those, not chosen — you map the roll through your existing drop table.”

[output line] “The verifier walks the log and returns PASS per record, or FAIL with the draw id and both the stored and the derived outcome — so a tamper names itself.”

[strip] “The only genuinely new work is: publish the commitment first, mix in the client seed, and log all five fields. Custody the seed in KMS, store an append-only hash-chained log in DynamoDB, and put the verify behind a Lambda. That’s the whole build.”

Depth slide — commit-reveal I/O & data sourcing. The 5-field audit record with when-created and source, plus the verifier output schema. Reframes the work as "record & commit randomness you already generate," with the concrete AWS custody/storage/verify mapping (KMS/DynamoDB/Lambda).

Minimal new work. The reassurance: you already roll RNG; the delta is commit-first + client entropy + log. Keeps scope small.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
CSPRNG
"Cryptographically-Secure Pseudo-Random Number Generator" — randomness an attacker can't predict, the right source for a server seed (not rand()).
Server seed / client seed
The secret server value behind the draw, and the player's contributed randomness mixed in so the house can't pre-pick a result.
Commitment
The published hash of the server seed, shown before the draw — the "sealed envelope."
Nonce
A simple counter that makes each draw unique so identical inputs can't collide.
Append-only / hash-chained log
A record store you can add to but not silently edit; chaining each row's hash to the prior makes any tampering break the chain.
KMS / HSM
AWS Key Management Service / Hardware Security Module — secure custody and signing of the secret seeds so insiders can't quietly swap them.
52/67R6 · Verify pipelineRung 6 · Commit-reveal — worked example
Rung 6 · Verify pipeline · one crate → the hash → tamper fails — the whole protocol on one draw

Follow one loot draw — then try to cheat it.

1
BEFORE the spin — server commits (publishes the fingerprint of its secret)
server_seed = "a3f9…" # secret, in KMS commitment = sha256(server_seed) = "7b41c2…" # shown to player NOW
2
player acts — adds their own seed; outcome is DERIVED, not chosen
client_seed = "f20b" nonce = 7 # player-supplied + counter roll = int( sha256("a3f9…:f20b:7")[:8], 16 ) % 10000 = 9912 outcome = (roll < 100) ? RARE : common → common # 9912 ≥ 100
3
AFTER reveal — anyone recomputes and confirms (the “verify” the player can run)
sha256(revealed_seed) == published commitment? ✓ re-derive roll from the seeds → 9912 → common == stored? ✓ PASS — predetermined, untouched.
4
now an insider tampers — rewrites the stored outcome to RARE
stored_outcome ← RARE # free rare item verify: re-derive from seeds → roll 9912 → common but stored says RARE → MISMATCH → FAIL (and the commitment can’t be changed — it was published first)

🔒 Not a confidence score — a deterministic check. The seeds reproduce common every time, so the forged RARE is caught with certainty, naming draw #7. What it proves: the result wasn’t changed after the fact. What it does NOT prove: that 1% was the honest rate — that’s the next rung up (VRF).

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — the bridge from fields to a caught tamper (verbatim)

“Five fields on a slide, a sealed-envelope story on another — let me fuse them into one draw you can follow end to end, then break.”

[step 1] “Before the spin, the server has a secret seed and publishes only its SHA-256 fingerprint — that string ‘7b41c2…’. The player sees it now, before anything happens. The outcome is already locked; it just can’t be read.”

[step 2] “Player acts. They add their own seed and a nonce, and the outcome is derived by hashing the three together — here the roll comes out 9912, which is ≥ 100, so it’s a common drop. Nobody chose common; the math produced it.”

[step 3] “At reveal the server publishes the secret seed, and anyone — the player, you, a regulator — recomputes: does its hash match the fingerprint from step one? Yes. Do the seeds re-derive the stored outcome? Yes. Pass.”

[step 4] “Now the insider attack. Someone rewrites the stored result to RARE for a free item. Re-run verify: the seeds still derive common, the row says RARE — mismatch, fail, draw #7 named. And they can’t fix the fingerprint to cover it, because it was published before they ever acted.”

[verdict] “Notice there’s no threshold, no probability — the seeds reproduce the outcome or they don’t. And the honest line I’ll keep repeating: this proves it wasn’t changed, not that 1% was the honest rate. For unbiasable odds you climb to a VRF — the next slide’s menu.”

Depth slide — commit-reveal WORKED EXAMPLE. One draw through the full protocol: commit (sha256 of seed, published first) → derive outcome from seeds+nonce → verify (commitment match + re-derive) → tamper → deterministic FAIL naming the draw. Mirrors tamper_check.py exactly. Ends by re-stating the ceiling (proves untouched, not fair-odds).

Determinism, not probability. The teaching contrast with Rung 1: no cutoff, no score — the seeds either reproduce the stored outcome or they don't. A hard true positive.

Re-state the ceiling. "Proves not-changed, not honest-odds → VRF" — keeps the credibility line intact and bridges to the methods menu.

53/67R6 · Verify pipelineRung 6 · Commit-reveal — the methods menu
Rung 6 · Verify pipeline · a ladder within the rung · pick by threat & trust model

How strong a guarantee, against whom?

MechanismGuaranteesBest for…Watch out
Commit-reveal (hash) our demooutcome wasn’t changed after the actLoot/gacha draws, shuffles — simple & cheapDoesn’t prove odds fair; needs good entropy
Signed commitments+ who issued it (non-repudiation)Multi-service backends, insider accountabilityKey management & rotation (use KMS)
VRF (verifiable random function)randomness unpredictable and server-unbiasableHigh-stakes draws where the house could profitHeavier crypto; key setup
Hash-chain / Merkle logthe whole history is tamper-evident at onceAuditing millions of draws; one root proves allDesign for efficient proofs & reads
Nitro Enclave attestation code integritythe running binary is the one you shippedProving the RNG service itself wasn’t swappedInfra setup; verify the attestation doc
ZK proof advanced“computed correctly” without revealing hidden stateProve a hand was adjudicated fairly w/o showing cardsSpecialist tooling; heaviest to build

Same audit log, escalating guarantees. Start with commit-reveal (kills “changed-it-after”). Add signing for insider accountability, a VRF when the house could bias odds, a Merkle log to audit at scale, attestation to prove the code, and ZK when you must verify hidden-information outcomes. Climb only as far as your threat model demands.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“There’s actually a little ladder within this rung, and you climb it by asking ‘how strong a guarantee, against whom?’”

[walk the rows] “Commit-reveal — our demo — proves the outcome wasn’t changed after the fact. Add a signature and you also prove who issued it — that’s insider accountability. Step up to a VRF when the house could actually profit by biasing the odds, because a VRF makes the randomness unbiasable, not just unchanged. A Merkle log lets you audit millions of draws at once — one root hash vouches for the whole history. Nitro Enclave attestation proves the RNG service itself is the binary you shipped, not a swapped one. And at the far end, a zero-knowledge proof lets you prove a hand was adjudicated correctly without revealing the cards — the advanced, fog-of-war case.”

[strip] “Same audit log underneath; escalating guarantees on top. Climb only as far as your threat model demands — for most loot boxes, commit-reveal plus signing is plenty.”

Depth slide — commit-reveal methods menu. Six escalating crypto mechanisms (commit-reveal → signing → VRF → Merkle → attestation → ZK) keyed to threat/trust model. Reinforces the talk's "match strength to stakes" thesis at the mechanism level.

A ladder within the ladder. Nice recursion of the core thesis: even inside rung 3, you escalate guarantee to match threat. ZK is named here as the advanced ceiling (and the bridge to "verifiable computation" if asked).

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Non-repudiation
A signature proving who produced a record, so they can't later deny it — insider accountability.
VRF
"Verifiable Random Function" — produces randomness plus a proof it was both unpredictable and not biased by whoever generated it.
Merkle tree / root
A way to summarise a huge log into one small "root" hash; you can prove any single record belongs without sending the whole log.
Attestation (Nitro Enclave)
Hardware-signed proof that a specific, unmodified program is what's running — proves the RNG service itself wasn't swapped.
Zero-knowledge (ZK) proof
Proves a computation was done correctly while hiding its secret inputs — e.g. "this card draw was legal" without revealing the deck.
Threat model
An explicit statement of who might attack and how — it tells you how high up this menu you actually need to climb.
54/67R6 · Verify pipelineRung 6 · Commit-reveal — before & after
Rung 6 · Verify pipeline · before → after · trust vs verify

An edited record: silent, then caught and named.

● BEFORE — “trust the server”traditional
# outcome just sits in a row
db.update(draw_id, outcome="RARE")
# verification?
def verify(draw):
    return True   # nothing to check
validation · insider edits 1 of 200 records tamper detected? no
the value simply changed; no signal exists
● AFTER — commit-reveal verifyrecommended
def verify(d):
    roll = derive(d.server_seed, d.client_seed, d.nonce)
    return sha256(d.server_seed) == d.commit        and outcome(roll) == d.stored_outcome
same edit, same 200 records result: 199 pass, 1 FAILS — names draw #N
“fingerprint derives ‘common’, stored ‘RARE’” — a hard true positive

No probability, no threshold to tune: the check either reproduces the stored outcome or it doesn’t. Tampering becomes mathematically undeniable, down to the exact record. (From tamper_check.py — real SHA-256, re-run it.)

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Last before-and-after. [left] ‘Trust the server’ means the outcome just sits in a database row, and ‘verification’ is a function that returns true because there’s nothing to check. An insider edits one of two hundred records — tamper detected? No. The value simply changed; no signal exists anywhere.”

[right] “Commit-reveal verification re-derives the outcome from the sealed seeds and checks it against both the published fingerprint and the stored value. Same edit, same records — 199 pass, one fails, and it names the exact draw: the fingerprint derives ‘common,’ but the row says ‘RARE.’”

[strip] “Notice what’s not here: no probability, no threshold to tune. The check either reproduces the stored outcome or it doesn’t. Tampering goes from invisible to mathematically undeniable, down to the record. That’s tamper_check.py — real SHA-256 — in the kit.”

Depth slide — commit-reveal before/after. The "trust" stub (verify returns True) vs the real derive-and-compare check. Drives home the rung-3 distinction: not a tuned threshold, a deterministic reproduction — hence a hard true positive, not a suspicion.

55/67R6 · Verify pipelineRung 6 · Tamper test — LIVE
▶ This is a live interactive demo in the full deck (drag / run / tap). Below is its surrounding content; open confidence-ladder-deck-v3.html on a laptop to run it.
Rung 6 · Verify pipeline · LIVE DEMO — tamper it, watch the real hash break ◆ SHARDFALL
SHARDFALL crate log — commit-reveal · real SHA-256, computed in your browser
Click any crate’s outcome to tamper it (gift yourself a RARE). Then press “verify all.”

“Trust the server” sees nothing — the value simply changed. Commit-reveal recomputes each fingerprint and the tampered record provably fails, naming itself. Not a confidence score — a hard true positive.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this — with the live demo (verbatim + actions)

“This is a real log of 24 SHARDFALL crate openings. Each tile has its sealed fingerprint up top and the drop the player got. And I want to be clear — these are real SHA-256 hashes, computed right here in the browser. No network, nothing pre-baked. A cryptographer in the room can check the algorithm.”

“First, the honest log.” [click ‘verify all records’ — tiles flash green] “Twenty-four out of twenty-four verify. The rare-drop rate is about what we configured. Everything matches its fingerprint.”

“Now let’s play the insider. Say I work on this game and I want to gift myself a rare item.” [click a ‘common’ tile — it flips to RARE and turns amber] “I’ll just reach into the database and rewrite this record from common to RARE. Done. The stored value changed — but notice the published fingerprint did not, because I committed to it before any of this.”

“Under ‘trust the server,’ this is invisible — the value simply changed, nothing flags it. So let’s run the check.” [click ‘verify all’ again — the tampered tile turns red] “There. That record fails, and it names itself: the sealed fingerprint derives ‘common,’ but the log says ‘RARE.’”

“And here’s the line that separates this rung from Rung one: this is not a confidence score. The math doesn’t think it was altered — it proves it, and points at the exact record. That’s the difference between a suspicion and a cryptographic control over your own pipeline.”

LIVE DEMO 4 — a real hash, breaking live. The grid shows ~24 SHARDFALL crate draws; each tile has a nonce, a short SHA-256 commitment, and an outcome (mostly "common," ~1% "RARE"). The hashes are computed by a real SHA-256 in the page — no network, works from file://.

How to run it. (1) Press "verify all" on the honest log → every tile flashes green, readout: "24/24 verify, rare rate ~as configured." (2) Click a "common" tile — it flips to RARE and turns amber (an insider gifting a free rare item). Note the stored outcome changed but the published commitment did NOT. (3) Press "verify all" again → the tampered tile turns RED and the readout names it: "draw #N FAILS — fingerprint says 'common', stored 'RARE'." (4) Optionally tamper several; each is caught independently.

Teaching point. This isn't a suspicion with a confidence score — the math doesn't think it was altered, it proves it, and pinpoints the record. That's the difference between probabilistic ML (Rung 1) and a cryptographic control over your own pipeline. The SHA-256 is real — invite a skeptic to verify the algorithm.

Reframe + AWS. Pitch it as an INTERNAL control (is the crate service running my configured odds? would a rewritten record be caught?), not a player gimmick. Production: KMS (signing), Lambda (verify endpoint), DynamoDB + application-level hash-chaining (QLDB reached end-of-support in 2025 — don't use it), Nitro Enclaves (attestation, which attests image measurements / PCRs). [SageMaker AI for the heavier ML in earlier rungs.]

56/67R6 · Verify pipelineRung 6 · today → result
Rung 6 · Verify pipeline · today → with this → expected result
Today

“Trust our server”

The server stores each crate outcome in a database. We assume nobody changed it.

Falls short: a bad config push, a buggy deploy, or an insider can rewrite a record — and nothing detects it. You trust your own pipeline on faith.

→
With this

Commit-reveal audit log

Publish the fingerprint before the spin; derive the outcome from sealed seeds.

The thing “trust us” can’t do: let anyone re-check and have tampering provably fail — not “look suspicious,” fail.

→
Expected result

Tampering becomes impossible to hide

A single altered record is caught and named, instantly and with certainty.

Bottom line: an insider/supply-chain control your player-facing analytics simply don’t provide.

✦ in your genre — “prove the outcome wasn’t tampered”
✦ Star Oracle (gacha)commit-reveal on every summon proves banner odds were applied as published — the core regulatory ask.
Card / TCGprove a pack was sealed before purchase and not reshuffled for the buyer.
Poker / casinoprovably-fair shuffles — the original home of this technique.
Any PvPattestation proves the match server ran the shipped build, not a modified one.

The strongest proof yet. Rung 5 proves the rules as written are sound. Rung 6 proves the live, running system actually matches them and wasn’t altered — and a failure here is a certainty, not a confidence score.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Rung six in one line. [Today] Today it’s ‘trust our server’ — the outcome sits in a database and we assume nobody touched it. But a bad config push, a buggy deploy, or an insider can rewrite a record and nothing detects it. You’re trusting your own pipeline on faith. [With this] With commit-reveal, anyone can re-check, and tampering doesn’t just look suspicious — it provably fails. [Result] The result: tampering becomes impossible to hide, caught and named instantly — an insider and supply-chain control your player-facing analytics simply don’t give you.”

“And notice I framed that entirely inward — this is about catching tampering in your own backend, not a player gimmick. That’s what makes it fundable without ever mentioning the player.”

[dashed box] “Across genres: in gacha, commit-reveal on every summon proves the banner odds were applied as published — that’s the core regulatory ask, and it’s our bridge to the last rung. In card games, prove a pack was sealed before purchase. In poker, provably-fair shuffles — that’s literally where this technique was born. And attestation proves your match server is running the build you shipped, not a modified one.”

Rung 6 comparison + genre breadth. Land the under-served idea: cryptographic verification turned inward.

Today. "Trust our server" — insider/config/deploy can rewrite a record undetected. A supply-chain/insider gap.

With this. Anyone re-checks; tampering provably fails, not "looks suspicious."

Expected result. Tampering impossible to hide; a control player-facing analytics don't provide. Reframe = internal control, not player gimmick → fundable without invoking the player.

Genre box. gacha summon odds (the regulatory core), TCG packs, poker shuffles (the technique's origin), PvP server attestation. Read the gacha one aloud — it's the regulatory bridge to Rung 7.

Strip. Completes the compounding: hunch → rules-as-written → running-system. AWS: KMS, Lambda, DynamoDB+hash-chain, Nitro.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Summon (gacha)
A single random pull for a character/item. "Commit-reveal on every summon" = prove each pull used the published odds.
Pack (card games)
A sealed set of random cards you buy. The worry: was it really sealed before you paid, or reshuffled to give you worse cards?
Provably-fair shuffle
A card shuffle the player can verify wasn't rigged — born in online poker/casino, the original home of commit-reveal.
PvP
"Player versus Player" — competitive modes where players fight each other (as opposed to PvE, players versus the game's computer enemies).
Attestation
A hardware-backed proof that a server is running the exact program you shipped — not a modified, cheat-friendly version.
Hash-chain
Linking records so each one's fingerprint includes the previous one — like a tamper-evident chain where altering any link breaks all the ones after it.
57/67R7 · ExposeRung 7 · Expose
Rung 7 · Expose · optional · plain English + comparison ✦ STAR ORACLE

Hand the sealed envelope to the public.

Imagine publishing the sealed envelope and the result, so any player — or a regulator — can check a STAR ORACLE banner pull themselves and confirm you didn’t cheat. The same Rung-6 proof, made outward-facing.

The honest boundary

This makes your game auditable — not automatically accountable. Math gives an outsider a record they can verify. Whether you act on it — refund, reverse, re-rank — is policy, not cryptography. Proof supplies the evidence; people supply the remedy.

Today

“Trust us, the odds are 1%”

Players are told the odds and asked to believe them. Disputes are a shouting match — your word vs. theirs.

With this

“Don’t trust us — check it”

The published record lets a player, regulator, or partner re-run the check independently and get the same answer.

Expected result

Evidence-based disputes

Fewer CS escalations and chargebacks; a real compliance posture; a brand differentiator — where it’s worth it.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Rung seven is optional — and it’s the shortest, because it’s not a new technique. It’s the same Rung-6 proof, just pointed outward.”

“Take that sealed envelope and publish it. Now any player — or a regulator — can check a Star Oracle banner pull themselves and confirm you didn’t cheat. [walk Today → With → Result] Today it’s ‘trust us, the odds are one percent,’ and every dispute is your word against theirs. With this, the published record lets them re-run the check independently. The result: disputes become evidence-based — fewer escalations, fewer chargebacks, a real compliance posture, and a genuine brand differentiator where it’s worth it.”

“But I have to draw one hard line, and it’s the most important sentence in the talk.” [read the boundary card slowly] “This makes your game auditable — it does not automatically make it accountable. The math gives an outsider a record they can verify. Whether you act on it — refund, reverse, re-rank — is your policy, not your cryptography. Proof supplies the evidence. People supply the remedy. Anyone who tells you the math alone makes you accountable is overselling it.”

Optional final rung (7) + the sharpest line: auditable vs accountable. Rail's top rung (bright). Brief but precise — over-claiming here undoes the credibility built so far. Compresses ELI5 + comparison because Rung 4 is "the same proof, pointed outward," not a new technique. Uses STAR ORACLE because gacha odds are the canonical exposure case.

Headline + imagine. Reuses the envelope; the verifier is now an outsider who doesn't trust you — exactly why internal hygiene isn't enough and the cryptographic artifact is.

Today/With/Result. belief-based → independent re-verification → evidence-based disputes (fewer escalations/chargebacks, compliance posture, differentiator) "where it's worth it" (ties to pick-and-choose).

Auditable vs accountable card. The most important honesty distinction. Accountability = duty to answer + redress; crypto supplies neither, only an evidentiary substrate. "Auditable, not automatically accountable — proof supplies the evidence, people supply the remedy." This earns trust on everything else.

58/67·What 'proof' means
The credibility check · what “proof” does and doesn’t mean

Be precise about what each proof actually proves.

TechniqueWhat it really provesWhat people assume it provesThe gap to name out loud
Commit-revealThe outcome was locked in before you acted.“The odds are fair.”You can honestly seal an envelope drawn from a rigged deck. It kills only the “changed-it-after” trick.
Automated ReasoningNo legal play breaks the rule — in the written rules.“The shipped game has no exploit.”Proof is over the rulebook, not the actual code that shipped.
AttestationProduction is running this exact program.“The game’s logic is correct.”Proves which program runs, not that the program is right.
Anomaly / graph (Rung 1)This looks unlike normal / forms a suspicious cluster.“This player is guilty.”A ranked suspicion with false positives — feeds human review, never an auto-ban.

Why a player would still trust it: they don’t trust your claim — they re-run the check. But it only ever covers the narrow thing the proof is about. Disclosure, governance, and audit bridge the rest — not the math.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“I’ve used the word ‘proof’ a lot. So let me be ruthless about what each proof actually covers — because the fastest way to lose a room of engineers is to let ‘mathematical proof’ mean more than it does.”

[walk the rows] “Commit-reveal proves the outcome was locked in before you acted. It does not prove the odds are fair — you can honestly seal an envelope drawn from a rigged deck. It only kills the ‘changed-it-after’ trick. Automated Reasoning proves no legal play breaks the rule — in the rulebook you wrote, not necessarily the code that shipped. Attestation proves which program is running, not that the program is correct. And Rung one proves nothing at all — it’s a ranked suspicion with false positives.”

“So why would a player ever trust any of it?” [read the strip] “Because they don’t trust your claim — they re-run the check themselves. That’s real power. But it only ever covers the narrow thing the proof is about. The gap from ‘this draw was sealed beforehand’ to ‘this game is fair to me’ is bridged by disclosure, governance, and audit — not by the math. Say that out loud, and the skeptics in the room will trust everything else you said.”

The credibility anchor of the whole talk. After climbing to "proof," fence in what the word covers — the fastest way to lose a technical room is to let "proof" imply more than it delivers. Delivering this unprompted earns the skeptic's trust. Rung-agnostic (rail dim).

Discipline. Separate the thing actually proven from the thing people assume.

Commit-reveal. Predetermined ≠ fair; you can honestly seal a rigged deck. Defeats only "changed-it-after." The most over-sold "provably fair" claim — correct it.

Automated Reasoning. Proves over the written rulebook, not the compiled code. Map ≠ territory.

Attestation. Proves which program runs, not that it's correct. Identity ≠ correctness.

Rung 1 row (added). Anomaly/graph prove nothing — a ranked suspicion with false positives. Keeps the whole ladder honest in one table.

Strip. "Don't trust, verify" works because trust is removed — but covers only the narrow proven property; the rest is governance, not math.

59/67·Keep the source of truth honest
Across every rung · the two things that quietly rot a source of truth

A ladder is only as trustworthy as the evidence stays.

The seven rungs give you strong evidence. But strong evidence can still quietly stop being true — and when it does, every rung above it inherits the rot. Two cross-cutting checks keep the whole ladder honest. They aren’t rungs — they don’t sit on the strength axis; they sit underneath all of it.

across TIME

Drift & continuous validation

A detector that was right last month can be silently wrong today — the meta shifts, cheats adapt, the population changes. Watch the inputs and the accuracy for drift, and re-validate.

across COHORTS

Fairness & bias auditing

If your own matchmaking or moderation AI skews against a region or dialect, your “abuse data” is distorted at the source. Slice every metric by cohort and check for disparity.

A reliable source of truth needs evidence that is strong (the ladder), that stays valid (drift), and that isn’t skewed (fairness). Skip these two and your dashboards drift into confident nonsense — green, and wrong.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“We’ve climbed all seven rungs. Before we tie it together, two checks that sit underneath the whole ladder — because here’s a failure mode the rungs alone don’t catch: strong evidence that quietly stops being true.”

“These are not rungs. A rung is a stronger grade of evidence. These are different — they’re what keeps every rung’s evidence trustworthy. Two of them. [left] Across time: drift. The detector that was sharp last month goes blunt as cheats adapt and the meta moves — and nothing tells you, unless you watch for it. [right] Across cohorts: fairness. If your own matchmaking or moderation model skews against a region or a dialect, the abuse data you’re building everything on is bent at the source.”

[strip] “So the full claim isn’t just ‘strong evidence.’ It’s strong evidence that stays valid and isn’t skewed. Miss these and you get the worst outcome of all — a dashboard that’s confidently green, and wrong. That’s the opening tension of this whole talk, coming back to bite. Let’s close both gaps.”

Cross-cutting band — intro. Frames the two honesty checks as NOT rungs (they don't sit on the evidence-strength axis) but as what keeps every rung trustworthy: drift (over time) and fairness (across cohorts). Closes a real gap — the ladder implicitly assumed evidence stays good forever. Ties back to slide 3's "green dashboard, angry players" tension. Placed after "what proof means", before the synthesis.

Why it's not a rung. Critical framing — say it out loud. A rung = a stronger grade of evidence; these are orthogonal quality controls on ALL rungs. Forcing them onto the ladder would re-break the strength-axis (same error as cramming recommenders in).

Thesis completion. "strong (ladder) + stays valid (drift) + isn't skewed (fairness)" = a genuinely reliable source of truth. This is the sharpened framing the whole deck builds toward.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Source of truth
The system/data an operator treats as authoritative — what dashboards, bans, and decisions are based on. The thing this whole talk is trying to make trustworthy.
Cross-cutting
A concern that applies to every part of a system rather than being one step in it — here, two quality checks that protect all seven rungs at once.
Drift
When live data or a model's accuracy slowly diverges from what it was built/calibrated on, so yesterday's correct answer is quietly wrong today.
Cohort
A subgroup of players (by region, platform, language, skill tier) — fairness means a metric holds within each, not just on average.
60/67·Honest over time · drift detection
Cross-cutting · across TIME · drift & continuous validation ◆ SHARDFALL

Yesterday’s detector, quietly wrong today.

⚖️🌡️📉
Imagine…

A kitchen scale you calibrated last year. It still shows a confident number — but it’s drifted, and every recipe since is subtly wrong. Nobody notices, because the number still looks fine. A model is the same: it keeps emitting confident scores long after the world it learned has moved on.

1

Snapshot “normal” — the input distributions & accuracy the detector had when you trusted it.

2

Compare live data to that snapshot every day — has the shape of the inputs shifted?

3

Alarm on drift — when the gap crosses a line, the detector is operating outside what it was validated for.

4

Re-validate / recalibrate — refresh the calibration set (Rung 2!), retrain, or roll back. The loop never ends.

two standard drift signals — for the engineersPSI · KS
# Population Stability Index: live vs reference, per feature
PSI = Σᵢ (live％ᵢ − ref％ᵢ) · ln(live％ᵢ / ref％ᵢ)
#  <0.1 stable · 0.1–0.25 watch · >0.25 DRIFTED
# Kolmogorov–Smirnov: largest gap between the two CDFs
KS = max|F_live(x) − F_ref(x)|     # + ADWIN for streaming change
worked example — SHARDFALL reaction-time feature Reference mean 270ms. After a new input-latency patch, live sessions shift — aim looks “too fast” to the Rung-1 model, false positives spike. PSI on reaction_ms = 0.31 (>0.25) → DRIFT alarm. The cheats didn’t change — the game did. You recalibrate instead of banning innocents.

Why it protects the whole ladder: drift silently breaks Rung 1’s scores and Rung 2’s calibration guarantee (conformal assumes live data resembles the calibration set — drift is exactly that assumption failing). Standard methods: PSI, KS-test, ADWIN; in prod, SageMaker AI Model Monitor.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“First honesty check: across time. [analogy] Picture a kitchen scale you calibrated a year ago. It still shows a confident number — but it’s drifted, and every recipe since is quietly off. Nobody notices, because the number looks fine. Your models are exactly that scale: they keep emitting confident scores long after the world they learned has moved on.”

[walk the steps] “So you snapshot what ‘normal’ looked like when you trusted the detector — the input distributions, the accuracy. You compare live data to that snapshot every day. You alarm when the gap crosses a line. And you re-validate — refresh the calibration set, retrain, or roll back. It’s a loop that never ends, because the adversary never stops.”

[code + worked example] “For the engineers, two standard signals: Population Stability Index — this log-ratio sum, under 0.1 is stable, over 0.25 is drifted — and the KS statistic, the biggest gap between the two distributions. Concretely on SHARDFALL: you ship an input-latency patch, reaction times genuinely shift, and suddenly half your players look ‘too fast’ to the Rung-1 model — false positives spike. PSI on reaction time hits 0.31. The cheats didn’t change; the game changed. Drift detection tells you to recalibrate — instead of banning a wave of innocent players.”

[strip] “And notice it protects the whole ladder: drift breaks Rung 1’s scores and silently voids Rung 2’s guarantee — because conformal’s one assumption is that live data resembles the calibration set, and drift is precisely that assumption breaking.”

Cross-cutting — drift / continuous validation. PSI + KS (+ ADWIN for streams) detect when inputs/accuracy diverge from the validated baseline. Worked example: a latency patch drifts reaction-time, spiking false positives — "the game changed, not the cheats." Key tie: drift specifically voids Rung 2's conformal exchangeability assumption, so it protects the calibration guarantee, not just raw scores.

The memorable line. "The cheats didn't change — the game did." Drift makes a correct detector wrong without anyone touching it.

PSI thresholds. <0.1 stable, 0.1–0.25 watch, >0.25 drifted — the standard rule of thumb. The 0.31 example is over the line.

AWS home. SageMaker AI Model Monitor (data-quality + model-quality drift). Verify current naming.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Distribution
The overall shape of a feature's values across players (e.g. most reaction times cluster around 270ms). Drift = that shape moving.
PSI (Population Stability Index)
A single number for how much a feature's distribution has shifted from a reference; >0.25 means "materially drifted, stop trusting the model here."
KS test (Kolmogorov–Smirnov)
Measures the largest gap between two distributions — a standard statistical test for "are these two samples the same shape?"
ADWIN
"ADaptive WINdowing" — a streaming algorithm that flags the moment a live data stream's behaviour changes, for real-time drift.
Continuous validation
Re-checking a deployed model's accuracy on fresh labelled data on an ongoing basis, rather than trusting the launch-day score forever.
Baseline / reference window
The snapshot of "known-good" data the model was validated on, that live data is compared against.
61/67·Honest across cohorts · fairness audit
Cross-cutting · across COHORTS · fairness & bias auditing ◆ SHARDFALL

A biased model corrupts the truth at the source.

📋🌍⚖️
Imagine…

A smoke detector that’s twice as sensitive in one wing of the building. The fire-incident report will say that wing is dangerous — but it’s the detector that’s skewed, not the wing. Act on that report and you punish the wrong people and trust a distorted map of reality.

1

Pick the groups that matter — region, platform, input device, language/dialect (for chat moderation).

2

Slice every metric by group — flag-rate, false-positive rate, accuracy. Don’t look only at the average.

3

Measure the disparity — is one group flagged far more, after controlling for actual behaviour?

4

Find the cause & correct — a proxy feature? thin training data for that cohort? Re-weight, re-sample, or constrain.

the standard fairness checks — for the engineerssliced metrics
# Demographic parity: flag-rates equal across groups?
DPD = max_g P(flag | g) − min_g P(flag | g)
# Equalised odds: equal FP rate among the INNOCENT?
ΔFPR = max_g FPR_g − min_g FPR_g      # the one that matters for bans
# Disparate impact ratio (the "4/5ths" lens):
DI = P(flag | g_low) / P(flag | g_high)   # <0.8 ⇒ concern
worked example — SHARDFALL toxicity moderation Overall false-positive rate looks fine at 4%. Slice by language: English 3%, but a dialect group sits at 11%. ΔFPR = 0.08, disparate-impact ratio well past the line. The model learned slang it doesn’t understand = “toxic.” Innocent players in one cohort eat 3× the wrongful mutes — and your “toxicity rate by region” chart is now fiction.

Why it protects the whole ladder: a skewed detector poisons the very data every higher rung reasons over — a causal study (Rung 3) on biased flags measures the bias, not the truth. Audit the source, or build proofs on sand. Standard methods: sliced metrics, demographic parity / equalised odds, SHAP; in prod, SageMaker AI Clarify.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Second honesty check: across cohorts. [analogy] Imagine a smoke detector that’s twice as sensitive in one wing of a building. The incident report will say that wing is dangerous — but it’s the detector that’s skewed, not the wing. Act on the report and you do two bad things at once: punish the wrong people, and trust a distorted map of reality.”

[walk the steps] “So you pick the groups that matter — region, platform, input device, language for chat. You slice every metric by group, never just the average. You measure the disparity — is one group flagged far more, after controlling for what they actually did? And you find the cause and fix it — usually a proxy feature or just thin data for that cohort.”

[code + worked example] “For the engineers: demographic parity asks if flag-rates match across groups; equalised odds — the one that matters for bans — asks if the false-positive rate among the innocent is equal; and the disparate-impact ratio is the classic ‘four-fifths’ lens, under 0.8 is a red flag. Concretely on SHARDFALL chat moderation: overall false-positive rate looks great at four percent. Slice by language — English is three, but one dialect group sits at eleven. The model learned slang it doesn’t understand and called it toxic. Innocent players in that cohort eat three times the wrongful mutes — and your ‘toxicity by region’ dashboard is now fiction.”

[strip] “And this is the one that poisons everything above it: a skewed detector corrupts the data every higher rung reasons over. Run a causal study from Rung 3 on biased flags and you’ll measure the bias, not the truth. Audit the source, or you’re building proofs on sand.”

Cross-cutting — fairness / bias auditing. Sliced metrics + demographic parity / equalised odds / disparate-impact (4/5ths). Worked example: 4% overall FP rate hides 11% for one dialect cohort in toxicity moderation. Key tie: bias corrupts the data at the SOURCE, so every downstream rung (esp. Rung 3 causal) inherits it — "proofs on sand." Frames fairness as a data-truth issue, not just an ethics one (on-thesis).

The reframe that keeps it on-spine. This isn't pitched as "be ethical" — it's "a biased model is a lying instrument; your source of truth is corrupted." That's why it belongs in an integrity/trust deck.

Equalised odds is the key metric. For bans, the FP-rate-among-the-innocent disparity matters most — wrongfully muting/banning one cohort more than another. Lead with that.

AWS home. SageMaker AI Clarify (bias metrics + SHAP explainability). Verify current naming.

✦ plain-English terms (new on this slide)
Demographic parity
The flag/ban rate is roughly equal across groups — a first, crude fairness check (can be misleading if groups genuinely differ in behaviour).
Equalised odds
The error rates — especially false positives among the innocent — are equal across groups. The fairness metric that matters most for bans.
Disparate impact (4/5ths rule)
A long-standing test: if one group's flag rate is less than 80% of another's, that's a legal/ethical red flag worth investigating.
Proxy feature
An input that secretly stands in for a protected/cohort trait (e.g. a region-correlated ping value), letting bias sneak in even without using the trait directly.
Sliced metric
A metric computed separately for each cohort instead of pooled — the simplest, most powerful way to expose hidden disparity.
62/67·It all connects
Bringing it together · every rung is the same five moves

Seven techniques. One shape. Your game data in, a graded verdict out.

Every rung you just saw ran the same pipeline — only the data, the engine, and the strength of the answer changed as we climbed.

Rung① your game data② the engine③ the formula / check④ what you getstrength
1 Detectsession features · match graphrobust-z / GNNΣ z² ≥ cutoff · strong-edge clustersranked suspects + reason
suspicion
2 Quantifythose scores + a clean calib setconformalp=(1+#≥)/(n+1) ≤ αact / abstain, FP rate ≤ α
calibrated
3 Attributea metric, treated vs controldiff-in-diffATT = Δtreat − Δctrltrue causal effect + CI
causal
4 Stress-testheadless build · agent profilesRL self-play / ABMmax 𝔼[Σγᵗrₜ] · market-clearexploits & econ failures, pre-launch
empirical
5 Verify rulesshop/craft configSMT solver∃ n: items=0 ∧ gold>0 ?exploit recipe, or proof of none
proof · rules
6 Verify pipelinedraw log (seeds, commitment)SHA-256 verifyre-derive == stored ?tamper caught & named
proof · crypto
7 Exposethe rung-6 artifact, publishedoutsider’s own rechecksame verify, run externallyindependent confirmation
as strong as ↑

Column ① is all data you already have. Column ⑤ is the climb: suspicion → calibrated → causal → empirical → proof → cryptographic → outsider-verifiable. Same four moves — data, engine, formula, verdict — at seven strengths. You just choose how high to climb — and drift & fairness checks keep every row honest.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim — the moment it all clicks)

“Step back with me. We went deep on seven techniques — and I want you to see they were never seven different things. They were the same four moves, every time, at seven different strengths.”

[point column by column] “One: your game data goes in — and look down that column, it is all stuff you already have: telemetry, the scores you compute, a metric split by region, your shop config, a draw log. Two: an engine — a robust score, conformal, diff-in-diff, a self-play agent, an SMT solver, a hash check. Three: a formula, the exact ones we computed by hand. Four: the verdict you get back.”

[run down the strength column slowly] “And the right-hand column is the entire talk in one stripe: suspicion, then calibrated suspicion, then causal, then empirically broken, then proven over the rules, then cryptographically proven, then outsider-verifiable. Same pipeline — data in, graded verdict out. You just decide how far up the stakes in front of you demand.”

“That’s the Confidence Ladder, end to end. Now — when do you actually stop climbing?” [→ the decision-rule slide]

Capstone bridge slide (7 rungs). Collapses all seven rungs into ONE pipeline (data → engine → formula → verdict → strength), proving they share one shape. Column ① reinforces "you already have this data"; the strength bars grow 20→100% across the seven, reprising the whole thesis. The single most photographed slide — make sure the 1–4 statistical vs 5–7 proof distinction is still felt (bars 20–58% vs 74–100%).

Why it lands. After seven deep dives the audience needs the zoom-out: not seven tools to memorise, one repeatable move with a tunable proof-strength.

63/67·The climb, compounded
The climb, in one view

Each rung answers a different question — with a stronger guarantee.

RungQuestion it answers (in SHARDFALL)Evidence strengthWho acts on itWhen it pays
1 DetectWhich players / rings look abusive — and why?
Probabilistic		Human reviewerContinuously — operational
2 QuantifyHow sure are we — within a guaranteed error rate?
Calibrated		Auto-act or abstain→humanBefore every ban wave
3 AttributeDid our fix actually cause the change?
Causal		Analysts, leadershipAfter every intervention
4 Stress-testWhat breaks if we let robots loose on it?
Empirical		Designers, pre-launchBefore launch & each patch
5 Verify rulesCan any legal trade break my economy?
Proof · rules		Designers, pre-shipOnce — exploit never ships
6 Verify pipelineIs the crate service running what I shipped?
Proof · crypto		Anyone — hard true positiveIn the tail — insider / dispute
7 ExposeCan an outsider verify without trusting me?
As strong as the artifact		Players, regulatorsWhen trust is the product

They compose, and the bars tell the story: rungs 1–4 climb through statistical strength (suspect → calibrate → attribute → stress-test); rungs 5–7 cross into mathematical proof. Different questions, increasing certainty — pick the rungs your stakes demand.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“Whole climb on one screen — this is the slide to photograph.”

“Each rung answers a different question, so they never compete. [walk the rows] Who looks abusive? How sure are we, provably? Did our fix actually cause the change? What breaks if robots attack it? Can any legal trade break the economy? Is the live service the one we shipped? Can an outsider check us?”

[run a finger up the strength bars] “Watch the bars grow — and notice the seam at rung four. Below it, the evidence is statistical: a suspicion, then a calibrated suspicion, then a causal claim, then an empirical break. Above it, rungs five to seven, it becomes mathematical proof. [point to ‘who acts’] And look who acts: a human at rung one, never an auto-ban; by the proof rungs it’s a certainty anyone can act on. That shift from ‘a human decides’ to ‘anyone can check’ is the climb from suspicion to proof.”

[read the strip] “They compose — but you pick the rungs your stakes demand, which is exactly the next slide.”

Synthesis slide (7 rungs). One comparable view: question / strength / who-acts / when-it-pays across all seven. The strength bars (20→100%) and the explicit "1–4 statistical, 5–7 proof" seam are the key takeaways. The slide to photograph.

The seam at rung 4/5. The most important conceptual line: statistical evidence (1–4) vs mathematical proof (5–7). Don't let the audience flatten all seven into "more math = better."

Who-acts column. human → abstain-or-act → analysts → designers → anyone. The drift toward "anyone can act/check" tracks the rising certainty.

64/67·Choose your rung
The takeaway that matters Monday · you don’t need all of it

Don’t climb for its own sake. Climb to your stakes.

Seven rungs is the menu, not the mandate. Answer these about your game — each “yes” lights up a rung; skip the rest.

Single-player, no money, no ladder? Rung 1, maybe nothing more. A gacha title with real money in a regulated market (hello, ✦ Star Oracle)? You’ll want most of the ladder — and you’d have needed it anyway.

  • 1An adaptive adversary attacks you at scale? → Detect
  • 2A wrong auto-ban is costly (PR, legal, refunds)? → Quantify
  • 3You make interventions (ban waves, patches, promos) and must know they worked? → Attribute
  • 4A pre-launch exploit or economy meltdown would be catastrophic? → Stress-test
  • 5A single rules/economy loophole must be ruled out for certain? → Verify rules
  • 6An insider or bad deploy could silently alter outcomes? → Verify pipeline
  • 7An outsider (regulator, player, partner) must verify without trusting you? → Expose
▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“This is the slide I actually want you to take to work on Monday. I just showed you seven rungs — and now I’m going to tell you not to build most of them. Seven is the menu, not the mandate. The skill was never climbing as high as possible; it’s climbing to your stakes.”

“So, seven questions — each ‘yes’ lights up a rung.” [walk the list] “Adaptive adversary at scale? Detect. A wrong auto-ban is expensive — PR, legal, refunds? Quantify, so you can bound that. You run ban waves, patches, promos and need to know they worked? Attribute. A pre-launch exploit or economy meltdown would be catastrophic? Stress-test. A single rules loophole must be ruled out for certain? Verify the rules. An insider or bad deploy could silently change outcomes? Verify the pipeline. And does an outsider need to verify without trusting you? Expose.”

[read the strip — the two extremes] “A single-player game with no money and no ladder? Rung one, maybe nothing more — anything higher is pure over-engineering. A gacha game with real money in a regulated market — hello, Star Oracle — lights up almost the whole ladder, and you’d have needed it anyway. Saying ‘sometimes the answer is don’t build this’ out loud is exactly what should make you trust everything else I recommended.”

The most credible — and most useful — slide (now 7 rungs). After seven rungs, explicitly tell people NOT to build most of them. Each yes/no question maps 1:1 to a rung, colour-matched. The "Monday" decision rule applied to their own game in minutes. "You don't need all of it" earns trust on everything else.

Question→rung map. Q1 adversary→Detect · Q2 costly wrong-ban→Quantify · Q3 interventions→Attribute · Q4 pre-launch catastrophe→Stress-test · Q5 loophole-certainty→Verify-rules · Q6 insider→Verify-pipeline · Q7 outsider-verify→Expose. Colour of each bullet matches its rung.

Two extremes. Single-player non-monetised → Rung 1 at most; regulated real-money gacha → most of the ladder, needed anyway. This is the worksheet shipped in the kit.

65/67·Take-home kit
What you take home · more than slides

Leave with a kit, not a memory.

Decide

Choose-your-rung worksheet

The seven-question rule as a checklist. Apply it to your title in ten minutes. The highest-value artifact.

Run · Rungs 1–2

Detectors + a conformal wrapper

Anomaly & ring starters with a documented data contract — and a conformal wrapper that turns any score into a guaranteed false-positive rate. Point at your CSV; flagged-with-reason, with an error bar, on day one.

Run · Rungs 3–4

Causal notebook + sim harness

A diff-in-diff / synthetic-control notebook to measure what your last ban wave really did, and an agent-sim harness to stress-test your economy before launch.

Run · Rungs 5–6

Money-pump prover + sealed-envelope verifier

Drop in your shop & crafting rules; the prover finds any free-gold loop or proves there’s none. Plus the real SHA-256 commit-reveal verifier for your draw log.

Decide & productionize

Worksheet + per-rung AWS arch

The seven-question choose-your-rung worksheet, plus one reference diagram per rung — SageMaker AI/Kinesis/Bedrock, KMS/Lambda/DynamoDB/Nitro — to hand your platform team.

One repo, runs offline in minutes. Theory → worked example → a thing you run on your own game this week. Every demo and worked example you saw today is in it — pick the rungs your game needs.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)

“I don’t want you to leave with a memory — I want you to leave with a kit. One repo, runs offline in under a minute, and every demo you watched today is in it.”

[walk the five cards] “First, decide: the seven-question worksheet from the last slide, as a checklist — apply it to your title in ten minutes. That’s the highest-value thing here and it costs nothing. Then run Rung one: the anomaly and ring detectors, with a documented data contract — point them at your own CSV and get flagged-with-a-reason on day one, no training, no GPU. Then run Rungs two and three: the money-pump prover — drop in your own shop and crafting rules and it’ll find the dupe or prove there’s none — and the real SHA-256 verifier for your draw log. And finally, productionize: one AWS reference diagram per rung to hand your platform team.”

[read the strip] “Theory, to a live demo, to a thing you run on your own game this week. That’s the whole point.”

The "real value add" slide. Five concrete artifacts, each tied to a verb (Decide/Run/Run/Run/Productionize), so the audience sees a path from "saw it" to "shipped it." Every interactive demo on stage is backed by real code in the kit (anomaly, rings, verify_economy.py, tamper_check.py).

Decide. The decision rule as a fill-in checklist — the judgment, portable, ten minutes, costs nothing.

Run Rung 1. The anomaly + ring starters with a minimal-columns data contract (player_id, accuracy, actions_per_min, reaction_ms). Own CSV → real flags-with-reasons, no training, no GPU.

Run Rungs 5–6. verify_economy.py (edit the action table to your economy) + tamper_check.py (real SHA-256, droppable into a draw-log pipeline).

Productionize. One AWS diagram per rung. Verify service names/status before presenting.

Strip. "One repo, offline, under a minute" + "theory → live demo → a thing you run on your own game this week." Mirror in the abstract's takeaways and the closing QR.

66/67·Close
Close

From “we think so” to “we can prove it.”

The techniques aren’t the point — the calibration is. Know what each signal proves, build the rung your stakes demand, attach a reason to everything, and stop there.

[ QR ]
THE QUICKSTART KIT
Worksheet · runnable rungs · AWS reference arch · every live demo.
One git clone away. Go climb it on your own game.

None of these techniques are mine or new — anomaly detection, GNNs, conformal prediction, causal inference, RL self-play, agent-based simulation, automated reasoning, commit-reveal, drift detection & fairness auditing are all standard public methods (credits, with links, in the appendix). What’s new is the framing: a seven-rung ladder — kept honest by drift & fairness checks — that matches evidence strength to stakes, for game integrity. SHARDFALL and STAR ORACLE are fictional games invented for this talk; any resemblance to real titles is coincidental.

▶ Speaker & reader notes
▶ Say this (verbatim script)
